entries.title: Bug Fix Prioritization

delegation.description: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?
delegation.example: AI suggests bug fix priorities based on impact and frequency.
delegation.antipattern: Letting AI fully decide bug fix order without human input.

description.description: What context/framing makes the AI most useful here?
description.example: Provide bug reports, user impact, and historical data.
description.antipattern: No context, leading to poor prioritization.

discernment.description: How do you evaluate if the AI output is trustworthy?
discernment.example: Cross-check AI prioritization with product manager feedback.
discernment.antipattern: Accepting AI priorities without review.

diligence.description: What human accountability is required after AI involvement?
diligence.example: Product manager must approve final bug fix order.
diligence.antipattern: Deploying fixes based solely on AI suggestions.

entries.summary: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?

collaboration.description: Human delegates triage scope, AI prioritizes bugs with context provided, human discerns the ranked list, diligence locks in the fix order — looping back to description if root cause is unclear.

sequence.label: Agree on AI triage autonomy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: I have 20 open bugs. Can you rank them by user impact and estimated fix time without me reviewing each one?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I can rank by impact category and complexity estimate. I'd recommend you confirm the top 5 before engineering schedules — want me to auto-assign the bottom 10 to backlog?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Auto-assign backlog tier, surface the top 10 for my review.

sequence.triggers_next: Triage scope and backlog rule agreed.

sequence.label: Provide bug list and product context

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Here are the 20 bug reports with user complaint frequency and affected feature areas.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I weight payment-flow bugs higher than UI bugs?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — payment and data-loss bugs are P0 regardless of frequency.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has priority rules and full bug context.

sequence.label: Evaluate prioritized list

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Bug #7 is ranked P1 but I know it's a duplicate of #12 — remove it.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Understood. Do you want me to merge the user complaint counts or keep them separate?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Merge them — #12 now carries the combined weight.

sequence.triggers_next: Prioritized list is validated and de-duplicated.

loop_back.condition: Root cause of a high-priority bug is unclear or disputed.
loop_back.reason: More engineering context is needed before ranking is meaningful.

sequence.label: Commit to fix schedule and document

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Engineering lead, confirm sprint assignment for P0 and P1 items.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Log the AI-generated ranking and the manual overrides in Jira for audit.

sequence.triggers_next: Sprint is committed. Cycle restarts at next triage session.

transitions.trigger: Triage scope agreed.
AI delivers ranked list.
List validated and overrides applied.
Root cause unclear — needs more context.
Next triage session — restart.

entries.title: Code Review Triage

delegation.description: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?
delegation.example: Use AI to triage code review comments, flagging those needing human attention.
delegation.antipattern: Fully automating code review without human oversight.

description.description: What context/framing makes the AI most useful here?
description.example: Provide repository context and recent commit history for accurate triage.
description.antipattern: No context, leading to irrelevant triage suggestions.

discernment.description: How do you evaluate if the AI output is trustworthy?
discernment.example: Compare flagged comments against senior developer feedback.
discernment.antipattern: Accepting AI triage without validation.

diligence.description: What human accountability is required after AI involvement?
diligence.example: Human must review flagged comments before merging.
diligence.antipattern: Merging code based solely on AI triage.

entries.summary: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?

collaboration.description: Human negotiates AI review scope, provides code context, evaluates findings, then approves — looping back to scope if false-positive rate is high.

sequence.label: Negotiate AI review scope and autonomy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Can you automatically approve style-only comments and flag logic and security issues for me?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I can flag logic and security issues with confidence levels. I'd recommend human approval for all security findings — want me to auto-close only style nits?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — auto-close style nits, surface everything else with a severity label.

sequence.triggers_next: Autonomy boundaries and severity thresholds are agreed.

sequence.label: Provide repository and PR context

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Here is the PR diff, our style guide link, and the three open issues this PR addresses.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Got it. Should I cross-reference the open issues when flagging findings?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — link any finding to the relevant issue if there's a match.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has enough context to begin analysis.

sequence.label: Evaluate AI triage findings

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Walk me through your findings. Item 4 — is that a real security issue or a false positive?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Item 4 is likely a false positive given the existing sanitization in line 42. I flagged it because the pattern matches a known injection vector — but the guard is already in place.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Agreed, dismiss item 4. The rest look valid.

sequence.triggers_next: Human has validated which findings are actionable.

loop_back.condition: More than 30% of findings are false positives.
loop_back.reason: Scope or severity thresholds need renegotiation.

sequence.label: Approve findings and document decisions

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Senior engineer, please sign off on items 1, 2, 3, and 5 before we merge.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: I'll add a comment to the PR logging which items were AI-flagged and which were human-dismissed.

sequence.triggers_next: PR is approved and decisions are documented. Cycle restarts for the next PR.

transitions.trigger: Autonomy level and severity thresholds agreed.
AI completes triage analysis.
Human validates findings as actionable.
False-positive rate too high — re-scope.
New PR arrives — restart cycle.

entries.title: Refactoring Suggestions

delegation.description: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?
delegation.example: AI proposes refactoring opportunities, human reviews and approves.
delegation.antipattern: AI automatically refactors code without human review.

description.description: What context/framing makes the AI most useful here?
description.example: Provide codebase structure and known pain points.
description.antipattern: No context, leading to generic suggestions.

discernment.description: How do you evaluate if the AI output is trustworthy?
discernment.example: Validate suggestions against test coverage and code quality metrics.
discernment.antipattern: Accepting AI refactoring without testing.

diligence.description: What human accountability is required after AI involvement?
diligence.example: Developer must run tests and review changes.
diligence.antipattern: Merging AI-refactored code without testing.

entries.summary: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?

collaboration.description: Human and AI iterate through multiple refactoring passes — each pass re-negotiates scope, provides updated code context, evaluates suggestions, and commits accepted changes.

sequence.label: Scope the refactoring pass

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: I want to reduce coupling in this module. Can you suggest where to start?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I see three high-coupling hotspots. Should I suggest refactors for all three in this pass or focus on the most impactful one?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Focus on the most impactful hotspot first — we'll iterate.

sequence.triggers_next: Refactoring focus for this pass is agreed.

sequence.label: Provide current code and constraints

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Here is the current module, the interface contracts it must preserve, and our no-breaking-changes constraint.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Are there performance benchmarks I should keep in mind?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Latency must stay under 50ms for the main path — flag anything that might affect it.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has code, contracts, and performance constraints.

sequence.label: Evaluate refactoring suggestions

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Suggestion 2 breaks the existing interface — reject that one.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Understood. Suggestion 3 is an alternative that preserves the interface — want me to elaborate?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes, and check if it affects the 50ms path.

sequence.triggers_next: Accepted suggestions are identified and validated.

sequence.label: Commit accepted changes and verify

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Running benchmark — 43ms, within budget. Committing suggestion 3.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Adding a commit note: AI-suggested refactor, human-validated against interface and perf budget.

sequence.triggers_next: Changes committed. Begin next iteration with remaining hotspots.

transitions.trigger: Refactoring scope for this pass agreed.
AI delivers suggestions for this pass.
Accepted suggestions validated.
Remaining hotspots — begin next iteration.

entries.title: Test Case Generation

delegation.description: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?
delegation.example: AI generates test cases, developer reviews and integrates.
delegation.antipattern: AI-generated tests merged without human review.

description.description: What context/framing makes the AI most useful here?
description.example: Provide function signatures and edge case requirements.
description.antipattern: No context, leading to incomplete tests.

discernment.description: How do you evaluate if the AI output is trustworthy?
discernment.example: Run generated tests and check coverage improvements.
discernment.antipattern: Accepting tests without execution.

diligence.description: What human accountability is required after AI involvement?
diligence.example: Developer must validate and maintain test suite.
diligence.antipattern: Relying solely on AI for test maintenance.

entries.summary: Should this task be automated, augmented, or agentic?

collaboration.description: Human delegates test scope, provides spec and edge cases, evaluates AI-generated tests, then merges after CI passes — looping back to description if coverage gaps appear.

sequence.label: Define test generation scope

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Generate unit tests for this module. Can you also decide which edge cases to include?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I can generate tests and propose edge cases, but I'd recommend you confirm the coverage targets — what's the minimum acceptable coverage percentage?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: 90% line coverage minimum. Flag anything you're uncertain about.

sequence.triggers_next: Coverage targets and uncertainty-flagging rule agreed.

sequence.label: Provide spec, examples, and constraints

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Here's the function spec, three existing test examples, and the list of known edge cases from the bug history.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I test for concurrency issues or only sequential inputs?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Sequential only for now — concurrency tests are a separate workstream.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has spec, examples, and constraint boundaries.

sequence.label: Review generated tests for correctness

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Test #8 asserts the wrong return type — it should be a string, not null.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Corrected. Also, I flagged test #12 as potentially flaky due to time-dependent assertion — want me to mock the clock?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes, mock the clock for #12.

sequence.triggers_next: Tests pass review and CI runs green.

loop_back.condition: Coverage is below target or significant edge cases are missing.
loop_back.reason: More spec detail or explicit edge case guidance is needed.

sequence.label: Approve tests and add to codebase

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Tests pass CI at 93% coverage. Merging to main.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Added a comment noting which tests were AI-generated for future reference.

sequence.triggers_next: Tests merged. Cycle restarts for next module.

transitions.trigger: Coverage targets agreed.
AI delivers test suite.
Tests validated and CI passes.
Coverage gaps require more spec detail.
Next module — restart.

human-dignity.description: The curriculum is designed to serve learners of all backgrounds with equal dignity — ensuring AI education does not embed cultural bias, exclude learners with disabilities, or reinforce existing power imbalances between those who "understand AI" and those who do not.
human-dignity.example: Curriculum designer: "Our AI literacy course uses examples from Silicon Valley companies exclusively. Is that consistent with human dignity for our predominantly African and Middle Eastern student body?" AI: "No — a curriculum that presents only one cultural context for AI normalizes a particular worldview as 'default.' UNESCO's principle of human dignity includes cultural respect and non-discrimination in education. I recommend auditing every case study and example for cultural diversity, and co-designing at least 30% of examples with students from the communities you serve."
human-dignity.antipattern: Designing an AI curriculum that treats a single cultural or economic context as universal — producing graduates who understand AI through one lens and are unprepared to question whose interests AI systems serve.

environment.description: The curriculum addresses the environmental cost of AI — training compute, data center energy, hardware lifecycle — and teaches learners to make environmentally informed decisions about AI system design.
environment.example: AI: "For the environmental module, I recommend three content blocks: (1) The energy cost of model training — a GPT-4-scale model consumes more electricity than 100 US households for a year. (2) Inference at scale — even deployed models have a per-query energy footprint that multiplies across billions of users. (3) Sustainable design choices — model distillation, efficient architectures, and green compute options. Make the environmental calculation a required component of every student capstone project."
environment.antipattern: Teaching AI design without any environmental impact component — producing AI practitioners who optimize for performance and cost but are blind to ecological externalities.

privacy.description: The curriculum teaches learners to design privacy-protective AI systems and applies privacy principles to the educational technology used to deliver the course itself — including AI-powered learning platforms.
privacy.example: Human: "Our LMS uses an AI engagement tracker that monitors keystroke patterns. Is that consistent with the privacy principle for our students?" AI: "That is a surveillance tool applied to learners without meaningful consent. UNESCO's privacy principle applies to learners in educational settings — they have the right to know what data is collected, why, and how to opt out. The curriculum should both teach privacy by design and practice it: audit the LMS, disclose data collection to students, and provide a genuine opt-out that does not disadvantage learning outcomes."
privacy.antipattern: Teaching AI privacy principles in the classroom while using AI-powered surveillance tools on students without their informed consent — undermining the curriculum's own ethical foundation.

transparency.description: The curriculum is transparent about its own design: learning objectives, assessment criteria, AI tools used in teaching, and the limitations of what the curriculum covers — including what it cannot teach.
transparency.example: AI: "For course transparency, I recommend publishing three documents to students at enrollment: (1) What AI tools are used in this course and how their data is used. (2) What the curriculum does not cover — students should know if advanced mathematics or specific programming languages are prerequisites for deeper engagement. (3) Assessment criteria in plain language — not just rubrics, but what the institution considers 'AI literacy' and why."
transparency.antipattern: Presenting an AI curriculum as comprehensive when it primarily covers one paradigm (e.g., large language models) without disclosing to students what is outside its scope.

accountability.description: The curriculum has a community accountability mechanism: a student-community advisory board reviews the curriculum annually, findings are published, and the institution commits to responsive updates based on community input.
accountability.example: AI closes the cycle: "Accountability structure for the curriculum: (1) Student advisory board — meets each semester, reviews content for relevance, equity, and cultural appropriateness. (2) Community review — annual open review inviting alumni, employers, and community organizations. (3) Published update log — every curriculum change is documented and explained to students. (4) Redress pathway — students can formally flag curriculum concerns with a 30-day response commitment from academic leadership."
accountability.antipattern: Reviewing the AI curriculum only through internal faculty committees without any mechanism for student or community input — producing curricula that serve institutional interests over learner needs.

collaboration.description: Dignity audit identifies cultural and accessibility gaps in curriculum content → environmental module is designed with concrete metrics for learners → privacy audit of teaching tools and curriculum privacy content → transparency documents are published to students at enrollment → community accountability structure is established with annual review cycle. Loops back to human-dignity if transparency review reveals undisclosed content biases.

sequence.label: Audit curriculum content for cultural equity and accessibility

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're designing a new AI ethics and literacy course for undergraduates. Start with dignity — what do we audit first?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three dignity audits before any content is finalized: (1) Cultural diversity — review every case study, example, and reading for cultural representation. Who are the AI leaders referenced? Whose AI failures are discussed? Is the student body's own cultural context represented? (2) Accessibility — is the curriculum accessible to learners with visual, auditory, and cognitive disabilities? AI tools used must meet WCAG 2.1 AA minimum. (3) Power balance — does the curriculum teach students to be AI consumers or AI critical agents? UNESCO's dignity principle requires the latter.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Case studies are 90% US-centric. Accessibility audit reveals the primary AI demo tool has no screen reader support.

sequence.triggers_next: Dignity gaps identified — cultural diversity gap and accessibility gap documented.

sequence.label: Design the environmental impact module

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we teach AI's environmental footprint in a way that is accurate and actionable for undergraduates?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three-week module structure: Week 1 — The hidden cost of AI: training compute, data center water use, hardware mining. Use ML CO2 Impact calculator as a hands-on exercise. Week 2 — Inference at scale: how each AI query has an energy cost; cumulative impact of billions of queries. Week 3 — Sustainable AI design: model distillation, efficient architectures, green compute providers. Capstone requirement: every student project must include an energy footprint estimate as part of the design documentation.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We'll add the environmental footprint requirement to the capstone rubric.

sequence.triggers_next: Environmental module designed with concrete student deliverables.

sequence.label: Audit teaching tools and design privacy curriculum content

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Our LMS has an AI engagement tracker. Should we keep it?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: The engagement tracker must meet three conditions to be consistent with UNESCO privacy principles: (1) Informed consent — students must be told exactly what is tracked, why, and for how long. (2) Data minimization — only data necessary for the educational purpose is collected. (3) Opt-out without penalty — a student who opts out must not be disadvantaged in grading or participation. If the tracker does not meet all three, it must be reconfigured or replaced. For the curriculum content: I recommend a dedicated privacy by design module where students audit a real AI system's data practices.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We'll reconfigure the tracker: opt-out available, data deleted after semester ends.

sequence.triggers_next: Teaching tool privacy audit complete; privacy curriculum module designed.

sequence.label: Publish curriculum transparency documents to students

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What transparency documents should students receive at enrollment?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three documents at enrollment: (1) AI tools disclosure — list every AI tool used in the course, what data it collects, and how students can opt out. (2) Curriculum scope notice — what this course covers and what it does not; prerequisites and pathways for deeper study. (3) Assessment transparency — plain-language explanation of what 'AI literacy' means for this institution's graduates and how each assessment criterion maps to that definition. Publish all three on the course page, not buried in the LMS.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All three documents drafted and published to the course page.

sequence.triggers_next: Transparency documents published to students at enrollment.

loop_back.condition: Transparency review reveals that undisclosed content biases exist in the curriculum.
loop_back.reason: Dignity audit must be re-run if new biases are uncovered through transparency review.

sequence.label: Establish community accountability and annual review cycle

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we build genuine accountability into the curriculum governance?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four-part accountability structure: (1) Student advisory board — 6 student representatives, meets each semester, reviews curriculum for equity and relevance. Board recommendations are responded to in writing within 30 days. (2) Annual community review — open event inviting alumni, employers, civil society. Findings are published. (3) Public curriculum changelog — every update is documented and explained to enrolled and prospective students. (4) Formal redress pathway — students can file curriculum concerns with a named academic ombudsperson; 30-day response commitment. This structure makes accountability real, not nominal.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Student advisory board established. First community review scheduled for end of academic year.

sequence.triggers_next: Community accountability structure operational with annual review committed.

transitions.trigger: Dignity gaps identified and documented.
Environmental module designed with student deliverables.
Teaching tool audit complete and privacy module designed.
Undisclosed content biases found — dignity audit re-run.
Transparency documents published.
Annual community review triggers next curriculum cycle.

entries.title: AI-Augmented Course Design

delegation.description: AI can accelerate the scaffolding work of course design: generating draft learning objectives from a topic brief, suggesting module sequencing options, proposing assessment formats, brainstorming reading list candidates, and drafting rubric language. The instructor must own: the pedagogical strategy, the theoretical framework underlying the course, the sequencing rationale, and all decisions about what students will actually experience. Pedagogical authority cannot be delegated.
delegation.example: A professor designing a new undergraduate ethics course asks AI to generate a draft list of possible weekly topics aligned with her stated course arc (from foundational theory to applied cases). She uses this list as a stimulus to compare against her own planned sequence, adopting two AI-suggested topics she had overlooked and discarding three that don't serve her theoretical framework.
delegation.antipattern: Asking AI to design the full course — objectives, module structure, readings, assignments, assessments — and adopting the output with cosmetic changes, without grounding the design in a coherent pedagogical theory or ensuring it reflects the instructor's scholarly expertise.

description.description: Provide AI with detailed instructional design context: course level and credit hours, student profile and prerequisite knowledge, program-level learning outcomes the course must serve, your pedagogical philosophy, assessment philosophy, any accreditation or institutional constraints, and the thematic arc you want the course to trace. Precision in the brief yields useful scaffolding.
description.example: Prompt: 'Help me draft learning objectives for a 3-credit upper-division course on algorithmic bias. Students are CS seniors with strong technical backgrounds but limited social science exposure. Program outcome: graduates can evaluate the societal impact of technology systems. I want the course to move from conceptual foundations (weeks 1-4) to case analysis (weeks 5-10) to policy and design response (weeks 11-14). Use Bloom's Taxonomy verbs appropriate to the upper-division level.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'Write learning objectives for a course on AI ethics.' Without specifying student level, program context, Bloom's taxonomy tier, or the course's theoretical arc, AI produces generic objectives that could apply to any course at any level and serve no specific program outcome.

discernment.description: Evaluate AI-generated course design elements against pedagogical soundness, alignment to Bloom's Taxonomy, internal coherence, and fit with your students' actual preparation. Check that assessment types genuinely measure the stated learning objectives, that the workload is realistic, and that reading suggestions are current, credible, and accessible to your student population.
discernment.example: An instructor reviews AI-generated weekly learning objectives and notices three of them are knowledge-level objectives (define, identify, list) in weeks designed for upper-division analysis. She revises them upward to analysis and evaluation verbs to match the cognitive demand appropriate for the course level.
discernment.antipattern: Adopting AI-generated course syllabi and assessment designs without checking internal alignment — using multiple-choice exams to assess objectives that require critical analysis, or setting a reading workload that AI estimated without accounting for the actual length and density of the suggested texts.

diligence.description: Verify the accuracy of all factual content AI contributes to course materials. Document the AI's role in course design per your institution's academic integrity and transparency policies. Ensure the final course design meets accreditation requirements, reflects current disciplinary knowledge, and embodies your own scholarly judgment and professional responsibility as the instructor of record.
diligence.example: A faculty member building a new public health law course uses AI to draft a draft syllabus framework, then spends three hours reviewing it against her institution's course approval criteria, the program's accreditation standards, and current public health law scholarship — revising heavily before submission for curricular approval.
diligence.antipattern: Submitting an AI-generated course proposal to a curriculum committee without personally verifying that the proposed learning objectives meet accreditation standards, that the reading list contains accurate and current citations, or that the assessment strategy is pedagogically defensible under institutional review.

entries.summary: AI can accelerate the scaffolding work of course design: generating draft learning objectives from a topic brief, suggesting module sequencing options, proposing assessment formats, brainstorming read

collaboration.description: Instructor delegates curriculum scaffolding, provides learning goals and constraints, evaluates AI-drafted structure, then approves — looping back to description if pedagogical alignment is off.

sequence.label: Agree on AI's curriculum design role

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Help me design a 10-week course on data literacy. You propose the weekly themes — I'll adjust.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I sequence from concepts to tools, or mix both throughout?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Concepts first, tools introduced in week 4 after foundations are set.

sequence.triggers_next: AI's design role and pedagogical sequencing approach agreed.

sequence.label: Provide learning outcomes and constraints

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Students are undergraduates with no statistics background. By week 10 they should be able to read and critique a published data study.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I include assessment design or only the content structure?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Include weekly assessments — short, formative, no exams.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has learner profile, outcomes, and assessment approach.

sequence.label: Evaluate curriculum structure

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Week 6 introduces regression too early — students won't have the algebra foundation yet.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I can move regression to week 8 and use week 6 for data visualization instead.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Do that. Also add a prerequisite check activity in week 1.

sequence.triggers_next: Curriculum validated for pedagogical soundness.

loop_back.condition: Curriculum consistently misaligns with stated learning outcomes or student level.
loop_back.reason: Learning goals or student profile constraints need to be re-specified.

sequence.label: Approve and document

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Department curriculum committee reviews and approves the final structure.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Filing AI-assisted design notes in the course development record.

sequence.triggers_next: Curriculum approved. Cycle restarts when course is revised.

transitions.trigger: Design role and sequencing approach agreed.
AI delivers curriculum draft.
Structure validated.
Persistent misalignment — re-specify goals.
Course revision cycle — restart.

entries.title: AI-Assisted Lesson Planning for Educators

delegation.description: Identify which lesson planning elements AI can accelerate versus which require the teacher's direct professional judgment. AI handles well: generating activity ideas, drafting discussion questions, suggesting differentiation strategies, proposing assessment rubrics, and creating example problems. Teachers must retain: selecting content aligned to standards, setting the pedagogical arc across a unit, understanding individual student needs, and making final instructional decisions.
delegation.example: A 7th-grade science teacher asks AI to generate five hands-on activity ideas for a lesson on cellular respiration at a middle school level, then selects the one that fits her lab budget and her students' prior knowledge, adapts it to her pacing, and writes her own formative assessment questions.
delegation.antipattern: Pasting an AI-generated full lesson plan directly into the school's curriculum system without reviewing it for standard alignment, age appropriateness, or fit with the specific class's learning trajectory — treating AI as a replacement for pedagogical expertise rather than a brainstorming assistant.

description.description: Provide AI with specific instructional context: subject and grade level, concrete learning objectives tied to standards, the students' current skill level and prior knowledge, the available time and resources, the preferred pedagogical approach, and any accessibility or differentiation needs. Generic requests produce generic plans.
description.example: Prompt: 'Help me plan a 50-minute 8th-grade ELA lesson on identifying unreliable narrators. Students have read chapters 1-5 of The Outsiders. Objective: students can cite textual evidence to explain how Ponyboy's perspective limits the reader's understanding of events. Pedagogy: discussion-based with a short collaborative activity. Class includes 3 ELL students and 2 students with reading IEPs.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'Write a lesson plan about unreliable narrators for middle school.' This produces a generic template disconnected from the specific text students are reading, the actual learning objective, the class's composition, and the teacher's instructional context.

discernment.description: Evaluate AI-generated lesson plans and activities critically before adopting them. Check standard alignment, age appropriateness of content and vocabulary, timing realism, whether suggested activities match your students' actual readiness, and whether the pedagogical sequence makes instructional sense. AI does not know your students; you do.
discernment.example: A teacher reviews an AI-generated lesson plan and notices the suggested 'exit ticket' question requires inferential reasoning two grade levels above her current class's demonstrated ability. She replaces it with a scaffolded sentence-starter version that matches where her students actually are.
discernment.antipattern: Implementing AI-suggested activities without mentally simulating how your specific students will respond — discovering mid-lesson that the activity assumes prior knowledge students don't have, or that the timing estimate was wildly optimistic for the class's working pace.

diligence.description: Maintain professional responsibility for every lesson you deliver. Verify that AI-suggested content is factually accurate, that activities are safe and appropriate for your students, and that lesson plans meet the curricular standards your students are accountable for. Your professional judgment is the final quality gate, not the AI.
diligence.example: An educator uses AI-generated lesson scaffolding as a starting framework, then personally verifies that all factual content aligns with state science standards, removes an activity that requires materials not available in her classroom, and adds a culturally responsive example relevant to her students' community.
diligence.antipattern: Delivering an AI-generated lesson that contains a factual error (e.g., an incorrect historical date or a flawed science explanation) because the teacher assumed the AI's content was accurate and did not independently verify it before presenting it to students.

entries.summary: Identify which lesson planning elements AI can accelerate versus which require the teacher's direct professional judgment. AI handles well: generating activity ideas, drafting discussion questions, su

collaboration.description: Teacher delegates lesson scaffolding, provides class context and learning objective, evaluates the AI plan, then uses it in class — single pass per lesson.

sequence.label: Define AI's lesson design role

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Draft a 45-minute lesson plan on photosynthesis for 8th graders. You propose the activities.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I include a hands-on activity or keep it lecture and discussion?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Include one hands-on activity — the class has lab access.

sequence.triggers_next: AI's design role and activity type agreed.

sequence.label: Provide class context and objectives

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Class of 28 students, mixed ability. Objective: understand the light-dependent reactions. Prior knowledge: cell structure.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Any students with specific accessibility needs I should account for?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: One student uses screen-reader-compatible materials — flag anything visual-only.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has class profile, objective, and accessibility constraints.

sequence.label: Evaluate lesson plan quality

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The lab activity takes 20 minutes but the setup alone is 10 — tighten it.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I can use a pre-setup version with materials already in place. Reduces activity time to 12 minutes.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Good — and flag the visual diagram for accessibility adaptation.

sequence.triggers_next: Lesson plan validated for timing and inclusivity.

sequence.label: Approve and deliver

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Head of department reviews lesson plan — approved.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Noting AI-drafted plan in the lesson record for reflection.

sequence.triggers_next: Lesson delivered. New lesson requires a fresh cycle.

transitions.trigger: Design role and activity type agreed.
AI delivers lesson plan.
Plan validated.
New lesson — fresh cycle.

entries.title: Student Career Planning with AI

delegation.description: Students can productively delegate to AI: resume drafting from bullet points they supply, generating practice interview questions for a target role, exploring career paths in an unfamiliar field, drafting cover letter frameworks, and researching industry norms. Students must retain: decisions about which opportunities align with their values and goals, truthful representation of their own experiences, and final responsibility for all submitted application materials.
delegation.example: A college junior asks AI to suggest ten career paths where her combination of biology major and data analytics minor would be valued, then researches the top three that interest her and uses that understanding to craft her own narrative about why she is pursuing biostatistics.
delegation.antipattern: Having AI write a cover letter that fabricates or exaggerates the student's experiences, then submitting it as an accurate representation — or using AI to answer interview questions on the student's behalf via an earpiece during a live interview.

description.description: Share with AI your actual skills, experiences, target roles, industry context, career constraints, and personal goals. The more accurately you describe yourself and your situation, the more useful the AI's suggestions will be. Vague or inflated self-descriptions lead to advice that fits a fictional version of you, not your real situation.
description.example: Prompt: 'I'm a 3rd-year environmental engineering student with a 3.4 GPA, one summer internship at a municipal water authority doing stormwater modeling, and a Python project analyzing local air quality data. I want to work in climate tech after graduation, preferably at a company working on water or air quality monitoring. Help me identify specific entry-level roles to target and the skills I should develop this year.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'I'm a student who wants a good job in tech. What should I do?' This produces generic career advice applicable to millions of people and useless for the student's actual situation, skills, and industry interest.

discernment.description: Evaluate AI career advice critically. Verify that suggested roles actually exist and match what you want. Check salary ranges and job market realities against current sources like Bureau of Labor Statistics data or industry salary surveys, not just AI assertions. Assess whether AI suggestions genuinely fit your personal values, lifestyle needs, and long-term goals — not just what sounds impressive.
discernment.example: A student receives AI-generated advice suggesting she pursue a specific niche certification. She researches the certification independently, reads LinkedIn profiles of people who hold it, talks to two professionals in the field, and determines the certification is valued by only one employer segment — not the area she actually wants to work in.
discernment.antipattern: Following AI career advice without verification — enrolling in a bootcamp AI recommended, discovering it is not recognized by employers in the target field, or pursuing a role because AI said it pays well without checking current market data or speaking with anyone actually working in that field.

diligence.description: Own every career decision you make. AI is a thinking partner, not a career counselor with knowledge of your full life context. Build your own understanding of your field. Ensure all application materials truthfully represent your actual experiences. Develop a personal policy for how you use AI in your professional development so your skills and judgment grow alongside your AI fluency.
diligence.example: A student uses AI to generate a list of potential resume bullet points based on her internship activities, then rewrites each one in her own voice, ensures they accurately reflect what she did, and has her university career center review the final document — treating AI as a drafting scaffold, not the author.
diligence.antipattern: Submitting AI-generated job applications with exaggerated credentials, relying entirely on AI career advice without informational interviews or real-world research, or using AI so heavily in application writing that the student cannot speak authentically about the experiences described in their own materials.

entries.summary: Students can productively delegate to AI: resume drafting from bullet points they supply, generating practice interview questions for a target role, exploring career paths in an unfamiliar field, draf

collaboration.description: Advisor delegates initial career path research, provides student profile, evaluates AI recommendations for fit, then presents to student — looping back if recommendations miss the student's actual goals.

sequence.label: Define AI's research role

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Research career options for a student interested in environmental science with a data analytics minor.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I focus on entry-level roles or also include graduate pathways?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Both — the student hasn't decided on grad school yet.

sequence.triggers_next: Research scope and career path types agreed.

sequence.label: Provide student profile and goals

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: GPA 3.4, strong in statistics, weaker in field work. Interested in policy impact, not just research.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I weight roles that offer remote work options?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — student is geographically constrained for the next 2 years.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has full student profile and constraint set.

sequence.label: Evaluate career path recommendations

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Option 3 (field monitoring) doesn't fit — student has limited mobility.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Replacing with environmental data analyst roles at NGOs — policy-adjacent and remote-friendly.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Good. Option 5 seems too senior for entry-level — adjust expectations.

sequence.triggers_next: Recommendations validated for fit and realistic expectations.

loop_back.condition: Recommendations consistently miss student's stated values or constraints.
loop_back.reason: Student profile needs richer goal or constraint detail.

sequence.label: Present to student and document

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Presenting AI-curated options to the student — student picks two to explore.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Documenting the advisory session and noting which options were AI-generated.

sequence.triggers_next: Session complete. Next advising session begins new cycle.

transitions.trigger: Research scope agreed.
AI delivers career options.
Options validated for fit.
Recommendations miss goals — re-profile student.
Next advising session — restart.

strategy.description: The ethics board's mandate is grounded in the organization's AI strategy — it exists to protect the organization's stated values and purpose, not to slow down deployment. The strategic framing determines what questions the board is designed to answer.
strategy.example: CEO: "We want an AI ethics board. But I need it to help us move responsibly, not to be a blocker." AI: "That framing is exactly right — and it should be written into the board's charter. The WEF Playbook frames an ethics board as a 'responsible acceleration' function: its job is to identify and resolve ethical risks early, when changes are cheap, not to review finished products. Define two things in the charter: (1) The board's purpose — protect the organization's stated values in AI deployment. (2) The board's success metric — time from ethics flag to resolution, not number of deployments blocked."
strategy.antipattern: Creating an ethics board whose implicit purpose is to say 'no' to AI initiatives — producing a governance structure that is adversarial to the product teams it is supposed to guide.

governance.description: The board's governance structure is designed with clear composition, real decision authority, defined escalation paths, and a cadence that can keep pace with the organization's AI deployment rhythm.
governance.example: AI: "For an ethics board with real authority, four design decisions: (1) Composition — include external independent members (at least 30%) who are not employees and have no financial interest in deployment speed. (2) Authority — the board must have explicit pause authority for deployments that fail ethics review; advisory-only boards are theater. (3) Escalation — define who the board escalates to when deadlocked: CEO, audit committee, or both. (4) Cadence — standing monthly meeting plus an async channel for urgent reviews that cannot wait 30 days. What is the fastest your organization currently ships an AI feature? That rhythm determines the async review SLA."
governance.antipattern: Designing an ethics board that meets quarterly and has a 60-day review cycle — making it structurally unable to influence AI deployments that ship on a 2-week sprint cycle.

risk-ethics.description: The board's scope covers the full ethical risk landscape — not just fairness and bias, but mission alignment, societal impact, dual-use risks, and the ethical implications of what the organization chooses not to build.
risk-ethics.example: Human: "What ethical risks should the board be able to review?" AI: "The WEF Playbook identifies five ethical risk categories that the board should own: (1) Fairness and discrimination — AI that produces disparate outcomes for protected groups. (2) Privacy and surveillance — AI that collects or infers more than necessary. (3) Mission alignment — AI that serves financial objectives at the expense of the organization's stated purpose. (4) Dual-use — AI that could be used for purposes the organization has not sanctioned. (5) Systemic risk — AI at scale that could harm markets, democracies, or public trust even if individual uses are benign. Define which categories require mandatory board review versus optional escalation."
risk-ethics.antipattern: Limiting the ethics board's scope to fairness and bias metrics — the most measurable risk category — while omitting mission alignment, dual-use, and systemic risks that are harder to quantify but often more consequential.

implementation.description: The ethics board is integrated into the development workflow through a defined review gate — triggered by risk criteria, not by voluntary submission — and is supported by a lightweight intake process that product teams can actually use.
implementation.example: AI: "For workflow integration, define two review triggers: (1) Mandatory review — any AI feature that: (a) affects a decision about an individual (hiring, lending, healthcare, benefits); (b) involves sensitive data categories; (c) is deployed at scale (>100,000 users); or (d) is in a new domain or use case not previously reviewed. (2) Voluntary review — product teams can escalate any AI feature they have questions about. Design a one-page intake form: describe the AI, its users, its decisions, and the three ethical risks you see. That form is the only pre-meeting requirement. The board reviews the form before the meeting and asks questions — not a presentation, a conversation."
implementation.antipattern: Requiring product teams to prepare a 20-page ethics impact assessment before requesting board review — creating a bureaucratic burden that incentivizes teams to find reasons their product does not require review.

accountability.description: The board's decisions and reasoning are documented and disclosed — to the organization, to regulators, and in some cases to the public — creating an accountability record that demonstrates responsible AI governance in practice.
accountability.example: AI closes the setup cycle: "Board accountability mechanisms: (1) Decision log — every board decision (approve, approve-with-conditions, pause, reject) is logged with a one-paragraph rationale. The log is reviewed quarterly by the audit committee. (2) Annual ethics report — the board chair presents an annual summary to the board of directors: how many reviews, how many conditions imposed, how many pauses, and what systemic themes emerged. (3) External disclosure — the organization's annual responsible AI report includes a summary of ethics board activity. (4) Whistleblower pathway — employees can escalate concerns about AI deployments directly to the board chair, bypassing the product team."
accountability.antipattern: Operating an ethics board whose deliberations and decisions are entirely internal and undisclosed — making it impossible for external stakeholders, regulators, or the public to assess whether the board is functioning effectively.

collaboration.description: Board mandate is grounded in organizational AI strategy → governance structure with real authority is designed → ethical risk scope is defined across five categories → review gate is integrated into the development workflow → accountability mechanisms make board impact visible. Loops back to governance if implementation reveals the board's cadence cannot keep pace with the deployment rhythm.

sequence.label: Ground the board mandate in AI strategy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Leadership has approved an AI ethics board. How do we frame its mandate?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Write the mandate in the charter with two sentences: (1) Purpose — 'The AI Ethics Board exists to ensure that the organization's AI deployments are consistent with its stated values of [X] and its commitments to [Y].' Fill in the actual values and commitments from the AI strategy document. (2) Success metric — 'The board's effectiveness is measured by the time from ethics flag to resolution, and by the quality of responsible AI practices embedded in the development process — not by the number of deployments reviewed or blocked.' This framing makes the board a collaborator with product teams, not a gatekeeper.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Mandate drafted: purpose tied to organizational values, success metric is resolution speed and practice quality.

sequence.triggers_next: Board mandate drafted with strategic grounding and success metric.

sequence.label: Design composition, authority, escalation, and cadence

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Who should be on the board and how often should it meet?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Composition: 5-7 members — (1) 2 external independent members with AI ethics, law, or civil society expertise; no financial stake in the organization. (2) 1 customer or community representative if the AI affects external stakeholders at scale. (3) Chief Ethics or Responsible AI Officer as chair. (4) Representatives from legal, product, and data science in a non-voting advisory capacity. Authority: the board has explicit pause authority for any mandatory-review deployment; decisions require majority vote. Escalation: deadlock goes to CEO within 48 hours. Cadence: monthly standing meeting (2 hours) plus an async Slack or email channel for urgent reviews with a 72-hour SLA. The async channel is the most important operational design decision — it is what makes the board usable at sprint speed.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Five-member board approved including two external members. Async channel with 72-hour SLA confirmed.

sequence.triggers_next: Governance structure approved: composition, authority, escalation, and cadence defined.

loop_back.condition: Implementation reveals the 72-hour async SLA cannot be met with the current member workload — cadence requires redesign.
loop_back.reason: A board that cannot operate at deployment speed is structurally ineffective — governance must be re-designed.

sequence.label: Define the board's ethical risk scope

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What should the board be able to review — and what is out of scope?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: In scope — mandatory review triggers: (1) AI decisions about individuals in regulated domains (employment, credit, healthcare, benefits, criminal justice). (2) AI involving sensitive data (health, biometric, financial, children's data). (3) AI deployed to more than 100,000 users. (4) AI in a new domain not previously reviewed. (5) AI with identified dual-use potential. Optional escalation — any AI feature a product team has questions about. Out of scope — AI infrastructure (APIs, databases) with no direct user-facing decision function; AI for internal analytics with no individual impact. Publish the in/out scope criteria so product teams can self-assess.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Scope criteria approved and published to all product teams.

sequence.triggers_next: Ethical risk scope defined and published.

sequence.label: Integrate the review gate into the development workflow

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we make the ethics review part of the standard development process?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three integration points: (1) Design gate — at the point where a product manager writes the AI feature specification, they complete the one-page ethics intake form and self-assess against the mandatory review criteria. If criteria are met, they submit to the board — this is not optional. (2) Pre-deployment gate — the deployment checklist includes a confirmation field: 'Ethics board review completed (date) or ethics criteria not triggered (rationale).' No deployment proceeds without one of the two. (3) Post-deployment monitoring — for features approved with conditions, the product team reports back to the board at 90 days with outcome data. The intake form is the key friction point — keep it to one page or adoption will fail.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: One-page intake form finalized. Design gate and deployment checklist updated. Engineering VP signed off.

sequence.triggers_next: Ethics review gate integrated into design and deployment workflow.

sequence.label: Design decision logging and external disclosure

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we show externally that the ethics board is working?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four accountability mechanisms: (1) Decision log — every board decision logged with: date, feature reviewed, decision (approve/approve-with-conditions/pause/reject), one-paragraph rationale. Log reviewed quarterly by the audit committee. (2) Annual ethics report to the board of directors — chair presents: total reviews, condition rate, pause rate, systemic themes (e.g., 'fairness in hiring AI was the most common concern in Q3'). (3) External disclosure — the organization's responsible AI report includes a summary of board activity — not individual case details, but aggregate statistics and themes. (4) Employee escalation pathway — employees can submit concerns directly to the board chair email; all submissions acknowledged within 5 business days.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Decision log template created. Annual report to board of directors committed. Employee escalation email established.

sequence.triggers_next: Accountability mechanisms operational; board launched.

transitions.trigger: Board mandate drafted with strategic grounding.
Governance structure approved.
Ethical risk scope defined and published.
Cadence cannot match deployment rhythm — redesign governance.
Review gate integrated into workflow.
Annual review — reassess board mandate against strategic evolution.

inclusive-growth.description: The audit assesses whether the AI system distributes its benefits and burdens equitably across demographic groups — identifying which groups are systematically advantaged or disadvantaged by the system's outputs.
inclusive-growth.example: Audit lead: "Our hiring AI has been in production for 18 months. The audit starts with distribution: are qualified candidates from all demographic groups advancing through the funnel at equivalent rates?" AI: "To measure equitable distribution, I need three datasets: application pool demographics, advancement rates at each stage, and baseline qualification rates by group. With these, I can compute disparity ratios and identify where in the funnel disparities concentrate."
inclusive-growth.antipattern: Auditing only aggregate accuracy metrics (overall precision, recall) without disaggregating results by protected group — missing systematic disparities that are invisible in aggregate statistics.

human-centred.description: The audit verifies that the system respects fundamental rights — particularly non-discrimination — and that its design choices are consistent with democratic values and human dignity.
human-centred.example: AI: "Beyond disparity metrics, the human-centred audit should check: (1) What proxy variables does the model use that correlate with protected characteristics? (2) Is there a mechanism for candidates to challenge AI-assisted decisions? (3) Are decision criteria consistent with applicable non-discrimination law in every deployment jurisdiction?"
human-centred.antipattern: Treating the fairness audit as purely statistical, without examining whether the system's design choices (features selected, objectives optimized) are themselves ethically justified.

transparency.description: The audit produces findings that are explainable to affected individuals and decision-makers — not just technical reports for data scientists. Affected candidates should be able to understand how the AI influenced their outcome.
transparency.example: Human: "The audit found that 'years of continuous employment' is a top predictive feature. Is that explainable to affected candidates?" AI: "That feature has a disparate impact on candidates who took career breaks for caregiving — disproportionately women. It may also violate applicable employment law. The explanation to a candidate should not just be 'this feature was used' but 'here is how it affected your score and here is how to request a human review.' That requires an explanation interface, not just a technical finding."
transparency.antipattern: Publishing an audit report in technical language inaccessible to the affected individuals, HR teams, or regulators — producing compliance theater without genuine transparency.

robustness.description: The audit methodology is itself validated — checking for data quality issues, sample size sufficiency, methodology limitations, and whether findings are stable across reasonable parameter variations.
robustness.example: AI: "Before finalizing the disparity findings, validate the audit methodology: (1) Is the sample size sufficient for statistical significance at the reported group level? (2) Are the demographic labels in the dataset accurate and self-reported? (3) Does the disparity ratio change materially if you use a different fairness metric (demographic parity vs. equalized odds)? Audit findings that are sensitive to metric choice need explicit qualification."
robustness.antipattern: Presenting audit findings as definitive without assessing the statistical reliability of the methodology — especially for small subgroup populations where sample sizes may be insufficient.

accountability.description: The audit produces a remediation plan with named owners, timelines, and success criteria — not just findings. Accountability extends to disclosing the audit to relevant stakeholders and regulators.
accountability.example: AI closes the audit: "Remediation accountability matrix: (1) Feature removal — 'years of continuous employment' replaced with validated alternative. Owner: ML team. Target: 60 days. (2) Candidate appeals mechanism — human review pathway added to hiring platform. Owner: HR tech. Target: 90 days. (3) Regulatory disclosure — audit report filed with the labor regulator. Owner: legal. Target: 30 days. (4) Re-audit — scheduled 6 months after remediation. Owner: audit lead."
accountability.antipattern: Completing the audit and circulating the report internally without a remediation plan, owner assignments, or a commitment to re-audit after changes are made.

collaboration.description: Equitable distribution analysis identifies disparity patterns by group → human-centred review checks feature choices and rights compliance → findings are translated into explanations accessible to affected parties → audit methodology is validated for robustness → remediation plan with owners and re-audit commitment closes the cycle.

sequence.label: Analyze benefit and burden distribution across groups

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Run a fairness audit on our AI hiring tool. Start with distribution.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: To compute equitable distribution, I'll need: application pool demographics for the past 12 months, stage-by-stage advancement rates broken out by gender, age band, and ethnicity, and baseline qualification rates by group for external benchmarking. With these three datasets, I can produce disparity ratios at each funnel stage and identify where the AI's influence is concentrated. Can you provide the data or access to the analytics system?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Data is available. Disparity ratios show a 1.4 ratio for women at the technical screen stage.

sequence.triggers_next: Disparity ratios computed; high-disparity stages identified.

sequence.label: Review feature design and rights compliance

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The technical screen disparity is concentrated in two features: 'GitHub activity' and 'years of continuous employment.' Is this a rights issue?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Yes — both features have documented disparate impact on women and caregivers. GitHub activity correlates with unpaid open-source contribution time — structurally unequal across gender and socioeconomic groups. 'Years of continuous employment' penalizes career breaks. In the EU and many US states, using these features without validation of their job-relatedness creates legal exposure under non-discrimination law. Recommendation: immediately flag both features for replacement and document the legal risk finding in the audit report.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Legal is reviewing. Flag both features as high-risk in the audit.

sequence.triggers_next: Rights and feature compliance findings documented.

loop_back.condition: Feature replacement changes the disparity pattern — distribution must be re-measured.
loop_back.reason: Removing a feature can shift disparities to other groups; re-measurement validates the fix.

sequence.label: Translate findings into explanations for affected individuals

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we explain the audit findings to the 340 candidates affected in the audit period?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three communication levels: (1) Affected candidates — a plain-language notice: 'Our AI screening tool has been audited and found to use criteria that may have disadvantaged some candidates. If you applied in the past 12 months and were rejected at the technical screen, you may request a human review.' (2) HR leadership — the disparity ratio findings with feature attribution. (3) Regulators — full technical audit report with methodology. Draft the candidate notice first — that is the most time-sensitive accountability action.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Agreed. Draft the candidate notice for HR review.

sequence.triggers_next: Candidate communication drafted; regulatory report scoped.

sequence.label: Validate audit methodology and qualify findings

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Is our sample size sufficient? We had 340 cases but some subgroups are small.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: For the disparity ratio to be statistically reliable at the 95% confidence level, you need at least 30 cases per subgroup. Check: (1) Women at technical screen — if n < 30, qualify the finding as directional rather than definitive. (2) Intersectional groups (e.g., women of color) may have n < 10 — do not report disparity ratios for these subgroups; note the gap as a data limitation requiring a larger longitudinal study. This qualification protects the audit from being challenged on statistical grounds.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Women n=68 — sufficient. Intersectional groups n=12 — will qualify.

sequence.triggers_next: Methodology validated; findings qualified where necessary.

sequence.label: Issue remediation plan with owners and re-audit commitment

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we close the audit with clear accountability?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Audit close accountability matrix: (1) Feature replacement — ML team removes 'GitHub activity' and 'continuous employment' and runs validation test before re-deployment. Target: 60 days. (2) Candidate appeals — HR tech adds human review pathway to rejected candidates. Target: 90 days. (3) Candidate notice — sent within 14 days of audit approval. (4) Regulatory disclosure — full report filed with relevant labor authority within 30 days. (5) Re-audit — scheduled 6 months after feature changes are deployed. All items require sign-off from CHRO before the audit is closed.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: CHRO signed off. Audit closed with remediation plan.

sequence.triggers_next: Audit closed with remediation plan, regulatory disclosure, and re-audit date.

transitions.trigger: Disparity ratios computed and high-disparity stages identified.
Feature replacement changes disparity pattern — re-measure.
Rights and feature compliance findings documented.
Candidate communication drafted; regulatory report scoped.
Methodology validated and findings qualified.
Re-audit cycle begins 6 months after remediation.

govern.description: The incident response governance structure is activated: the incident commander is identified, escalation thresholds are applied, and the AI system's risk owner is notified and engaged within the defined SLA.
govern.example: Incident commander: "The AI-generated customer communication contained incorrect legal information sent to 2,300 customers. I'm activating our AI incident protocol. Risk owner and legal are notified. Severity is High — system is paused pending investigation."
govern.antipattern: Treating an AI incident as a software bug fix without activating risk governance — allowing the response to be owned by engineering alone, without legal, compliance, or leadership involvement.

map.description: The team maps the incident: what the AI did, which customers or systems were affected, what data was involved, whether regulatory notification is triggered, and what caused the failure.
map.example: AI: "Based on the incident log, I can help map four dimensions: (1) Output scope — what was generated and sent. (2) Affected population — who received it and any vulnerable groups. (3) Root cause candidates — model error, prompt injection, data pipeline issue, or deployment configuration. (4) Regulatory triggers — do breach notification or AI incident reporting obligations apply? Which do you want to map first?"
map.antipattern: Jumping to root cause analysis before scoping who was affected — fixing the technical problem while customers remain harmed and notifications are delayed.

measure.description: The team quantifies the incident's impact: number of affected parties, severity of harm, legal exposure, reputational damage potential, and the likelihood of recurrence without remediation.
measure.example: Human: "Legal says the incorrect information could expose us to consumer protection complaints. How do we measure the legal risk?" AI: "Measure it along two axes: (1) Volume — 2,300 affected customers is above most jurisdictions' mandatory notification thresholds. (2) Materiality — if any customer acted on the incorrect information (e.g., missed a deadline), individual harm is material. Pull a sample of 50 customers for a harm survey before filing the incident report."
measure.antipattern: Reporting an incident as "low impact" based on the volume alone without assessing whether individual harm occurred — underestimating legal exposure.

manage.description: The team executes the response: customer remediation, regulatory notification if required, root cause fix, system restart protocol, and a post-incident review that updates the risk register and governance policy.
manage.example: AI closes the response: "Here is the remediation matrix: (1) Customer remediation — correction notice sent within 24 hours, owner: customer success. (2) Regulatory notification — filed within 72 hours if notification is triggered, owner: legal. (3) Root cause fix — output validation layer added to the prompt pipeline, owner: engineering. (4) Post-incident review — scheduled for next week, output feeds back into the risk register, owner: risk lead."
manage.antipattern: Closing the incident after the technical fix without conducting a post-incident review — missing the governance improvement loop and allowing the same failure to recur.

collaboration.description: Governance activates the incident protocol and escalates within SLA → the team maps affected scope, root cause candidates, and regulatory triggers → impact is measured across volume, severity, and legal dimensions → remediation is executed with owners and timelines, and a post-incident review closes the loop back into governance. Loops back to Map if new affected parties are discovered during measurement.

sequence.label: Activate incident governance and escalate

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Our AI recommendation engine served a batch of incorrect outputs to users this morning. We're seeing complaints. What's the first step?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: First, activate your AI incident protocol: (1) Name an incident commander now — this can't be the engineer who runs the system. (2) Pause the AI system if you can't immediately determine the scope of incorrect outputs. (3) Notify the AI risk owner within your defined SLA — if you don't have one, escalate to the most senior accountable leader immediately. (4) Open an incident ticket with timestamp. Who is the named incident commander?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: I'm taking incident command. Risk owner is the Head of Product. System is paused. Ticket opened at 09:14.

sequence.triggers_next: Incident command activated, system paused, risk owner notified.

sequence.label: Map scope, affected parties, and root cause candidates

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Logs show the model returned outputs outside its expected range for about 400 users between 08:00 and 09:14. Help me map the full scope.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Scope map: (1) Affected users — 400 users received anomalous outputs. Check whether any are in protected categories (minors, healthcare, financial decisions). (2) Data involved — what personal data did the model process for these users? (3) Root cause candidates — a/b test misconfiguration, upstream data pipeline failure, model update without validation, or prompt injection. (4) Regulatory triggers — does your jurisdiction require notification for AI incidents involving personal data? I'd check GDPR Art. 33 and any sector-specific AI incident rules.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Some users are in the healthcare domain. That changes the severity.

sequence.triggers_next: Affected scope, data categories, and regulatory triggers mapped.

loop_back.condition: Measurement reveals additional affected users or data categories not in the initial map.
loop_back.reason: Scope must be updated before regulatory notifications are filed.

sequence.label: Measure impact severity and legal exposure

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: 400 users, healthcare domain, some may have received treatment recommendations. How do we measure this?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Measure along three axes: (1) Clinical harm potential — did any user receive a recommendation they could have acted on? Pull a sample of 20 outputs and have a clinician review. (2) Legal exposure — healthcare AI incidents in most EU and US jurisdictions trigger mandatory incident reporting. Check your sector regulator. (3) Reputational impact — if this becomes public, what is the narrative risk? Prepare a holding statement now, before any media inquiry. Severity: Critical pending clinical review.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Clinical review in progress. Legal filing timeline confirmed: 72 hours.

sequence.triggers_next: Impact severity quantified, legal exposure assessed, notification timeline set.

sequence.label: Execute remediation and close with post-incident review

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Clinical review found no actionable harm, but we're notifying all 400 users. Root cause was a misconfigured A/B test. How do we close?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Remediation matrix: (1) User notification — send within 24 hours, include what happened and what to do if they acted on the output. Owner: customer success. (2) Regulatory — file incident report to sector regulator within 72-hour window. Owner: legal. (3) Technical fix — disable A/B test framework for AI systems until validation gates are added. Owner: engineering, target: today. (4) Post-incident review — scheduled for 5 business days, outputs update the risk register and A/B test governance policy. Owner: you as incident commander. This cycle restarts at the next AI system launch or quarterly AI risk review.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Agreed. All four owners confirmed.

sequence.triggers_next: Remediation complete, regulatory filed, post-incident review scheduled.

transitions.trigger: Incident command activated and system paused.
Scope, affected parties, and regulatory triggers mapped.
New affected parties or data categories discovered.
Impact severity and legal exposure assessed.
Post-incident review triggers governance policy update — cycle restarts.

context.description: The audit verifies that the AIMS scope and organizational context remain valid — checking whether changes in the organization's AI footprint, regulatory environment, or stakeholder landscape have rendered the current scope obsolete.
context.example: Auditor: "The AIMS was scoped to five AI systems 12 months ago. We now have eight. Is the scope still valid?" AI: "Scope validity check: (1) The three new AI systems must be assessed for inclusion — are they within the AIMS boundary as currently defined? If yes, they are a nonconformity: in-scope systems not covered by the AIMS. If no, a scope update is required to explicitly exclude them with documented rationale. (2) Have any new regulations come into force in the past 12 months that affect the AIMS scope? (3) Have any new stakeholders emerged — a new regulator, a new customer segment, a new supplier — who should be in the interested parties register? Document findings for each."
context.antipattern: Auditing the AIMS against the original scope document without checking whether the scope is still accurate — allowing new AI systems to operate outside the AIMS boundary undetected.

leadership.description: The audit verifies that top management commitment is genuine and current — checking whether the AI policy has been reviewed, whether the AI management representative has the authority and resources needed, and whether AI risk appears in executive reporting.
