leadership.example: AI: "Leadership evidence checklist for the audit: (1) AI policy review — was the AI policy reviewed in the past 12 months? Who approved the review? (2) Management representative authority — have there been cases where the AI management representative needed to make a decision and could not due to resource or authority constraints? (3) Executive reporting — pull the last four board or executive reports: does AI risk appear as a standing item? If AI risk only appears when there is an incident, governance is reactive, not systematic."
leadership.antipattern: Accepting a signed AI policy as evidence of leadership commitment without checking whether the policy has been reviewed, whether the management representative has real authority, and whether AI risk is integrated into executive reporting.

planning.description: The audit assesses progress against the AIMS objectives set in the previous planning cycle — verifying that risk treatments were implemented on schedule, that objectives are still measurable, and that the risk register is current.
planning.example: Human: "Three AIMS objectives were set last year. How do we audit progress?" AI: "For each objective: (1) Is the objective still measurable — is the metric and target still valid, or has it become obsolete due to system changes? (2) What is the current performance against target — provide evidence, not narrative. (3) For objectives not on track — what is the root cause? Was the target unrealistic, was the treatment action not implemented, or did circumstances change? (4) Risk register currency — pull five risk entries at random: are treatment actions implemented? Are residual risk ratings still accurate given changes in the past 12 months?"
planning.antipattern: Reporting AIMS objectives as 'in progress' without providing quantitative evidence of current performance against target — producing an audit finding that cannot distinguish between genuine progress and stalled implementation.

operation.description: The audit samples operational records to verify that documented AIMS processes are being followed in practice — checking model cards, change control logs, incident logs, and supplier documentation.
operation.example: AI: "Operational sampling for the audit — pull records for three AI systems deployed in the past 12 months and verify: (1) Model card completeness — does the model card exist, is it current (updated within 30 days of the last material change), and does it contain all required fields? (2) Change control — were there material AI system changes? Was a documented impact assessment completed before deployment? (3) Incident log — are all AI incidents logged? Sample three months and cross-reference with the service desk for unreported incidents. (4) Supplier documentation — are model cards or technical specifications on file for all AI suppliers?"
operation.antipattern: Auditing AIMS operational processes by interviewing process owners rather than sampling records — hearing how processes are supposed to work rather than verifying that they actually work.

performance.description: The audit produces formal findings, nonconformities, and observations — and feeds these into a management review that produces a written AIMS effectiveness conclusion and an approved corrective action plan.
performance.example: AI closes the audit cycle: "Audit summary for management review: Two major nonconformities — (1) Three AI systems deployed in the past 6 months have no model cards (Clause 8, Operation). Root cause: new development team was not trained on AIMS documentation requirements. Required action: training completed, retrospective model cards drafted. (2) Risk register not updated for 9 months — two risk treatments overdue (Clause 6, Planning). Required action: risk register reviewed and updated within 30 days. Two observations — scope does not cover three new AI systems; AI policy has not been reviewed in 18 months. Management review must produce a written AIMS effectiveness conclusion and approve corrective action owners and deadlines."
performance.antipattern: Completing the internal audit, identifying nonconformities, and then allowing the management review to be cancelled or deferred — leaving nonconformities without corrective actions and the AIMS without a formal effectiveness determination.

collaboration.description: AIMS scope validity is verified against current AI footprint → leadership commitment evidence is checked against four concrete indicators → planning objective progress is assessed with quantitative evidence → operational records are sampled for process conformity → audit findings feed the management review which produces corrective actions and an effectiveness conclusion.

sequence.label: Verify AIMS scope validity and context currency

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're running the annual AIMS internal audit. Start with context.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Scope validity check — four questions: (1) How many AI systems are in production today versus when the scope was last updated? Any new systems must be assessed for AIMS inclusion. (2) Have any new regulations come into force affecting the AIMS scope — EU AI Act, sector-specific AI rules? (3) Are all interested parties in the register still current — any new regulators, customers, or suppliers? (4) Has the organizational context changed — mergers, acquisitions, new markets — that affects the AI risk landscape? Document findings for each; scope gaps are a likely nonconformity finding.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Three new AI systems not in scope. EU AI Act now applies — not reflected in the AIMS. Two interested parties added.

sequence.triggers_next: Scope validity findings documented — three new systems and regulatory gap identified.

sequence.label: Check leadership commitment evidence

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we audit leadership commitment beyond checking the signed AI policy?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four evidence checks: (1) AI policy review — request the review record: who reviewed it, when, who approved it? If no review in the past 12 months, that is an observation (not a major nonconformity unless the policy is materially outdated). (2) Management representative — interview them: have they had authority or resource constraints in the past 12 months that prevented AIMS decisions? If yes, document specific cases. (3) Executive reporting — review board minutes or executive reports for the past four quarters. Does AI risk appear as a standing agenda item? (4) AIMS budget — was the approved AIMS budget fully allocated? If significant budget was rescinded, that is a leadership commitment finding.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: AI policy not reviewed in 18 months. Executive reports show AI risk only appears in incident months. Observation documented.

sequence.triggers_next: Leadership commitment evidence assessed; two observations documented.

sequence.label: Assess objective progress and risk register currency

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we audit the three AIMS objectives from last year's planning cycle?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: For each objective, pull quantitative evidence: (1) 'All new AI deployments have a model card by Q3' — how many deployments occurred? How many have model cards? If three of six have cards, that is a 50% achievement rate against a 100% target — a major nonconformity. (2) 'Zero unlogged AI incidents by Q2' — cross-reference the incident log with service desk records. Any discrepancy is a nonconformity. (3) 'First internal audit completed by Q4' — we are doing it now, so this objective is met. For the risk register: pull five entries at random and check: are treatment actions implemented? Are target dates past due? Overdue treatment actions are a nonconformity finding.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Model card objective: 3 of 6 — major nonconformity. Risk register: two overdue treatments — major nonconformity.

sequence.triggers_next: Two major nonconformities documented from planning audit.

loop_back.condition: Planning audit reveals that the risk register was not updated when new AI systems were added — scope and planning are linked nonconformities.
loop_back.reason: Linked nonconformities require both context and planning sections to be re-audited together.

sequence.label: Sample operational records for process conformity

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Which operational records should we sample for the audit?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Sample three AI systems deployed in the past 12 months. For each, check: (1) Model card — exists, is current, contains required fields. (2) Change control — pull the change log: were there material changes? Was an impact assessment completed before deployment? (3) Incident log — pull the past 6 months. Cross-reference with the service desk: any incidents reported to the service desk but not in the AIMS incident log? (4) Supplier documentation — pull the AI supplier register: do all suppliers have model cards or technical specifications on file? Sampling is sufficient for the audit — you are not required to check every system.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Three systems sampled. Two have no model cards — already flagged. One change control record missing impact assessment — additional nonconformity.

sequence.triggers_next: Operational sampling complete — third major nonconformity identified.

sequence.label: Conduct management review and approve corrective actions

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We have three major nonconformities. How do we close the audit?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Management review agenda: (1) Audit findings summary — present all three major nonconformities with root cause analysis. Do not just list findings; explain why each occurred. (2) Corrective action approval — for each nonconformity, management approves: the corrective action, the owner, and the target date. Required: (a) Model cards for all six deployments within 30 days; training for new dev team. Owner: AIMS manager. (b) Risk register reviewed and all overdue treatments completed within 30 days. Owner: risk lead. (c) Change control process retrained and missing impact assessment documented retrospectively. Owner: engineering lead. (3) AIMS effectiveness conclusion — management must formally conclude: is the AIMS achieving its intended outcomes? Document the conclusion in the review minutes. (4) Next audit date — set the date for the next internal audit now.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Management review complete. Three corrective actions approved with owners. AIMS effectiveness conclusion: Partially Effective — improvement required.

sequence.triggers_next: Management review complete; corrective actions approved; next audit date set.

transitions.trigger: Scope validity findings documented.
Leadership commitment evidence assessed.
Risk register gap linked to scope — re-audit context.
Planning objective progress assessed; nonconformities documented.
Operational sampling complete.
Next annual audit cycle begins.

govern.description: Before assessing any risk, the team confirms that governance structures are in place: a named AI risk owner, a defined risk appetite, and a clear mandate for who can accept residual risk.
govern.example: Risk governance lead: "Before we map any risks, I need us to confirm three things: who owns this AI system's risk posture, what our risk appetite is for automated decisions in this domain, and what sign-off is required before deployment."
govern.antipattern: Starting a risk assessment without an owner — producing a risk register that no one is accountable for acting on.

map.description: The team systematically identifies what the AI system does, who is affected (including non-users), what can go wrong, and where risks originate — distinguishing technical, operational, and societal risks.
map.example: AI: "Based on the system description, I can identify six risk categories: data quality risks affecting prediction accuracy, fairness risks for the demographic groups in scope, security risks from adversarial inputs, operational risks from model drift, legal risks from the jurisdiction's AI regulations, and reputational risks from public misperception. Which of these are within scope for this assessment?"
map.antipattern: Mapping only technical risks (model accuracy, latency) and omitting societal, legal, and operational risks that are harder to quantify.

measure.description: Each mapped risk is scored for likelihood and impact using consistent criteria. The team uses evidence from testing, historical data, and stakeholder input — not gut feel — to justify scores.
measure.example: Human: "The AI flags fairness risk. How do we score likelihood?" AI: "I recommend using your testing data: run the model outputs across demographic groups and compare disparity ratios. If the disparity ratio exceeds your defined threshold, likelihood of harm is high regardless of intent."
measure.antipattern: Scoring risks subjectively without evidence — producing scores that reflect the team's optimism rather than the system's actual behavior.

manage.description: The team selects a treatment for each measured risk (accept, mitigate, avoid, transfer), documents residual risk, assigns owners, and defines monitoring checkpoints for post-deployment.
manage.example: Risk owner: "For the fairness risk — treatment is mitigation via quarterly disparity audits. Owner is the data team lead. Residual risk is accepted at Low. If disparity ratio exceeds threshold, the system is paused pending review." AI: "I'll draft the risk register entry with those fields. Do you want me to also generate the monitoring alert specification?"
manage.antipattern: Closing a risk entry after selecting mitigation without documenting residual risk or a monitoring trigger — leaving the risk effectively untracked after deployment.

collaboration.description: Governance readiness is confirmed before any risk work begins → risks are mapped across technical, operational, societal, and legal dimensions → each risk is scored using evidence → treatment plans with owners and monitoring triggers are documented. Loops back to Map if measurement reveals unmapped risks.

sequence.label: Confirm governance readiness and risk ownership

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're about to assess a new loan-decisioning AI. Before we start mapping risks, can you help us confirm what governance needs to be in place?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three things need to be confirmed first: (1) Who is the named risk owner with authority to accept or reject deployment? (2) What is your organization's risk appetite for automated decisions in lending — do you have a documented threshold? (3) What approval is required before deployment proceeds? Without these, any risk register we produce will be orphaned.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The CRO owns lending AI risk, our appetite is Low for decisions affecting credit access, and deployment requires CRO sign-off plus legal review.

sequence.triggers_next: Risk owner named, appetite defined, approval path confirmed.

sequence.label: Map risks across all affected dimensions

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The system uses applicant financial data and a proprietary model to automate 80% of loan decisions. Map the risk landscape.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I'll map six risk categories: (1) Data quality — training data may not reflect current economic conditions. (2) Fairness — automated decisions at scale amplify demographic disparities. (3) Security — model inversion attacks could expose applicant data. (4) Operational — model drift without monitoring could degrade accuracy undetected. (5) Legal — jurisdiction-specific fair lending laws may impose explainability requirements. (6) Reputational — public perception of algorithmic lending decisions. Which require deeper mapping?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Fairness and legal are top priority — map those in detail.

sequence.triggers_next: Risk categories mapped with stakeholders and impact domains identified.

loop_back.condition: Measurement reveals a risk not captured in the initial map.
loop_back.reason: Scope must be extended before the new risk can be measured.

sequence.label: Score each risk using evidence and consistent criteria

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We ran the model on a holdout set. Demographic disparity ratio is 1.34 for one protected group. How do we score this?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: A ratio of 1.34 is above the 1.25 threshold used by most fair lending regulators as a trigger for adverse impact review. Likelihood of harm: High. Impact: High, given the credit access consequences and legal exposure. This scores as a Critical risk — it requires mitigation, not acceptance.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What evidence do we need for the legal risk score?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: For legal risk, you need a jurisdiction mapping: list every jurisdiction where the system will operate and check each against applicable AI and fair lending regulation. The evidence is the legal review memo, not the model output.

sequence.triggers_next: All priority risks scored with evidence citations.

sequence.label: Define treatment, residual risk, and monitoring for each risk

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: For the fairness risk — what treatment options do we have?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three options: (1) Mitigate — rebalance training data and re-evaluate; target disparity ratio below 1.15. (2) Avoid — remove the demographic-correlated features from the model. (3) Transfer — purchase fairness insurance and accept current disparity with disclosed risk. Given your Low risk appetite, option 1 or 2 is required. Which do you want in the risk register?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Option 1. Owner is data science lead, target date is next sprint, monitoring is monthly disparity audit. Residual risk: Medium pending re-evaluation.

sequence.triggers_next: Risk register complete, all entries have treatment, owner, timeline, and residual risk.

transitions.trigger: Risk owner named, appetite defined, approval path confirmed.
Risk landscape mapped with stakeholders and impact domains.
New risk identified during measurement — not in original map.
All priority risks scored with evidence.
New AI system or material change triggers a new assessment cycle.

entries.title: AI Strategy Alignment with Organizational Purpose

strategy.description: The leadership team articulates how AI initiatives connect to the organization's stated purpose, values, and societal commitments — and uses this connection as a filter for AI investment decisions.
strategy.example: CEO: "We've approved six AI initiatives this year. I want to know which ones actually serve our mission as a public health organization." AI: "Apply the purpose test to each initiative: (1) Does this AI initiative serve patients and communities, or does it primarily serve operational efficiency for the organization? (2) Could this AI use create a conflict of interest between our mission and our financial incentives? (3) If this initiative became public tomorrow, would it be consistent with how we want to be seen? Run all six initiatives through these three questions and rank them by mission alignment before the next board meeting."
strategy.antipattern: Approving AI initiatives based exclusively on ROI projections and operational efficiency gains, with no mechanism to verify whether they serve the organization's stated mission and societal purpose.

governance.description: The organization designs an oversight structure for responsible AI with real decision authority — including who can pause or veto deployments, how ethics concerns are escalated, and what the governance cadence is.
governance.example: AI: "For responsible AI governance, the WEF Playbook recommends three layers: (1) Operational — an AI review team embedded in the product and engineering workflow that screens every AI deployment before it goes live. (2) Strategic — an AI ethics committee at the VP/Director level with authority to pause high-stakes deployments. (3) Board — an AI governance item on the board agenda at least quarterly, with a named board member as AI accountability sponsor. Which layer is currently missing or weakest in your organization?"
governance.antipattern: Creating an AI ethics committee that meets quarterly and can only advise — with no authority to pause deployments and no connection to operational AI decisions being made daily in product teams.

risk-ethics.description: The organization assesses the ethical risks of its AI portfolio — not just technical risks — including who could be harmed, how, and whether the harm is proportionate to the benefit claimed.
risk-ethics.example: Human: "Our patient scheduling AI prioritizes patients with better payment histories. Is that an ethical risk?" AI: "Yes — this is a high-severity ethical risk. The harm is direct and systematic: patients with worse payment histories (often lower-income or underinsured) receive worse access to care. In a public health context, this directly conflicts with your mission and may violate health equity law. The WEF Playbook would classify this as an unacceptable ethics risk — a mission conflict, not just a fairness metric. Recommendation: pause this feature and replace the payment history signal with clinically appropriate prioritization criteria."
risk-ethics.antipattern: Treating 'payment history' as a neutral data feature without assessing its societal and mission implications — allowing financial logic to override clinical and ethical judgment.

implementation.description: The organization embeds responsible AI practices into its operational workflows — making ethics reviews, documentation requirements, and fairness checks part of the standard development process, not optional extras.
implementation.example: AI: "To embed responsible AI in your product workflow, I recommend three changes to the current development process: (1) Add an ethics checkpoint at the design phase — before any AI feature is engineered, a one-page ethics impact brief is required. (2) Require an equity review for any AI feature affecting patient access or outcomes — run by clinical and social work staff, not just engineers. (3) Add a responsible AI sign-off to the deployment checklist — a named individual confirms the ethics checkpoint and equity review were completed. These add less than one week to the average release cycle."
implementation.antipattern: Publishing a responsible AI policy and expecting development teams to self-apply it without any process change, tooling, or accountability mechanism — producing policies that exist on paper but not in code.

accountability.description: The organization tracks whether its AI initiatives are achieving their stated purpose, reports transparently on outcomes — including failures — and uses evidence to drive continuous improvement of the responsible AI program.
accountability.example: AI closes the cycle: "Accountability structure for the responsible AI program: (1) Quarterly outcome report — for each AI system, track three metrics: mission alignment score (purpose test results), equity metric (disparity in outcomes across patient groups), and incident count. (2) Annual external report — published to stakeholders including community partners, describing responsible AI commitments and progress. (3) Board accountability item — AI ethics committee presents to the board annually with a forward improvement plan. (4) Whistleblower pathway — staff can report responsible AI concerns anonymously with a named executive reviewer."
accountability.antipattern: Reporting only AI successes externally while keeping failures and ethical concerns internal — producing a responsible AI narrative that is not grounded in transparent evidence.

collaboration.description: AI initiatives are filtered against organizational purpose → governance structure with real authority is designed → ethical risks in the AI portfolio are assessed → responsible AI practices are embedded in development workflows → outcome tracking and external reporting closes the accountability loop. Loops back to strategy if risk-ethics assessment reveals initiatives that conflict with mission.

sequence.label: Filter AI initiatives against organizational purpose

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We have a board-approved AI roadmap with 8 initiatives. How do we know which ones are aligned with our mission?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Run the purpose test on all 8 initiatives. For each, answer: (1) Who is the primary beneficiary — the organization or the people it serves? (2) Does this initiative serve our core mission or a financial objective that happens to use AI? (3) Would this initiative be defensible if reported by a journalist covering 'AI in healthcare'? Score each initiative Red (mission conflict), Yellow (unclear alignment), or Green (clear mission alignment). Share the results before the next board meeting — Red and Yellow items require a strategy discussion, not just an approval.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Three initiatives score Red including the payment-priority scheduler. Two are Yellow. Three are Green.

sequence.triggers_next: AI initiatives scored against mission; conflicts identified.

sequence.label: Design responsible AI governance with real authority

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We have an AI ethics advisory group but it has no authority to stop anything. How do we fix this?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Restructure in three steps: (1) Rename the group to AI Ethics Committee and update the charter to give it explicit pause authority for any AI deployment that scores Red on the purpose test or fails an equity review. (2) Define the escalation path — product team flags concern → AI review team → ethics committee → CEO if committee is deadlocked. (3) Give the committee a direct reporting line to the board's governance committee, not to the CTO. The CTO is an interested party in deployment decisions; the board is the right accountability principal.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Charter update drafted. CEO approved pause authority for the ethics committee.

sequence.triggers_next: Governance structure with real authority approved and documented.

sequence.label: Assess ethical risks in the AI portfolio

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The three Red initiatives — what are the specific ethical risks?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: For the payment-priority scheduler: ethical risk is direct — it systematically disadvantages lower-income patients in access to care. This is a mission conflict and a potential health equity law violation. Recommend: pause immediately, replace with clinically validated priority criteria. For the AI-assisted discharge planning tool: risk is opacity — clinicians cannot see why the model recommends discharge timing, creating accountability gaps. Recommend: require explainability layer before deployment. For the predictive readmission model: risk is feedback loop — if the model is used to ration follow-up care, its errors compound. Recommend: human review required for all model outputs above a risk threshold.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Ethics committee has reviewed all three. Pause orders issued for payment scheduler.

sequence.triggers_next: Ethical risks assessed; pause orders issued for mission-conflicting initiatives.

loop_back.condition: Risk-ethics assessment reveals additional initiatives with mission conflicts not identified in the purpose test.
loop_back.reason: Strategy filter must be re-run with updated criteria before ethics committee reviews remaining portfolio.

sequence.label: Embed responsible AI practices in development workflow

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we make sure the remaining five Green initiatives stay responsible as they're built?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three workflow changes: (1) Ethics impact brief — one-page document required at the design phase for every AI feature. Template: what problem are we solving, who could be harmed, what is the mitigation. Owner: product manager. (2) Equity review — any AI feature affecting patient access or clinical outcomes goes through a 2-hour review with clinical informatics and social work. Owner: clinical lead. (3) Deployment checklist — responsible AI sign-off added as a required gate before any AI feature goes to production. Named individual confirms the ethics brief and equity review were completed. These changes add approximately 3-5 days to the standard release cycle.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Three changes approved by engineering VP. Adding to the SDLC documentation.

sequence.triggers_next: Responsible AI practices embedded in development workflow.

sequence.label: Establish outcome tracking and external reporting

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we demonstrate accountability for our responsible AI program externally?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Accountability framework: (1) Quarterly internal report — AI ethics committee receives: mission alignment scores, equity metrics per system, incident log, open pause items. (2) Annual external report — published to community partners and on the website: responsible AI commitments made, progress against them, and one honest failure and what was learned. (3) Board agenda item — ethics committee presents annually with a forward improvement plan. (4) Community input mechanism — annual open meeting with patient advocacy groups to review the responsible AI program. The external report is the most important item — it creates genuine accountability by making commitments public.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Annual report committed for Q4. Community meeting scheduled. Board agenda item approved.

sequence.triggers_next: Accountability structure operational; external reporting committed.

transitions.trigger: AI initiatives scored against mission; conflicts identified.
Governance structure with real authority approved.
Additional mission-conflicting initiatives discovered — re-run purpose test.
Ethical risks assessed and pause orders issued where needed.
Responsible AI practices embedded in workflow.
Annual review cycle — reassess AI portfolio against mission.

context.description: The organization maps the supplier's role within the AIMS scope — whether the supplier develops, deploys, or provides AI infrastructure — and identifies the specific regulatory and contractual context that governs the supplier relationship.
context.example: AIMS manager: "We are evaluating a cloud AI platform provider. How do we define their role in our AIMS?" AI: "For ISO 42001, supplier classification determines what is required. Three roles to assess: (1) AI system developer — do they develop the AI model itself? If yes, they are a critical AIMS supplier and must meet your AI policy minimums. (2) AI infrastructure provider — do they provide compute, storage, or APIs without developing the model? Different obligations apply. (3) AI-enabled service provider — do they use AI internally in services they provide to you? The most common and often overlooked category. Document the supplier's role before setting assessment requirements."
context.antipattern: Applying the same supplier assessment template to all AI-related vendors regardless of their role — over-burdening infrastructure providers while under-assessing AI developers with more material risk.

leadership.description: The organization evaluates the supplier's own AI governance maturity — whether they have an AI policy, named accountability, and a governance structure that can back the commitments they make in the contract.
leadership.example: AI: "Four leadership maturity questions for the supplier: (1) Do they have a published AI policy? Ask for it — a policy that exists only for sales conversations is not evidence of governance. (2) Is there a named executive accountable for AI risk? Not a 'responsible AI team' — a named individual with a title and reporting line. (3) Have they completed any AI management system certification (ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF alignment)? Third-party evidence is more reliable than self-attestation. (4) What is their incident notification track record — have they disclosed AI incidents to clients previously? Ask references."
leadership.antipattern: Accepting a supplier's AI ethics statement as evidence of governance maturity without asking for the four concrete evidence items above — assessing values rather than systems.

planning.description: The organization documents the specific AI risks the supplier introduces into the AIMS risk register and plans treatment actions — including contractual mitigations, technical controls, and monitoring requirements.
planning.example: Human: "The supplier's AI platform processes our customer data. What risks do they introduce to our AIMS?" AI: "Four supplier-introduced risks to add to the AIMS risk register: (1) Data sovereignty — where is customer data processed? If outside your jurisdiction, transfer mechanisms must be in the DPA. Risk: High. (2) Model change without notice — if the supplier updates their AI model, it could change outputs affecting your products. Require 30-day advance notice of material model changes. Risk: Medium. (3) Supplier AI incident — if the supplier has an AI incident, how quickly are you notified and what are your obligations? Require 48-hour notification SLA. Risk: High. (4) Concentration risk — are you dependent on a single AI platform provider? Risk: Medium. Document treatment for each."
planning.antipattern: Adding a supplier to the AIMS without updating the risk register — treating supplier onboarding as a procurement task rather than an AIMS risk management event.

operation.description: The organization defines the operational controls that govern the supplier relationship throughout the contract lifecycle — audit rights, documentation requirements, change management, and incident handling.
operation.example: AI: "Operational controls for the supplier agreement: (1) AI documentation requirement — supplier must provide a model card or technical specification for each AI component within the scope of your AIMS. (2) Audit right — annual audit right with 30-day notice; supplier must make AI risk documentation available. (3) Change management — material AI model changes trigger a 30-day notice period and a change impact assessment before your organization accepts the change. (4) Incident integration — supplier AI incidents are integrated into your AIMS incident log within 24 hours of supplier notification. All four must be in the contract before signature."
operation.antipattern: Signing a supplier contract with standard SaaS terms that make no provision for AI-specific documentation, audit rights, or AI incident notification — leaving the AIMS with no operational visibility into supplier AI behavior.

performance.description: The organization monitors supplier AI performance against agreed metrics throughout the contract, conducts periodic assessments, and includes supplier performance in the annual AIMS management review.
performance.example: AI closes the assessment cycle: "Supplier performance monitoring plan: (1) Quarterly review — track: AI incident count, model change notifications received, documentation currency (is the model card up to date?). (2) Annual assessment — re-run the supplier risk assessment; has the supplier's governance maturity improved or degraded? (3) AIMS management review input — include supplier performance in the annual management review as a standing agenda item. (4) Corrective action trigger — if the supplier fails two consecutive quarterly reviews, issue a formal corrective action request with a 60-day remediation deadline. Failure to remediate triggers contract review."
performance.antipattern: Completing the initial supplier assessment at onboarding and conducting no further performance monitoring — treating supplier AI governance as a one-time due diligence event rather than an ongoing AIMS obligation.

collaboration.description: Supplier role in the AIMS is classified → supplier governance maturity is assessed against four evidence criteria → supplier-introduced risks are added to the AIMS risk register with treatments → operational controls are embedded in the contract → ongoing performance monitoring is defined for the contract lifecycle.

sequence.label: Classify supplier role within the AIMS

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're onboarding a new AI platform vendor. How do we integrate them into our AIMS?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Start by classifying the supplier's role: (1) Are they developing the AI model — the algorithm itself? (2) Are they providing AI infrastructure — compute, APIs, data pipelines — without model development? (3) Are they delivering an AI-enabled service where AI is embedded in what they provide? The classification determines the assessment depth and contract requirements. A model developer carries the most AIMS risk; an infrastructure provider carries less but is often overlooked. What does this supplier do?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: They provide an AI-powered customer analytics platform — role 3, AI-enabled service.

sequence.triggers_next: Supplier classified as AI-enabled service provider; assessment scope defined.

sequence.label: Assess supplier AI governance maturity

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What evidence should we request to assess their AI governance?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four evidence requests: (1) Published AI policy — ask for their public or shareable AI policy document. A policy that exists only for sales is not governance. (2) Named AI accountability — who is the named executive accountable for AI risk? Request name, title, and reporting line. (3) Third-party certification or alignment — have they completed ISO 42001 certification, NIST AI RMF alignment, or an equivalent independent assessment? Self-attestation is not sufficient. (4) Incident disclosure track record — have they disclosed AI incidents to clients? Ask two reference customers. Score governance maturity as Mature, Developing, or Absent based on evidence received.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Supplier has a published AI policy and a named Head of AI Ethics. No third-party certification. References report one disclosed incident handled well. Score: Developing.

sequence.triggers_next: Supplier governance maturity scored as Developing; gaps documented.

loop_back.condition: Governance assessment reveals the supplier also develops the underlying AI model — role reclassification required.
loop_back.reason: AI model developers require a more rigorous assessment scope than AI-enabled service providers.

sequence.label: Add supplier-introduced risks to the AIMS risk register

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What AIMS risks does this supplier introduce?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four risks for the register: (1) Data residency — customer analytics data processed outside our jurisdiction. Likelihood: High (US-based supplier). Impact: High (GDPR exposure). Treatment: Mitigate via Standard Contractual Clauses in DPA. Owner: Legal. (2) Model opacity — proprietary analytics model with no published model card. Likelihood: High. Impact: Medium (limits our ability to explain outputs to customers). Treatment: Require model card as contract condition. Owner: AIMS manager. (3) Supplier AI incident — 48-hour notification SLA not currently in standard contract terms. Likelihood: Low. Impact: High. Treatment: Contractual SLA. Owner: Legal. (4) Concentration risk — no alternative analytics provider evaluated. Risk: Medium. Treatment: Evaluate one alternative provider annually.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All four risks added to the AIMS risk register with owners and target dates.

sequence.triggers_next: Supplier risks registered with treatments and owners.

sequence.label: Define operational controls and embed in the contract

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What contract clauses do we need for AI-specific operational control?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four contract clauses beyond standard SaaS terms: (1) Model documentation — supplier provides a model card or technical specification for the analytics AI within 30 days of contract signature and updates it within 30 days of any material model change. (2) Change notification — 30-day advance notice of material AI model changes; buyer may request a change impact assessment period before accepting. (3) Audit right — annual audit right; supplier provides AI risk documentation on request with 30-day notice. (4) AI incident notification — supplier notifies buyer within 48 hours of any AI incident affecting buyer's data or analytics outputs. All four to be included in the main agreement or an AI schedule appended to the contract.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All four clauses accepted by supplier. Contract signed with AI schedule attached.

sequence.triggers_next: Operational controls embedded in contract; supplier onboarded into AIMS.

sequence.label: Define ongoing monitoring and AIMS management review input

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we monitor this supplier's AI performance throughout the contract?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Quarterly supplier review cadence: (1) Metrics to track: AI incident count and resolution time, model change notifications received vs. expected, model card currency (last update date). (2) Annual re-assessment: re-run the governance maturity assessment; has the supplier moved from Developing to Mature? (3) AIMS management review: include a supplier AI performance summary as a standing agenda item; flag any corrective actions open. (4) Corrective action trigger: two consecutive quarterly reviews with performance below agreed thresholds triggers a formal corrective action request. Non-resolution within 60 days triggers contract review. Add the first quarterly review date to the AIMS calendar.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: First quarterly review scheduled. Supplier added to AIMS management review agenda.

sequence.triggers_next: Supplier performance monitoring operational; integrated into AIMS calendar.

transitions.trigger: Supplier role classified and assessment scope defined.
Governance assessment reveals role reclassification needed.
Supplier governance maturity scored and gaps documented.
Supplier risks added to AIMS risk register.
Operational controls embedded in contract.
Annual re-assessment cycle — re-classify and re-assess.

inclusive-growth.description: The report discloses which populations benefit from the organization's AI systems, which bear disproportionate risk, and what the organization is doing to close identified equity gaps — backed by data, not assertions.
inclusive-growth.example: Reporting lead: "Our transparency report says we are 'committed to inclusive AI.' How do we make that substantive?" AI: "Replace the commitment statement with three data points: (1) For each major AI system, who uses it and what demographic groups benefit? (2) Where disparity audits found gaps, what was the disparity ratio and what action was taken? (3) What is the organization's equity improvement target for next year, and how will it be measured? A transparency report that makes no measurable commitments is a public relations document, not an accountability one."
inclusive-growth.antipattern: Publishing general statements about 'commitment to inclusive AI' without any data on who benefits, who is disadvantaged, and what concrete actions are being taken to close identified gaps.

human-centred.description: The report identifies which human rights are most implicated by the organization's AI systems and discloses how those rights are protected — including the results of any rights impact assessments conducted.
human-centred.example: AI: "For the human-centred section, structure it as: (1) Rights at stake — list the top three human rights implicated by your AI systems (e.g., right to non-discrimination for hiring AI, right to privacy for data AI, right to explanation for credit AI). (2) Protection measures — for each right, what mechanism protects it? (3) Assessment results — were any rights impact assessments conducted? What did they find? If no assessment was conducted, say so — incomplete disclosure is still more credible than vague assurance."
human-centred.antipattern: Referencing 'human rights' in the report without naming which rights are implicated, how they are protected, or whether any assessment of rights impacts was conducted.

transparency.description: The report itself is transparent about its scope limitations — what AI systems are covered, what is excluded and why, and what the organization does not yet know about its own AI impacts.
transparency.example: Human: "Should we disclose that we have three AI systems we couldn't include because they are under NDA with the vendor?" AI: "Yes — disclosing scope limitations is more credible than implying complete coverage. State: 'This report covers eight of eleven AI systems in production. Three systems operated under third-party vendor agreements that restrict disclosure. We are working with vendors to enable disclosure in next year's report.' Readers who discover undisclosed systems will question everything in the report; readers who see honest scope limitations will trust the disclosed content more."
transparency.antipattern: Publishing a transparency report that does not disclose its own scope limitations — implying complete coverage when significant AI systems are excluded.

robustness.description: The report discloses the organization's approach to AI system safety and security testing — including what testing was conducted, by whom, and what the results indicated — not just that testing occurred.
robustness.example: AI: "For robustness disclosure, three elements: (1) Testing scope — which systems were tested for safety and security, and which were not? (2) Methodology — was testing internal, third-party, or regulatory? Third-party testing is more credible. (3) Material findings — were any significant safety or security issues found? If so, what was the remediation? Disclosing a finding and its remediation demonstrates a mature program; concealing it creates legal and reputational risk if the issue is later discovered externally."
robustness.antipattern: Stating 'all AI systems undergo rigorous testing' without specifying what was tested, by whom, and what the results were — making the claim unverifiable and therefore meaningless.

accountability.description: The report names who is accountable for the organization's AI program, what governance structures exist, and how stakeholders can raise concerns — with a commitment to respond to feedback before the next report.
accountability.example: AI closes the reporting cycle: "Accountability section structure: (1) Named AI leadership — the executive responsible for AI governance and their reporting line. (2) Governance bodies — AI ethics committee, board oversight item, internal audit scope. (3) Incident summary — how many AI incidents occurred, how many were resolved, and what systemic changes resulted. (4) Stakeholder engagement — how did the organization gather external input for this report? (5) Contact for feedback — a named channel for stakeholders to respond to this report, with a commitment to acknowledge receipt within 30 days."
accountability.antipattern: Publishing an AI transparency report with no named executive, no governance disclosure, no incident summary, and no way for stakeholders to provide feedback — making 'accountability' a heading rather than a reality.

collaboration.description: Equity data is gathered and converted from assertions to evidence → human rights implications are named and protection measures disclosed → report scope limitations are explicitly disclosed → robustness testing results are summarized with methodology → accountability structure and feedback mechanism close the report. Loops back to inclusive-growth if rights impact assessment reveals equity gaps not captured in the initial data pull.

sequence.label: Convert equity commitments to data-backed disclosures

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We want to publish our first AI transparency report. The draft says we are 'committed to equitable AI.' How do we make this substantive?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three data requests to replace the commitment statement: (1) Pull disparity audit results for each AI system — do they exist? If not, note the gap and commit to conducting them by a named date. (2) For systems with audits, what were the disparity ratios and what actions were taken? (3) What is the measurable equity target for next year — e.g., 'reduce disparity ratio in hiring AI from 1.34 to below 1.15 by Q4'? A report with one specific target and its baseline is more credible than five pages of aspirational language.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Hiring AI disparity ratio: 1.34. Credit AI: no audit conducted. Equity target: hiring AI ratio below 1.15 by Q4.

sequence.triggers_next: Equity data gathered; targets set; audit gaps disclosed.

sequence.label: Name rights at stake and disclose protection mechanisms

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Which human rights should we reference in the report?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Map your AI systems to rights: (1) Hiring AI — right to non-discrimination (Article 21, EU Charter; Title VII equivalent in US); right to explanation for automated decisions (GDPR Article 22). (2) Credit AI — right to non-discrimination; right to human review of automated credit decisions. (3) Customer analytics AI — right to privacy and data protection. For each right, document: what mechanism protects it? Has a rights impact assessment been conducted? If no assessment exists, say so and commit to one — that honest disclosure is more credible than vague assurance.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: No formal rights impact assessment has been conducted. We'll disclose this and commit to one for hiring AI by Q2.

sequence.triggers_next: Rights at stake named; protection mechanisms disclosed; assessment gaps acknowledged.

loop_back.condition: Rights impact assessment reveals equity gaps not captured in the initial disparity data.
loop_back.reason: Equity section must be updated to reflect rights-based findings.

sequence.label: Disclose report scope and limitations explicitly

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Three of our AI systems are covered by vendor NDAs. Do we include them?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Disclose the limitation explicitly: 'This report covers eight of eleven AI systems in production. Three systems are operated under third-party vendor agreements that currently restrict public disclosure. We are engaging vendors to enable disclosure in next year's report.' This is the right approach for three reasons: (1) Stakeholders who find the undisclosed systems will question the entire report. (2) Named limitations signal a mature transparency program. (3) The vendor engagement commitment creates accountability for next year's disclosure. Add the three system categories without identifying the vendors if NDA permits.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Scope limitation section drafted with vendor engagement commitment.

sequence.triggers_next: Report scope and limitations explicitly disclosed.

sequence.label: Summarize testing methodology and material findings

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Our security team ran penetration tests on four AI systems. Should we disclose results?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Disclose at the finding level, not the vulnerability level: 'Four AI systems underwent third-party penetration testing in Q2. Two systems had findings that were remediated within 30 days of discovery. No findings remain open.' This discloses that testing occurred, that issues were found and fixed, and that nothing is outstanding — without providing a map for attackers. If a finding is still open, say so with its severity and target remediation date. Concealing open findings in a transparency report creates legal exposure if the vulnerability is later exploited.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Two findings, both remediated. Summary language drafted for robustness section.

sequence.triggers_next: Testing methodology and material findings summarized without operational security risk.

sequence.label: Name executives, governance bodies, and feedback channel

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we close the report with genuine accountability?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Accountability section: (1) Named AI executive — 'AI governance is the responsibility of [Name], Chief Technology Officer, reporting to the CEO and the Board Governance Committee.' (2) Governance bodies — AI Ethics Committee (members listed by role, not name if preferred), meets quarterly, has pause authority for high-risk deployments. (3) Incident summary — '3 AI incidents logged in the reporting period; all resolved; 1 systemic process change resulted.' (4) Stakeholder engagement — 'This report was reviewed by an external civil society organization before publication.' (5) Feedback channel — 'Submit feedback at [email/form]; we commit to acknowledging receipt within 30 days and publishing a summary of feedback received in next year's report.'

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Accountability section complete. Report ready for executive review before publication.

sequence.triggers_next: Report complete with named accountability, incident summary, and feedback channel.

transitions.trigger: Equity data gathered and targets set.
Rights assessment reveals equity gaps not in initial data.
Rights implications named and protection mechanisms disclosed.
Scope limitations explicitly disclosed.
Testing methodology and findings summarized.
Annual reporting cycle — begin data collection for next report.

govern.description: The enterprise defines what governance requirements apply to third-party AI vendors — minimum documentation standards, audit rights, incident notification obligations, and the internal owner responsible for vendor AI risk throughout the contract lifecycle.
govern.example: Procurement lead: "Before we evaluate any vendor, we need to confirm our minimum governance requirements: What AI documentation must vendors provide? Do we have audit rights in the contract? Who internally owns this vendor relationship for AI risk? Without these baselines, every vendor assessment will be inconsistent."
govern.antipattern: Evaluating vendors on functionality and price without establishing governance baselines — signing contracts that grant no audit rights and impose no AI risk notification obligations on the vendor.

map.description: The team maps risks introduced by the vendor's AI: data handling practices, model opacity, supply chain dependencies, jurisdictional exposure, and what happens to the enterprise's risk posture if the vendor is acquired, changes its model, or has an incident.
map.example: AI: "For this vendor, I can map six risk areas: (1) Data residency — where is your data processed and stored? (2) Model transparency — can you inspect or audit the model? (3) Vendor concentration risk — how dependent would you be on this single supplier? (4) Supply chain — what third parties does the vendor rely on? (5) Regulatory exposure — does using this vendor trigger AI regulation obligations for you? (6) Continuity risk — what happens if the vendor exits this product? Which are in scope?"
map.antipattern: Mapping only the vendor's feature set and price, and ignoring the risk that the vendor's AI practices introduce into the buyer's own risk and compliance posture.

measure.description: The team scores each vendor risk area using evidence from the vendor's documentation, security questionnaire, third-party audits, and reference checks — not marketing materials or verbal assurances.
measure.example: Human: "Vendor claims their model is 'fair and unbiased.' How do we score the fairness risk?" AI: "A claim is not evidence. Ask for: (1) A third-party bias audit report, not older than 12 months. (2) Disparity metrics across the demographic groups relevant to your use case. (3) Their process for updating the model when disparity is detected. If they cannot provide these, score fairness risk as High and unknown — which is worse than a known High."
measure.antipattern: Accepting vendor self-attestation as evidence for risk scoring, especially for fairness, security, and regulatory compliance claims.

manage.description: The team converts risk assessment outcomes into contractual obligations, operational controls, and ongoing monitoring requirements — including the right to audit, incident notification windows, and exit provisions.
manage.example: AI: "Based on the risk scores, here are the contract provisions I recommend: (1) Data residency — explicitly named jurisdictions in the DPA. (2) Audit rights — annual audit right with 30-day notice. (3) Incident notification — 48-hour notification SLA for any AI incident affecting your data or outputs. (4) Model change notification — 30-day notice before any material model update. (5) Exit provision — data portability and 90-day transition support."
manage.antipattern: Completing the vendor risk assessment and then handing off to legal with no specific contract requirements derived from the risk findings — allowing standard contract terms to override the assessed risks.

collaboration.description: Enterprise governance baselines are set before any vendor contact → vendor-introduced risks are mapped systematically → risks are scored using evidence, not self-attestation → risk findings are converted into contract provisions and ongoing monitoring requirements. Loops back to Map if due diligence reveals undisclosed vendor dependencies.

sequence.label: Set enterprise AI vendor governance baselines

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're evaluating three AI vendors for our HR screening tool. Before we start, what governance baselines do we need?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three baselines before any vendor contact: (1) Minimum documentation requirements — what must every vendor provide? At minimum: model card or equivalent, data handling practices, most recent security audit. (2) Contract non-negotiables — audit rights, incident notification SLA, data deletion on contract termination. (3) Internal ownership — who owns this vendor relationship for AI risk throughout the 3-year contract lifecycle? Name the person now, not after signature.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: HR tech risk owner is the CHRO's office. Baselines agreed: model card required, 48-hour incident SLA, annual audit right.

sequence.triggers_next: Governance baselines defined and internal owner named.

sequence.label: Map risks introduced by the vendor's AI

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Vendor A is US-based, uses a proprietary LLM, and will process EU applicant data. Map the risks.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Risk map for Vendor A: (1) Data residency — EU applicant data processed by a US company triggers GDPR Chapter V (international transfers). Is a Standard Contractual Clause or adequacy decision in place? (2) Model opacity — proprietary LLM with no published model card means fairness risk is unmeasurable without a vendor disclosure. (3) Regulatory exposure — the EU AI Act classifies AI used in employment screening as high-risk. You inherit compliance obligations as the deployer. (4) Concentration risk — single vendor for a compliance-critical process. (5) Continuity — what is their exit policy?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: EU AI Act high-risk classification changes our obligations significantly. We need to add that to the assessment scope.

sequence.triggers_next: Vendor risk landscape fully mapped including regulatory exposure.

loop_back.condition: Due diligence reveals undisclosed vendor sub-processors or supply chain dependencies.
loop_back.reason: Sub-processor risks must be mapped before scoring can be accurate.

sequence.label: Score each vendor risk using evidence

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Vendor A provided a one-page 'AI ethics statement.' Is that sufficient for our fairness risk score?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: No. For an HR AI classified as high-risk under the EU AI Act, you need: (1) A third-party bias audit against your jurisdiction's protected characteristics — not an internal statement. (2) Disparity metrics for gender, age, and ethnicity for the role types you'll screen. (3) Their re-training process when disparity is detected. A one-page statement scores as: evidence quality — Insufficient. Fairness risk — High and unknown. That is a disqualifying finding unless they provide substantive documentation within your due diligence window.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We'll issue a formal evidence request with a 10-business-day deadline.

sequence.triggers_next: All priority risks scored with evidence ratings and gaps documented.

sequence.label: Convert risk findings into contract terms and monitoring plan

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Risk assessment complete. Vendor A has high fairness risk and data residency gaps. What contract terms do we need?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Contract requirements derived from risk findings: (1) Data residency — Standard Contractual Clauses with named processing locations, no sub-processor changes without 30-day notice. (2) Fairness — annual third-party bias audit provided to buyer within 30 days of completion; material disparity findings trigger a 60-day remediation obligation. (3) EU AI Act compliance — vendor certifies conformity assessment status at contract signature and at each annual renewal. (4) Incident — 48-hour notification of any AI incident affecting buyer's data or outputs. (5) Exit — 90-day transition support, data deletion certificate within 30 days of termination. Monitoring: quarterly vendor review cadence with CHRO office.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: These terms go to legal for contract drafting.

sequence.triggers_next: Contract provisions drafted, monitoring cadence defined, vendor risk register created.

transitions.trigger: Governance baselines and internal owner confirmed.
Vendor risk landscape fully mapped.
Due diligence reveals undisclosed sub-processors or dependencies.
All priority risks scored with evidence.
Contract renewal or new vendor triggers a new assessment cycle.

context.description: The organization defines the internal and external context for the AIMS: which AI systems are in scope, who the relevant stakeholders are, what legal and regulatory obligations apply, and where the organization's current AI practices stand relative to the standard's requirements.
context.example: Project lead: "We run five AI systems in production. How do we determine which are in scope for the AIMS?" AI: "ISO 42001 scoping requires you to assess three factors: (1) Which AI systems are you responsible for as the developer or deployer? (2) What is the potential impact of each system — high-impact systems (those affecting employment, credit, healthcare) should be in scope regardless of size. (3) What are your legal obligations — does any jurisdiction require you to have an AI management system? Start with a gap assessment: map each of the five systems against these criteria."
context.antipattern: Scoping the AIMS to only the systems that are easiest to document, while excluding higher-risk systems that require more effort — producing a certified AIMS that does not cover the organization's material AI risks.

leadership.description: Top management demonstrates commitment to the AIMS by approving an AI policy, assigning roles and responsibilities, providing resources, and integrating the AIMS into the organization's existing management system structure.
leadership.example: AI: "For the ISO 42001 leadership requirements, the CEO or equivalent must: (1) Approve the AI policy in writing — not delegate approval. (2) Name an AI management representative with the authority to make AIMS decisions without seeking approval for each action. (3) Allocate a budget for AIMS implementation, maintenance, and certification. (4) Integrate AI risk into the existing enterprise risk management reporting cadence. Has leadership explicitly committed to these four?"
leadership.antipattern: Delegating AIMS implementation entirely to a technical team without top management commitment — producing an AIMS that has no authority to enforce AI policy across business units.

planning.description: The organization conducts an AI risk and opportunity assessment scoped to all in-scope AI systems, sets measurable AI objectives, and produces treatment plans for identified risks — all documented as formal inputs to the AIMS operational stage.
planning.example: Human: "We've scoped five systems and have leadership commitment. What does the risk assessment look like for ISO 42001?" AI: "ISO 42001 risk assessment has two layers: (1) AIMS-level risks — what could prevent the management system itself from working? (e.g., inadequate documentation, key-person dependency, regulatory change.) (2) AI system-level risks — what risks does each AI system pose to the organization and to affected parties? For each AI system, document: system purpose, affected parties, risk categories (fairness, safety, privacy, operational), likelihood, impact, and treatment option."
planning.antipattern: Producing an AIMS risk register that describes risks in general terms without linking each risk to a specific AI system and a specific treatment action — creating a compliance document rather than a decision tool.

operation.description: The organization implements controlled processes for AI development and deployment, including documentation requirements, change management, supplier AI requirements, and incident handling — all within the AIMS governance structure.
operation.example: AI: "For AIMS operation, I recommend implementing four process controls in the first 90 days: (1) AI system lifecycle documentation — model card or equivalent required before any AI system is deployed to production. (2) Change control — material AI system changes require a documented impact assessment before deployment. (3) Supplier AI requirements — third-party AI vendors must meet your AI policy minimums as a contract condition. (4) Incident logging — all AI incidents are logged and reviewed monthly by the AI management representative."
operation.antipattern: Implementing AIMS documentation requirements without change control — allowing AI systems to be updated post-documentation without triggering a documentation update, leaving the AIMS records diverged from reality.

performance.description: The organization monitors AIMS effectiveness through KPIs, conducts the first internal audit within the implementation timeline, holds a management review that formally assesses AIMS performance, and identifies nonconformities for corrective action.
performance.example: AI closes the implementation cycle: "For the first internal audit, audit three areas: (1) Clause conformity — are all required AIMS elements implemented? (2) Operational effectiveness — are the process controls working as designed? Pull three AI system records and verify they meet the documentation requirements. (3) Risk register currency — are the risk entries up to date? Have treatments been implemented on schedule? Audit findings feed the first management review, which must produce a formal AIMS effectiveness decision from top management."
performance.antipattern: Scheduling the first internal audit only in the week before the external certification assessment — treating it as a rehearsal rather than a genuine performance check.

collaboration.description: AIMS scope and gap assessment completed → leadership commitment and AI policy approved → risk assessment and objectives set → operational controls implemented → first internal audit conducted and management review held. Loops back to planning if operational implementation reveals unscoped risks.

sequence.label: Define AIMS scope and conduct gap assessment

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We want to implement ISO 42001. Where do we start?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Start with a context and gap assessment before any documentation work. Three outputs needed: (1) AIMS scope document — which AI systems, which organizational units, which jurisdictions are in scope. (2) Interested parties register — who has a stake in the organization's AI activities? Include regulators, customers, employees, and AI suppliers. (3) Gap assessment — for each ISO 42001 clause, what currently exists and what is missing? This gives you a prioritized implementation roadmap rather than a generic project plan.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Scope: five production AI systems, EU and US operations, financial services sector. Gap assessment: no formal AI policy, no AI risk register, no incident logging.

sequence.triggers_next: AIMS scope defined; gap assessment complete with prioritized gaps.

sequence.label: Secure top management commitment and AI policy approval

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we get the right leadership commitment for ISO 42001?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: ISO 42001 requires documented top management commitment — not just verbal support. Deliverables: (1) AI policy document approved and signed by CEO. Policy must include: organizational commitment to responsible AI, key AI values and prohibited uses, and a commitment to continual improvement of the AIMS. (2) AI management representative named with formal authority. (3) AIMS budget approved in the current fiscal cycle. (4) AI risk added to the board/executive risk reporting cadence. I can draft the AI policy for CEO review — do you want a one-page or full policy document?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Full policy document please. CEO review is next week.

sequence.triggers_next: AI policy approved; AI management representative named; budget allocated.

sequence.label: Conduct AI risk assessment and set measurable objectives

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we structure the risk assessment for all five AI systems?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Use a structured risk register with six columns per system: (1) System name and purpose. (2) Affected parties — who is impacted by this system's outputs? (3) Risk categories — fairness, privacy, safety, operational, legal. (4) Likelihood and impact score (1-5 scale). (5) Treatment option — accept, mitigate, avoid, or transfer. (6) Owner and target date. For objectives: set at least three AIMS-level objectives (e.g., 100% of new AI deployments have a model card by Q3; zero unlogged AI incidents by Q2; first internal audit completed by Q4). I'll draft the risk register template.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Risk register drafted. Three AIMS objectives approved by AI management rep.

sequence.triggers_next: Risk register complete; measurable AIMS objectives approved.

loop_back.condition: Operational implementation reveals AI systems or risk categories not captured in the initial assessment.
loop_back.reason: Planning documents must be updated before operational records can be accurate.

sequence.label: Implement operational process controls

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Which operational controls should we implement first?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Prioritize by risk: (1) Model card requirement — implement within 30 days; all five existing systems need retrospective cards within 60 days. (2) Change control — any material AI system change requires a documented impact assessment before deployment; implement in the CI/CD pipeline. (3) Incident log — create a shared log, set a monthly review meeting with the AI management rep. (4) Supplier AI requirements — add an AI policy conformance clause to all new AI vendor contracts. These four controls address the most common ISO 42001 nonconformity findings in initial certification audits.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All four controls implemented. Model cards complete for four of five systems.

sequence.triggers_next: Operational controls implemented and documented.

sequence.label: Conduct first internal audit and management review

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're approaching the first internal audit. What should it cover?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: First internal audit scope: (1) Clause conformity check — walk through all ISO 42001 clauses and verify documented evidence exists for each. (2) Operational effectiveness sample — pull three AI system records (model card, change control log, incident log) and verify they meet the documented requirements. (3) Objective progress — are the three AIMS objectives on track? (4) Risk register currency — are treatments being implemented on schedule? Audit report goes to top management for the first formal management review. The review must produce a written AIMS effectiveness conclusion — not just minutes.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Internal audit complete. Two minor nonconformities found. Management review scheduled.

sequence.triggers_next: Internal audit complete; management review held; corrective actions assigned.

transitions.trigger: AIMS scope defined and gap assessment completed.
AI policy approved and AI management representative named.
Risk register complete and AIMS objectives approved.
New AI systems or risk categories discovered during implementation.
Operational controls implemented and documented.
Annual AIMS review cycle — reassess context and scope.

risk-classification.description: The conformity assessment begins by confirming that the risk classification is accurate and current — verifying the Annex III basis, the intended purpose as actually deployed, and whether any post-classification changes trigger a new assessment.
risk-classification.example: Compliance officer: "We classified the system as high-risk six months ago. Do we need to re-confirm before the conformity assessment?" AI: "Yes — the conformity assessment must be based on the current deployed configuration, not the original classification. Three re-confirmation checks: (1) Has the system's intended purpose changed since classification? A widened scope may add Annex III categories. (2) Has the system been materially updated — new training data, new model version, new deployment geography? Material changes may require a new conformity assessment. (3) Has the regulatory environment changed? New harmonized standards or Commission guidance may affect which conformity pathway applies. Document each check with a 'confirmed' or 'change identified' status."
risk-classification.antipattern: Running the conformity assessment against the original classification document without re-confirming that the system has not changed materially in the intervening months — producing a conformity assessment that does not match the deployed system.

transparency.description: The conformity assessment audits the technical documentation package — technical file, instructions for use, logging specification, and user notification — for completeness, accuracy, and currency against the currently deployed system.
transparency.example: AI: "Technical file audit checklist for the conformity assessment: (1) Completeness — does the file contain all required sections under Article 11? Check against the annex listing. (2) Currency — is every section current with the deployed system version? Compare the file's version timestamps against the deployment changelog. (3) Performance metrics accuracy — do the metrics in the file match the most recent production evaluation results? Pull the current production metrics and compare. (4) Instructions for use — are they in plain language accessible to the deployer's operational staff? Have deployers reviewed them? (5) User notification — is the notification mechanism live in production? Test it."
transparency.antipattern: Accepting the technical file at face value without auditing it against the deployed system — certifying documentation that describes a system that no longer exists in its original form.

human-oversight.description: The conformity assessment verifies that the human oversight mechanism meets Article 14's effectiveness standard — checking design, training, override capability, and production override rate evidence.
human-oversight.example: Human: "Our conformity assessment auditor asked for evidence that human oversight is 'effective' under Article 14. What do we provide?" AI: "Four evidence items for Article 14 effectiveness: (1) Override capability — demonstrate the override function in a screen recording of the production system; show that an operator can pause or override the AI output without technical barriers. (2) Training records — provide completion records for all current operators showing they completed the oversight training program, including the module on the system's known failure modes. (3) Production override data — pull the past 90 days of override events: count, rate, and a sample of 5 documented override decisions with their documented reason. (4) Operator survey — a brief survey asking operators: do they feel they understand the system's limitations well enough to know when to override? Score of less than 70% confident is a red flag."
human-oversight.antipattern: Providing only the written oversight procedure as Article 14 evidence — demonstrating how oversight is supposed to work rather than evidence that it actually works in production.
