example_prompts.text: Full policy document please. CEO review is next week.

sequence.triggers_next: AI policy approved; AI management representative named; budget allocated.

sequence.label: Conduct AI risk assessment and set measurable objectives

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we structure the risk assessment for all five AI systems?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Use a structured risk register with six columns per system: (1) System name and purpose. (2) Affected parties — who is impacted by this system's outputs? (3) Risk categories — fairness, privacy, safety, operational, legal. (4) Likelihood and impact score (1-5 scale). (5) Treatment option — accept, mitigate, avoid, or transfer. (6) Owner and target date. For objectives: set at least three AIMS-level objectives (e.g., 100% of new AI deployments have a model card by Q3; zero unlogged AI incidents by Q2; first internal audit completed by Q4). I'll draft the risk register template.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Risk register drafted. Three AIMS objectives approved by AI management rep.

sequence.triggers_next: Risk register complete; measurable AIMS objectives approved.

loop_back.condition: Operational implementation reveals AI systems or risk categories not captured in the initial assessment.
loop_back.reason: Planning documents must be updated before operational records can be accurate.

sequence.label: Implement operational process controls

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Which operational controls should we implement first?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Prioritize by risk: (1) Model card requirement — implement within 30 days; all five existing systems need retrospective cards within 60 days. (2) Change control — any material AI system change requires a documented impact assessment before deployment; implement in the CI/CD pipeline. (3) Incident log — create a shared log, set a monthly review meeting with the AI management rep. (4) Supplier AI requirements — add an AI policy conformance clause to all new AI vendor contracts. These four controls address the most common ISO 42001 nonconformity findings in initial certification audits.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All four controls implemented. Model cards complete for four of five systems.

sequence.triggers_next: Operational controls implemented and documented.

sequence.label: Conduct first internal audit and management review

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're approaching the first internal audit. What should it cover?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: First internal audit scope: (1) Clause conformity check — walk through all ISO 42001 clauses and verify documented evidence exists for each. (2) Operational effectiveness sample — pull three AI system records (model card, change control log, incident log) and verify they meet the documented requirements. (3) Objective progress — are the three AIMS objectives on track? (4) Risk register currency — are treatments being implemented on schedule? Audit report goes to top management for the first formal management review. The review must produce a written AIMS effectiveness conclusion — not just minutes.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Internal audit complete. Two minor nonconformities found. Management review scheduled.

sequence.triggers_next: Internal audit complete; management review held; corrective actions assigned.

transitions.trigger: AIMS scope defined and gap assessment completed.
AI policy approved and AI management representative named.
Risk register complete and AIMS objectives approved.
New AI systems or risk categories discovered during implementation.
Operational controls implemented and documented.
Annual AIMS review cycle — reassess context and scope.

risk-classification.description: The conformity assessment begins by confirming that the risk classification is accurate and current — verifying the Annex III basis, the intended purpose as actually deployed, and whether any post-classification changes trigger a new assessment.
risk-classification.example: Compliance officer: "We classified the system as high-risk six months ago. Do we need to re-confirm before the conformity assessment?" AI: "Yes — the conformity assessment must be based on the current deployed configuration, not the original classification. Three re-confirmation checks: (1) Has the system's intended purpose changed since classification? A widened scope may add Annex III categories. (2) Has the system been materially updated — new training data, new model version, new deployment geography? Material changes may require a new conformity assessment. (3) Has the regulatory environment changed? New harmonized standards or Commission guidance may affect which conformity pathway applies. Document each check with a 'confirmed' or 'change identified' status."
risk-classification.antipattern: Running the conformity assessment against the original classification document without re-confirming that the system has not changed materially in the intervening months — producing a conformity assessment that does not match the deployed system.

transparency.description: The conformity assessment audits the technical documentation package — technical file, instructions for use, logging specification, and user notification — for completeness, accuracy, and currency against the currently deployed system.
transparency.example: AI: "Technical file audit checklist for the conformity assessment: (1) Completeness — does the file contain all required sections under Article 11? Check against the annex listing. (2) Currency — is every section current with the deployed system version? Compare the file's version timestamps against the deployment changelog. (3) Performance metrics accuracy — do the metrics in the file match the most recent production evaluation results? Pull the current production metrics and compare. (4) Instructions for use — are they in plain language accessible to the deployer's operational staff? Have deployers reviewed them? (5) User notification — is the notification mechanism live in production? Test it."
transparency.antipattern: Accepting the technical file at face value without auditing it against the deployed system — certifying documentation that describes a system that no longer exists in its original form.

human-oversight.description: The conformity assessment verifies that the human oversight mechanism meets Article 14's effectiveness standard — checking design, training, override capability, and production override rate evidence.
human-oversight.example: Human: "Our conformity assessment auditor asked for evidence that human oversight is 'effective' under Article 14. What do we provide?" AI: "Four evidence items for Article 14 effectiveness: (1) Override capability — demonstrate the override function in a screen recording of the production system; show that an operator can pause or override the AI output without technical barriers. (2) Training records — provide completion records for all current operators showing they completed the oversight training program, including the module on the system's known failure modes. (3) Production override data — pull the past 90 days of override events: count, rate, and a sample of 5 documented override decisions with their documented reason. (4) Operator survey — a brief survey asking operators: do they feel they understand the system's limitations well enough to know when to override? Score of less than 70% confident is a red flag."
human-oversight.antipattern: Providing only the written oversight procedure as Article 14 evidence — demonstrating how oversight is supposed to work rather than evidence that it actually works in production.

robustness.description: The conformity assessment verifies that the robustness evidence meets the technical standards applicable to the system — checking whether harmonized standards were applied, what the current production performance metrics show, and whether cybersecurity is current.
robustness.example: AI: "Robustness conformity check: (1) Harmonized standards — which harmonized European standard or common specification was applied? If no harmonized standard exists for the specific domain, document the alternative technical solution and its equivalence justification. (2) Performance currency — pull current production metrics, not just pre-deployment test results. High-risk systems must demonstrate continued accuracy in production, not just at launch. (3) Cybersecurity currency — is the penetration test result still valid (less than 12 months)? If not, commission a new test before the conformity assessment is closed. (4) Fairness metrics — are subgroup performance metrics still within the acceptable range in production? Model drift can widen disparity gaps post-deployment."
robustness.antipattern: Submitting pre-deployment robustness evidence for a conformity assessment of a system that has been in production for months — missing the possibility that model drift or production conditions have degraded performance below conformity thresholds.

governance.description: The conformity assessment produces the formal outputs required for lawful market deployment: the completed assessment record, the Declaration of Conformity, EU database registration confirmation, and the operational post-market monitoring system.
governance.example: AI closes the conformity cycle: "Conformity assessment closure: (1) Assessment record — document the assessment process: who conducted it, what was reviewed, what findings were identified and how resolved, and the final conformity conclusion. Retain for 10 years. (2) Declaration of Conformity — signed by the provider's authorized representative; includes system identification, conformity assessment basis, and applied standards. (3) EU database entry — verify the registration is current and complete; update if system details have changed since initial registration. (4) Post-market monitoring activation — confirm the monitoring system is live, the first review is scheduled, and the incident reporting escalation path has been tested end-to-end. Deployment is lawful when all four are complete."
governance.antipattern: Signing the Declaration of Conformity before all assessment findings are resolved — treating the declaration as the last step in a checklist rather than the formal attestation that all requirements are met.

collaboration.description: Classification currency is re-confirmed before assessment begins → technical documentation is audited for completeness and currency → human oversight effectiveness is verified with production evidence → robustness evidence is checked for currency and standard alignment → assessment record, Declaration of Conformity, and database registration complete the governance closure.

sequence.label: Re-confirm classification currency before assessment

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're starting the conformity assessment for our hiring AI. Where do we begin?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Before any assessment work: re-confirm the classification is still accurate. Three checks: (1) System changes — pull the change log since the original classification. Any material change (new training data, new model version, new deployment geography) requires a classification review before the assessment proceeds. (2) Scope changes — has the system been used for purposes beyond the originally classified use? If yes, additional Annex III entries may apply. (3) Regulatory updates — have any Commission delegated acts or guidance documents been issued since the original classification that affect the Annex III interpretation for employment AI? Document each check with a status.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Classification confirmed current. One model update in the past 6 months — assessed as non-material. No scope changes. EU AI Office issued guidance on employment AI — reviewed and classification unaffected.

sequence.triggers_next: Classification currency confirmed; non-material change documented.

sequence.label: Audit technical documentation for completeness and currency

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We have a technical file from the initial deployment. What do we audit?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Technical file audit — four checks: (1) Section completeness — walk through Article 11 Annex IV: does each required section exist? Flag missing sections as major findings. (2) Version currency — for each section, compare the document version date to the deployment changelog. Any section not updated since the last material change is a currency finding. (3) Performance metrics — pull current production evaluation results and compare to the metrics in the technical file. If production metrics have degraded more than 5% from the file's figures, update required. (4) User notification — test the notification mechanism in production: do applicants receive notification that AI is used in screening? Provide a test log as evidence.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Section completeness: all sections present. Currency issue: performance metrics section last updated 8 months ago. Notification: live and tested.

sequence.triggers_next: Technical file audit complete — performance metrics section requires update.

sequence.label: Verify oversight effectiveness with production evidence

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The auditor wants Article 14 effectiveness evidence. What do we produce?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four evidence items: (1) Override function demonstration — screen recording of an operator using the override in the production system (with test data). Duration: under 3 minutes. Shows: how the override is accessed, what information the operator sees, and what happens after override. (2) Training completion records — list of all current operators with training completion date and assessment score. Flag any operator with completion more than 18 months ago — refresher training may be required. (3) Override rate data — past 90 days: total AI recommendations, total overrides, override rate. Provide a table broken down by operator and week. (4) Operator survey results — what percentage of operators report confidence in knowing when to override? If below 70%, this is an Article 14 finding.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Override rate: 11%. Operator confidence: 82%. Training records current for all 12 operators.

sequence.triggers_next: Human oversight effectiveness evidenced — all four items compiled.

loop_back.condition: Oversight evidence reveals the system is being used in additional deployment contexts not covered by the current classification.
loop_back.reason: New deployment contexts require classification re-review before the assessment can be completed.

sequence.label: Verify robustness evidence currency and standard alignment

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Our pre-deployment bias audit is 14 months old. Is it still valid for the conformity assessment?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: A 14-month-old bias audit has two issues: (1) Currency — 12 months is the practical freshness threshold for employment AI bias audits; 14 months is borderline. Check if there have been model updates or population shifts in the past 14 months that could have changed the disparity profile. (2) Standard alignment — does the audit methodology align with any harmonized standard or Commission guidance issued in the past 14 months? If new guidance specifies a different disparity metric, the old audit may not satisfy it. Recommendation: commission a refresh audit scoped to the current production model version; it will be faster than the original since the methodology is established. The 14-month gap is a manageable finding, not a blocker, if you can show it was reviewed and no material change is expected.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Refresh bias audit commissioned. Cybersecurity penetration test: 9 months old — still valid.

sequence.triggers_next: Robustness evidence plan in place; refresh audit underway.

sequence.label: Complete assessment record and conformity declaration

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All findings are resolved. How do we close the conformity assessment?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four closure actions: (1) Assessment record — document: assessment scope, methodology, all findings identified, evidence reviewed for each finding, resolution status. The record must be signed by the assessment lead and retained for 10 years. (2) Open findings — confirm all findings from steps 2-4 are resolved: technical file performance metrics updated, refresh bias audit complete and results acceptable, operator training refresh scheduled. No Declaration of Conformity until all major findings are closed. (3) Declaration of Conformity — signed by the provider's authorized representative. Contains: system name and version, Annex III basis, conformity assessment route, applied standards, assessment date, and authorized representative name and address. (4) EU database update — if any system details changed since initial registration (version, deployment scope), update the database entry. Confirm registration is current.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All findings closed. Declaration signed. EU database updated. Post-market monitoring active.

sequence.triggers_next: Conformity assessment complete; deployment lawful.

transitions.trigger: Classification currency confirmed.
Technical documentation audit complete.
New deployment contexts discovered — classification re-review required.
Oversight effectiveness evidenced.
Robustness evidence plan in place.
Material system change triggers new conformity assessment cycle.

strategy.description: Before engineering begins, the deployment is evaluated against the organization's AI strategy — confirming it serves the mission, is consistent with stated values, and would pass the organization's own purpose test.
strategy.example: Product manager: "We want to add an AI feature that recommends premium subscription upgrades to users based on their engagement patterns. Does this pass the purpose test?" AI: "Apply the purpose test: (1) Who is the primary beneficiary — the user getting a relevant recommendation, or the organization increasing revenue? If the recommendation is optimized for conversion rather than user value, the primary beneficiary is the organization. (2) Is this consistent with your stated AI values? Pull your AI policy and check. (3) If this feature were reported in a consumer protection context tomorrow, would it be defensible? A recommendation engine that targets high-engagement users for upselling — particularly if those users are in vulnerable demographics — may not be. Flag for ethics review."
strategy.antipattern: Beginning engineering work on an AI feature before evaluating whether it serves the organization's stated purpose — discovering mission alignment issues when the feature is already built and deployment pressure is high.

governance.description: The deployment passes through the organization's ethics review gate — with a completed intake form, a board decision, and any conditions documented in the deployment plan before a single line of production code is written.
governance.example: AI: "Governance checklist for this deployment: (1) Ethics intake form completed — does the feature trigger mandatory review criteria? If it targets behavior patterns linked to engagement (potentially addictive behavior), it may trigger the 'individual impact in a sensitive context' criterion. (2) Ethics board review — if triggered, submit the intake form before engineering sprint planning. (3) Conditions documented — if the board approves with conditions (e.g., exclude users below a usage frequency threshold), those conditions are written into the product spec as non-negotiable requirements, not post-launch enhancements."
governance.antipattern: Submitting to ethics review after the feature is built — when conditions imposed by the board require architectural changes that are expensive to implement, creating pressure to water down or ignore the conditions.

risk-ethics.description: The product team conducts a focused ethical risk assessment before the ethics board review — identifying the three most significant ethical risks and proposed mitigations, so the board can focus on evaluation rather than discovery.
risk-ethics.example: Human: "The ethics intake form asks for our three biggest ethical risks. What are they for a subscription upsell AI?" AI: "Three material ethical risks: (1) Targeting vulnerability — if high engagement correlates with addictive behavior patterns, targeting these users for upselling raises a harm question. Mitigation: exclude users flagged by the safety or wellness team. (2) Dark patterns — if the recommendation is displayed in a way that makes it hard to dismiss, it may cross into manipulative design. Mitigation: design review with UX ethics checklist. (3) Data use beyond original consent — were users told their engagement data would be used for upsell targeting? Mitigation: check consent terms; add explicit disclosure if not covered."
risk-ethics.antipattern: Listing only compliance risks (GDPR, data minimization) in the ethics assessment while omitting behavioral and mission alignment risks — producing a legally safe product that is ethically questionable.

implementation.description: Ethics board conditions are implemented as engineering requirements — not as optional enhancements — and the deployment includes responsible AI operational controls: fairness checks, bias testing, and a documented override pathway for the ethics conditions.
implementation.example: AI: "Ethics conditions to engineering requirements: (1) Exclusion list — 'Exclude users in the safety flag category from recommendation targeting' becomes: a safety_flag boolean check in the recommendation service, tested in CI, and verified in the deployment QA checklist. (2) Disclosure — 'Add explicit disclosure that engagement data is used for recommendations' becomes: a UI string approved by legal and product, required on the recommendation card before launch. (3) Override monitoring — set up a dashboard tracking exclusion list coverage and disclosure click-through rate. Any deviation from expected metrics triggers a responsible AI incident."
implementation.antipattern: Documenting ethics board conditions in a decision log but not converting them into engineering tickets with acceptance criteria — allowing conditions to be 'completed' without verification that they were actually implemented.

accountability.description: Ninety days post-launch, the product team reports back to the ethics board with outcome data — verifying that conditions were met, that no unexpected ethical harms emerged, and that the feature is performing consistently with the responsible AI commitments made before deployment.
accountability.example: AI closes the cycle: "90-day accountability report structure: (1) Condition verification — for each ethics board condition, provide evidence of implementation: exclusion list coverage rate, disclosure display rate. (2) Outcome metrics — what happened? Conversion rate, exclusion rate, and any complaints or escalations related to the feature. (3) Unexpected harms — were any ethical issues identified post-launch that were not anticipated in the risk assessment? If yes, what action was taken? (4) Forward plan — is the feature performing within the ethical parameters approved? Any changes planned that would require a new ethics review? The 90-day report is the accountability mechanism that closes the loop — without it, deployment is a one-way gate."
accountability.antipattern: Treating the ethics board approval as permanent and never reporting back — allowing a feature to evolve post-launch in ways that move it outside the ethical parameters it was approved under, with no governance visibility.

collaboration.description: Deployment passes purpose test before engineering begins → ethics intake form and board review completed before sprint planning → product team conducts focused ethical risk assessment as board input → ethics conditions are converted to engineering requirements → 90-day post-launch accountability report closes the loop. Loops back to governance if risk assessment reveals risks requiring board guidance before conditions can be specified.

sequence.label: Apply purpose test before engineering begins

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're planning an AI feature for the next sprint. What's the responsible AI process?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Before any sprint planning: apply the purpose test to the feature. Three questions: (1) Does this feature primarily serve the user or primarily serve the organization's financial metrics? If the answer is unclear or mixed, that is a flag. (2) Is this feature consistent with the organization's AI policy commitments — specifically the values and prohibited use cases listed? (3) If a journalist covered this feature in the context of 'AI companies manipulating users,' would we be comfortable with how it is described? If the answer to any of these is 'no' or 'uncertain,' the feature requires ethics board review before sprint planning. Document the purpose test result in the feature spec.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Purpose test result: primary beneficiary is mixed — some user value, some upsell optimization. Flagged for ethics review before sprint.

sequence.triggers_next: Purpose test completed; feature flagged for ethics review.

sequence.label: Complete ethics intake and obtain board decision

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We need to submit to the ethics board. What does the intake form need?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: One-page intake form: (1) Feature description — what does the AI do, what data does it use, who are the users? (2) Mandatory review trigger — which criterion triggered review? (3) Three ethical risks — the product team's own assessment of the top three risks. (4) Proposed mitigations — what has the product team already designed to address each risk? (5) Timeline — when is the feature planned for launch? The board needs 72 hours minimum for async review; 5 business days for a full session. Submit now — before sprint planning, not after the sprint is 80% complete.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Intake form submitted. Board async review requested. 72-hour window started.

sequence.triggers_next: Ethics intake submitted; board review in progress.

loop_back.condition: Board requests additional risk analysis before issuing a decision.
loop_back.reason: Board guidance on specific risk dimensions must inform the risk assessment before conditions can be set.

sequence.label: Conduct focused ethical risk assessment as board input

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The board wants a deeper risk analysis on the targeting and data consent risks. What do we provide?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: For targeting risk: (1) Pull engagement distribution data — what percentage of targeted users are in the top 10% of engagement? Is that distribution correlated with any demographic variables? (2) Define 'high engagement' operationally — is it time spent, frequency, or a behavioral pattern? If time spent, is there a threshold above which targeting becomes potentially exploitative? (3) For data consent: pull the current consent terms — what did users consent to when they signed up? Provide the exact text to the board. If engagement data for targeting is not covered, identify when the next consent refresh is scheduled. The board needs evidence, not narrative.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Engagement data: top 10% skews younger, 18-24 demographic. Consent terms: engagement data for targeting not explicitly covered. Both findings submitted to board.

sequence.triggers_next: Risk analysis with evidence submitted to board.

sequence.label: Convert ethics conditions to engineering requirements

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The board approved with three conditions. How do we make sure they are implemented?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Convert each condition to an engineering ticket with an acceptance criterion: (1) Condition: 'Exclude users under 21 from targeting.' Ticket: Add age_gate check (age < 21 → exclude from recommendation cohort). Acceptance: unit test passes; QA verifies exclusion in staging with test accounts. (2) Condition: 'Update consent terms before launch.' Ticket: Legal drafts consent update; product adds consent re-affirmation flow for existing users. Acceptance: legal sign-off and consent re-affirmation completion rate >80% before feature activates. (3) Condition: 'Add responsible AI monitoring dashboard.' Ticket: Dashboard showing exclusion coverage rate and consent completion rate. Acceptance: dashboard live in production with alert thresholds set. No deployment gate closes until all three acceptance criteria are verified.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Three engineering tickets created with acceptance criteria. Added to sprint as blocking requirements.

sequence.triggers_next: Ethics conditions converted to blocking engineering requirements.

sequence.label: Submit 90-day post-launch accountability report

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: It's been 90 days since launch. What does the ethics board report need?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: 90-day report: (1) Condition verification — exclusion rate: X% of users excluded via age gate (expected >5% for 18-24 skewed user base). Consent re-affirmation rate: 87% (above 80% threshold). Monitoring dashboard: live with zero alert triggers. (2) Outcome metrics — conversion rate among targeted users: +12%. No complaints or escalations related to the feature in support tickets or social media. (3) Unexpected issues — the exclusion logic had a bug for 3 days post-launch; 340 users under 21 were incorrectly included. Bug fixed within 72 hours; incident logged. (4) Forward plan — no planned changes that would require new ethics review. Feature performing within approved parameters. This report is submitted to the board chair and logged in the decision record.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Report submitted. Board acknowledges and closes the review cycle.

sequence.triggers_next: 90-day report submitted; ethics review cycle closed.

transitions.trigger: Purpose test flags feature for ethics review.
Board requests additional risk analysis.
Board approves with conditions.
Risk analysis with evidence submitted to board.
All ethics conditions implemented and verified.
Feature changes or new AI deployment trigger new cycle.

human-dignity.description: The institution ensures that AI-assisted heritage digitization and description respects the cultural dignity of source communities — including their right to determine how their heritage is represented, who has access to it, and whether certain sacred or sensitive materials should be excluded from AI processing.
human-dignity.example: Project lead: "We are digitizing 40,000 ethnographic photographs using AI tagging. What dignity issues should we address?" AI: "Four dignity considerations before deploying AI on ethnographic materials: (1) Source community consent — have the communities whose heritage is depicted been consulted about AI processing of these images? Consent for original archiving does not extend to AI analysis. (2) Sacred or restricted materials — some communities restrict who may view certain ceremonial objects or images. AI tagging and search could make restricted materials publicly discoverable. (3) Representation — AI object recognition systems are known to mislabel and stereotype non-Western cultural objects. Human expert review by community members is required. (4) Repatriation alignment — if communities have outstanding repatriation claims, AI accessibility projects must not undermine those claims by creating commercial derivatives."
human-dignity.antipattern: Using AI to automate description and public access for ethnographic collections without consulting source communities — treating cultural objects as data assets rather than living heritage with community rights.

environment.description: The project chooses AI approaches that minimize computational waste — preferring efficient, task-specific models over large general-purpose ones for digitization and cataloging tasks that do not require general intelligence.
environment.example: AI: "For cultural heritage digitization, three efficient AI choices: (1) For optical character recognition of manuscripts — use a domain-specific OCR model trained on historical scripts rather than a large language model; the task is well-defined and the efficiency difference is significant. (2) For image tagging — a fine-tuned vision model on your specific collection domain uses a fraction of the compute of a general-purpose multimodal model. (3) For metadata generation — use AI to draft, with mandatory human expert review, rather than full automation; this reduces both compute and error costs."
environment.antipattern: Deploying large general-purpose AI models for well-defined cataloging tasks (OCR, classification, tagging) where specialized efficient models would produce equivalent or better results at a fraction of the environmental cost.

privacy.description: The project protects the privacy of living individuals who may appear in heritage materials — particularly photographs — and respects community data sovereignty over materials that belong culturally to specific groups.
privacy.example: Human: "Our collection includes photographs of living community members from the 1960s–1990s. Can we use AI facial recognition for cataloging?" AI: "No — facial recognition on living individuals without consent is a serious privacy violation under GDPR and most data protection laws, regardless of the cultural heritage context. For the photography collection: (1) Do not use facial recognition for any image that could contain living individuals. (2) For deceased individuals, check jurisdiction-specific rules — some protect images of recently deceased persons. (3) For community data sovereignty — consult with the communities whether they consider community-wide images to be personal community data requiring collective consent, not just individual consent."
privacy.antipattern: Using AI facial recognition on heritage photography collections on the assumption that historical context removes privacy obligations — missing both legal requirements and the deeper community sovereignty dimension.

transparency.description: The institution is transparent with the public, researchers, and source communities about what AI was used, what it produced, how it was validated, and what the known error rates and limitations are for AI-generated metadata.
transparency.example: AI: "For the public-facing collection, add an AI disclosure banner: 'Descriptive metadata for this collection was generated with AI assistance and reviewed by human curators. AI-generated descriptions may contain errors; report corrections using the feedback link below.' For researchers, provide a methodology document: which AI tools were used, what the validation process was, and what the estimated error rate is for different material types. For source communities, provide a separate report in accessible language explaining what AI did and did not do to their heritage."
transparency.antipattern: Presenting AI-generated heritage metadata as authoritative curatorial description without disclosing its AI origin, known error rates, or the validation process — misleading researchers and the public about the metadata's reliability.

accountability.description: The project establishes a community advisory board with real authority over how AI-processed heritage materials are described, accessed, and used — including the right to request removal or restriction of specific materials.
accountability.example: AI closes the cycle: "Community accountability governance for the heritage AI project: (1) Community advisory board — one representative per source community with materials in scope; meets quarterly; has authority to request removal, restriction, or reclassification of any item. (2) Correction pathway — public-facing feedback tool for reporting AI metadata errors; institution commits to review within 30 days. (3) Annual community report — published in accessible language describing what AI produced, what was corrected, and what will change in the next year. (4) Commercial use prohibition — AI-processed heritage materials may not be licensed for commercial use without separate community consent."
accountability.antipattern: Creating a heritage AI project with an academic advisory board of heritage scholars but no representation from the source communities whose cultural materials are being processed — producing governance that serves institutional interests over community rights.

collaboration.description: Source community consultation determines which materials may be AI-processed and under what conditions → efficient AI tools are selected to minimize environmental footprint → privacy protections are designed for living individuals and community data sovereignty → public transparency disclosure is prepared → community advisory board with real authority is established before launch.

sequence.label: Consult source communities on consent and access restrictions

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We want to use AI to catalog 40,000 ethnographic photographs. Where do we start?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Before any AI processing: identify the source communities for each collection segment and initiate consultation. The consultation must address four questions: (1) Do community members consent to AI processing of these materials — including object recognition, automated description, and public search indexing? (2) Are any materials sacred, restricted, or ceremonially sensitive and should be excluded from AI processing? (3) Who from the community has authority to give consent — an individual elder, a cultural council, a formal governance body? (4) What are the community's conditions for participation — do they want co-authorship of descriptions, veto over public access, or a separate community-controlled access layer? This consultation cannot be replaced by a website notice or opt-out form.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Three communities identified. Two agreed to participate with restrictions. One requested all their materials be excluded from AI processing until a formal agreement is signed.

sequence.triggers_next: Community consent and access restrictions documented per collection segment.

sequence.label: Select efficient AI tools proportionate to the task

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Our vendor recommends using GPT-4V for image description across all 40,000 photographs. Is that the right choice?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: That is likely over-engineered and environmentally wasteful for this task. For heritage photograph description, compare three options: (1) A fine-tuned domain-specific vision model trained on similar cultural collections — higher accuracy for your specific material types, significantly lower compute per image. (2) GPT-4V — high general capability but expensive and energy-intensive at 40,000 images; better suited for complex or ambiguous edge cases. (3) Hybrid — use the efficient model for 90% of straightforward images, route ambiguous or complex cases to the larger model. The hybrid approach is both the most accurate and the most environmentally responsible. Estimate compute cost for all three before deciding.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Hybrid approach approved. Efficient model handles standard catalog; GPT-4V handles complex or ambiguous cases.

sequence.triggers_next: AI tooling selected with environmental efficiency rationale documented.

sequence.label: Design privacy protections for individuals and community data

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Many photographs include identifiable people, some of whom may still be living. How do we handle this?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three-tier privacy approach: (1) Living individuals — do not use facial recognition. For photographs estimated to include living individuals (roughly 1960s onward), manually review before AI processing. If AI is used for description, configure the model to describe the scene without identifying individuals. (2) Recently deceased — check applicable law; some jurisdictions protect image rights for 70 years post-death. (3) Community data sovereignty — for materials depicting community ceremonies or practices, the entire community's collective consent is required for AI processing and public access, not just the consent of individuals pictured. Document the privacy approach per collection segment and make it auditable.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Facial recognition disabled. 1960s onward photographs flagged for manual review before AI processing.

sequence.triggers_next: Privacy controls implemented per collection segment with documented rationale.

sequence.label: Prepare AI disclosure for public, researchers, and communities

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we disclose the AI's role in our catalog to different audiences?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three disclosure levels: (1) Public-facing — banner on every AI-assisted record: 'Description generated with AI assistance and reviewed by a human curator. Estimated error rate: 8% for object classification, 12% for cultural context. Report corrections below.' (2) Researcher methodology document — AI tools used, training data sources, validation methodology, error rates by material type, and human review process. Posted in the collection's methodology section. (3) Community report — plain-language summary for each source community: what AI did to their heritage materials, what restrictions are enforced, and how to request corrections or restrictions. Delivered in the community's primary language if possible.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All three disclosure documents drafted. Community reports translated into two languages.

sequence.triggers_next: Transparency documents prepared for all three audiences.

loop_back.condition: Community review of AI-generated descriptions reveals systematic misrepresentation of cultural objects.
loop_back.reason: Systematic misrepresentation is a dignity issue requiring community re-consultation before public access.

sequence.label: Establish community advisory board with real authority

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we build genuine community accountability into the project governance?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Community advisory board structure: (1) Composition — one representative per source community, appointed by the community (not by the institution). Two academic heritage experts. One digital rights specialist. Chair rotates annually. (2) Authority — the board has binding authority to: request removal or access restriction of any item; reject AI-generated descriptions for culturally sensitive materials; approve or reject any secondary use of the digitized collection. Recommendations must be implemented within 60 days. (3) Quarterly reporting — institution provides the board with: AI error reports, correction log, access statistics, any commercial use requests. (4) Public correction channel — anyone can report AI metadata errors; institution commits to 30-day review and public correction log.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Community advisory board established with binding authority charter. First meeting scheduled.

sequence.triggers_next: Community advisory board operational with binding authority.

transitions.trigger: Community consent documented and access restrictions defined.
AI tooling selected with environmental rationale.
Privacy controls implemented per collection segment.
AI descriptions show systematic cultural misrepresentation — re-consult communities.
Transparency documents prepared for all audiences.
Annual review cycle — community re-consultation on scope changes.

general.title: Professional Email Writing with AI

delegation.description: Decide whether AI should produce the full draft or only assist with structure, tone, or phrasing. High-stakes emails (termination notices, executive communications, sensitive negotiations) should remain human-led with AI as an editor at most. Routine emails (meeting requests, status updates, acknowledgment replies) are strong candidates for full AI drafting.
delegation.example: A manager uses AI to draft a meeting invitation and a project status update, then writes a performance feedback email herself because it requires nuanced personal knowledge of the employee's growth journey.
delegation.antipattern: Delegating every email including sensitive HR communications, client dispute responses, or apology letters entirely to AI without assessing the relationship stakes or the potential for misrepresentation.

description.description: Provide AI with the full context it needs to draft an effective email — recipient relationship, communication purpose, desired tone, relevant background, and any constraints on length or formality. The quality of the draft is directly proportional to the richness of the context you provide.
description.example: Prompt: 'Draft a follow-up email to Sarah Chen, our enterprise client at Meridian Corp. We met Tuesday to discuss their Q3 data migration delay. Tone: professional but warm — we have a 3-year relationship. Acknowledge the delay empathetically, confirm next steps (kick-off call Friday 2pm), and close with confidence. Keep it under 200 words.'
description.antipattern: Prompting with only: 'Write an email to my client about the project delay.' This gives AI no information about tone, relationship history, specific facts, or the desired outcome, resulting in a generic template that fails to represent the actual situation.

discernment.description: After receiving the AI draft, carefully evaluate whether the tone matches the real relationship, whether all factual claims are accurate, whether the intent is captured correctly, and whether the email could be misinterpreted. Read it as the recipient would, not as the author.
discernment.example: A marketing director reviews an AI-drafted client email and notices the phrase 'as we discussed' refers to a meeting that hasn't happened yet — a factual error the AI introduced. She corrects the timeline before sending.
discernment.antipattern: Copying the AI draft directly into the email client and hitting send without reading it through the recipient's eyes, missing tone mismatches (e.g., too formal for a casual colleague) or factual inaccuracies introduced by the model.

diligence.description: Take responsibility for the email you send regardless of how it was drafted. In professional contexts, consider whether AI-assisted drafting should be disclosed. Verify all facts, names, dates, and commitments in the email before sending. Your name on the email means you own the content.
diligence.example: A consultant discloses in her firm's engagement letter that AI tools assist with drafting client communications, and she maintains a personal checklist — verify dates, names, attachments, and factual claims — that she runs on every AI-assisted email before sending.
diligence.antipattern: Sending an AI-drafted email containing an incorrect meeting date, a misspelled client name, or a commitment you didn't actually make, because you assumed the AI had accurate information and skipped personal review.

general.summary: Decide whether AI should produce the full draft or only assist with structure, tone, or phrasing. High-stakes emails (termination notices, executive communications, sensitive negotiations) should rema

collaboration.description: Human delegates email drafting, provides recipient context and goal, evaluates AI draft for tone and accuracy, then sends — single-pass for routine communication.

sequence.label: Define AI's drafting role

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Draft a follow-up email to a prospect after a demo call. You write the body — I'll adjust the opening and sign-off.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I include next steps or leave that for you to add?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Propose next steps — I'll confirm or modify them.

sequence.triggers_next: AI's drafting scope and human adjustment responsibility agreed.

sequence.label: Provide recipient and goal context

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Recipient is the VP of Engineering at a mid-size SaaS company. They seemed interested in the security features. Goal: schedule a technical deep-dive.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I reference the specific feature they asked about?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — mention the SSO integration they asked about.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has recipient profile, goal, and specific talking point.

sequence.label: Evaluate draft for tone and accuracy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The opening is too casual — this is a VP, not a peer. Adjust tone.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Revised to a more formal but still warm register. Does this work?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes. Also change 'our best feature' to 'the SSO integration' — more specific.

sequence.triggers_next: Draft validated for tone, accuracy, and specificity.

sequence.label: Final review and send

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Re-reading in full — looks good. Sending.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Logging in CRM with AI-draft tag for tracking.

sequence.triggers_next: Email sent. Next communication requires a fresh cycle.

transitions.trigger: Drafting scope agreed.
AI delivers draft.
Draft validated.
Next email — fresh cycle.

general.title: From Idea to Action: AI as a Complementary Expert Panel

delegation.description: The human owns the idea and all decisions about direction and viability. AI is granted autonomy to roleplay expert personas and surface blind spots, but never to decide whether the idea is good or should proceed. Persona selection is negotiated explicitly at the start of each session.
delegation.example: Amara says: "I have an idea for a community food hub. I don't have a business background. Can you act as a business model strategist and ask me the ten questions an investor would ask — but explain each one in plain language before asking it?" AI responds: "Understood. I'll act as a business strategist who explains jargon before using it. You decide which questions matter for your context. Ready when you are."
delegation.antipattern: Letting AI declare the idea "viable" or "not viable" without the human having verified the assumptions the AI used to reach that judgment.

description.description: The human describes the idea in their own words — not in technical or business language — and explicitly names what they know, what they don't know, and which expertise gaps they feel most exposed by. The AI uses this to assemble the right persona(s) for the session.
description.example: "I want to create a space where local farmers sell directly to urban families. I know the neighborhood. I don't know how food regulations work, how to price things, or whether anyone has tried this and failed. I feel most lost on the legal and financial side." AI: "I'll play three roles for you: a food-sector regulatory guide (plain language, no legal advice), a pricing strategist, and a 'what-went-wrong' researcher who will surface similar initiatives that struggled and why."
description.antipattern: AI adopting a single generic "expert" persona without asking which expertise gaps the human actually feels, leading to answers that are confident but miss the real blind spots.

discernment.description: The human explicitly challenges AI outputs before accepting them. For each key claim the AI makes — market size, regulatory summary, pricing model — the human asks: "How confident are you? What would change this answer? Can you show me a counter-argument?" AI is prompted to flag its own uncertainty rather than maintain a persona of false authority.
discernment.example: AI (as pricing strategist): "A 15–20% margin on fresh produce is typical for direct-to-consumer models." Human: "Are you sure about that? Where would that figure break down?" AI: "Good push. That figure holds for established platforms with volume. A new, low-volume hub could see margins squeezed to 5–8% in the first year. I'd recommend verifying this with a local food-sector operator before using it in any plan."
discernment.antipattern: Human accepting AI's persona-delivered facts as expert knowledge without asking for confidence levels or sources — the persona voice creates an illusion of authority that plain-text answers do not.

diligence.description: Before any idea output (pitch, plan, financial model, regulatory checklist) is used in the real world, the human must route at least one AI-generated claim per domain through a real-world check: a practitioner conversation, a public database, a regulatory body. The AI closes each session by naming the specific claims that need external validation and by whom.
diligence.example: AI closes the session: "Three claims in today's session need verification before you act on them: (1) the 20% margin figure — validate with a farmer's market operator; (2) the food safety certification requirement — confirm with your local health authority; (3) the 'community hub grant' I mentioned — check availability with your municipal economic development office. These are the three things I cannot verify for you."
diligence.antipattern: Human treating the AI session output as a completed plan, moving to execution without validating the domain-specific claims that the AI surfaced under persona authority.

general.summary: The human owns the idea and all decisions about direction and viability. AI is granted autonomy to roleplay expert personas and surface blind spots, but never to decide whether the idea is good or sho

collaboration.description: Human shares raw idea and named gaps → AI assembles personas → AI probes and surfaces blind spots → Human challenges outputs → AI closes with a specific validation checklist → Human validates before acting. Loops back to Description if the human's framing shifts during the session.

sequence.label: Negotiate persona scope

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: "I have an idea but I'm out of my depth on the business and technical sides. Can you play the roles I'm missing and ask me the hard questions without overwhelming me with jargon?"

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "Yes. Tell me your idea in plain words, then tell me what you know well and what feels most unclear. I'll assemble the right roles and explain what each one will do before starting."

sequence.triggers_next: Persona roles and autonomy limits agreed

sequence.label: Human frames idea and gaps

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: "I want to build X. I know Y. I don't know Z. The part that scares me most is [financial / legal / technical / market]."

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "Understood. I'll play [role A], [role B], and [role C]. [Role A] will handle [domain]. I'll flag when I'm uncertain and ask you before making assumptions."

sequence.triggers_next: AI has enough context to begin structured probing

loop_back.condition: Idea scope shifts significantly during probing
loop_back.reason: Persona mix may need to change if the core idea changes

sequence.label: Human challenges AI persona outputs

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Are you confident about that? What would make that wrong?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "My confidence here is [low/medium/high] because [reason]. This would break down if [condition]. I'd recommend verifying [specific claim] with [specific source type] before using it."

sequence.triggers_next: Human accepts output with named uncertainties acknowledged

sequence.label: AI delivers validation checklist

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "Before you act on anything from this session, validate these three claims: [claim 1] — check with [source]; [claim 2] — confirm via [source]; [claim 3] — speak to [practitioner type]. These are the things I cannot verify for you."

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Understood. I'll take [claim 1] to [person/source] this week.

sequence.triggers_next: Human has a concrete validation action for each AI-sourced claim

transitions.trigger: Persona scope agreed and autonomy limits set
AI has enough framing to begin probing
Human has challenged outputs and acknowledged uncertainties
Idea scope shifted — persona mix needs renegotiation
New idea dimension emerges after validation

general.title: Description-Discernment Iterative Refinement Loop

delegation.description: Treat AI as a co-creator in an ongoing dialogue, not a one-shot command executor. The first AI output is a draft that begins a conversation, not an end product. Structure your workflow so that each AI output triggers a human evaluation, which generates a refined prompt, which produces a better output — repeating until the result meets your standard. This iterative posture is the fundamental AI collaboration pattern across all domains.
delegation.example: A product manager uses AI to draft a feature brief, evaluates it, identifies three gaps (missing user persona context, weak success metrics, no edge case consideration), feeds those gaps back into a refined prompt, gets a second draft, and repeats twice more until the brief meets her standard — treating each cycle as a productive step, not a failure.
delegation.antipattern: Running a single AI prompt, receiving an output that is clearly incomplete or off-target, and then either submitting that output as-is or abandoning the AI approach entirely — failing to use the iterative loop that is the actual mechanism through which AI collaboration produces quality results.

description.description: In iterative refinement, each round of prompting is an act of description — but later rounds are more powerful because they are informed by what you learned evaluating the previous output. Describe not just what you want but what was wrong with the last version and why. Specific corrective feedback is the engine of the loop.
description.example: Round 1: 'Draft a product requirements doc for a mobile notification system.' Round 2 (after evaluation): 'The previous draft lacked user permission flow details and assumed push always on. Revise to add: (1) permission request UX at onboarding, (2) notification preference center spec, (3) opt-out flows per notification type. Keep the same structure.'
description.antipattern: After receiving a poor first output, re-prompting with an equally vague request ('Make it better' or 'Try again') rather than articulating specifically what was insufficient and why — failing to use what you learned from evaluating the first output to produce a meaningfully improved next prompt.

discernment.description: After each AI output, conduct a structured evaluation before generating the next prompt. Assess accuracy against facts you know, relevance to your actual goal, completeness against your requirements, audience fit, and quality of reasoning. The discernment step is not a final judgment — it is the input to the next description. Make your evaluation explicit so you can articulate it in your refined prompt.
discernment.example: A writer receives an AI-drafted article section and explicitly scores it against four criteria: factual accuracy (3/5 — two claims need verification), tone (4/5 — mostly right, one paragraph too formal), completeness (2/5 — missing the key counterargument), audience fit (4/5 — appropriate vocabulary). This structured evaluation drives her next prompt precisely.
discernment.antipattern: Skimming AI output and forming a vague sense that 'it's not quite right' without identifying specifically what is wrong — then producing a corrective prompt that is equally vague, resulting in a marginally different output that still misses the target in the same ways.

diligence.description: Do not ship the first AI output, or even the second, without it having survived genuine evaluative scrutiny. Document your iteration path — the prompts used, the key decisions at each evaluation, and why you accepted the final output — especially for consequential work. Iteration transparency is accountability: it shows the work was human-directed, not just human-forwarded.
diligence.example: A communications team maintains a prompt log for all AI-assisted client reports, recording each prompt version and the reason for the refinement. When a client questions a report's methodology, the team can demonstrate the three-round refinement process and the human judgment applied at each evaluation step.
diligence.antipattern: Accepting and submitting the first AI output that seems 'good enough' without completing even one full evaluate-refine cycle — then, when the output contains errors or misses the mark, having no record of the process and no basis for explaining why the AI's first guess was treated as a final answer.

general.summary: Treat AI as a co-creator in an ongoing dialogue, not a one-shot command executor. The first AI output is a draft that begins a conversation, not an end product. Structure your workflow so that each AI

collaboration.description: Human and AI iterate through multiple refinement passes — each pass establishes scope, provides richer context from prior output, evaluates quality, and commits the improvement before the next round.

sequence.label: Define refinement goal for this pass

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: This draft is too verbose. In this pass, focus on cutting word count by 30% without losing key ideas.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I prioritize cutting filler phrases or condensing full paragraphs?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Both — flag any paragraph I should consider cutting entirely.

sequence.triggers_next: Refinement goal and cut strategy agreed for this pass.

sequence.label: Provide current artifact and refinement constraints

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Here is the current draft. The core argument in section 2 must not be weakened — everything else is fair game.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Is the technical terminology in section 3 intended for a specialist audience?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — keep the technical terms, simplify the surrounding prose.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has current artifact, non-negotiables, and simplification targets.

sequence.label: Evaluate refinement quality

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The cut version of paragraph 4 loses the nuance — restore the second sentence.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Restored. Overall word count is now down 28% — one more light pass would reach 30%.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Let's do a final light pass in the next iteration.

sequence.triggers_next: Refinement quality validated for this pass.

sequence.label: Commit refined version

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Saving this version as v3. The prior version is archived.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Noting what was changed and why in the revision log.

sequence.triggers_next: Version committed. Next iteration targets remaining improvements.

transitions.trigger: Refinement goal for this pass agreed.
AI delivers refined version.
Quality validated for this pass.
Next refinement pass — new goal.

general.title: From Problem to Solution: AI as a Structured Problem-Solving Partner

delegation.description: The human owns the problem definition and the final decision on which solution path to pursue. AI is granted autonomy to ask structured diagnostic questions, propose solution frameworks, and surface resource and financial considerations — but never to declare a solution "correct" or a budget "sufficient" without the human having verified the underlying assumptions. The human explicitly sets the constraint envelope (budget ceiling, timeline, available people) before AI begins structuring anything.
delegation.example: Human: "I run a small community health clinic. Our patient records are still on paper. I know this is a problem but I don't know if the solution is software, people, or both — and I have no idea what it costs or how long it takes. Can you help me structure this without assuming I have a tech team?" AI: "Understood. I'll help you structure the problem and map what a solution would require — people, money, time, and procurement. I won't assume any technical capacity on your side. You tell me what's real; I'll tell you what questions to ask next. First: what is the problem costing you right now, even roughly?"
delegation.antipattern: AI jumping directly to solution recommendations before fully understanding the human's actual constraints — proposing a cloud-based EHR system to a clinic with no IT budget and unreliable internet, because that is the "standard" answer.

description.description: The human describes the problem from lived experience — not in technical or managerial language — and explicitly names what they have tried, what failed, and what constraints are non-negotiable. AI uses this to map four axes before proposing anything: (1) financial envelope and funding sources, (2) procurement path (buy, build, hire, partner), (3) human resources available or needed, (4) realistic timeline given the constraint set. Each axis is surfaced as a question to the human, not as an AI assumption.
description.example: AI: "Before I suggest anything, I need to map four things with you. First, finances: do you have a rough budget in mind, or do we need to figure out what this could cost before you can say? Second, procurement: are you open to buying an off-the-shelf tool, hiring someone, or doing this yourself with guidance? Third, people: who in your clinic could own this — even part-time? Fourth, time: is there a deadline driving this, or is it open?" Human: "Budget: I genuinely don't know. Procurement: I'd rather buy than build. People: me and one administrator. Time: before the rainy season when patient volume doubles — four months." AI: "Good. Four months, two people, buy-not-build. That constraint set rules out most custom solutions immediately. Let me map what exists in that envelope."
description.antipattern: AI presenting a fully-formed solution plan — with costs, vendors, and timelines — before the human has confirmed the constraint set, forcing the human into a reactive "yes/no" rather than a collaborative build.

discernment.description: For every resource estimate, cost figure, vendor recommendation, or timeline the AI surfaces, the human explicitly asks: "Is this real for my context, or is this a global average?" AI is required to flag when a figure comes from a general knowledge base versus local market reality, and to name the specific assumption that could make the estimate wrong. The human is coached to distinguish between AI-structured thinking (high reliability) and AI-generated numbers (medium-to-low reliability without local validation).
discernment.example: AI: "A basic electronic health record system for a small clinic typically costs between $200 and $500 per month on a subscription model, with a one-time setup of $500 to $2,000." Human: "Is that true for Morocco?" AI: "Honest answer: that figure is from North American and European market data. In Morocco, locally-hosted solutions or regional vendors may be significantly cheaper — or have different pricing models entirely. The number I gave you is useful as a ceiling estimate, not a budget figure. Before using it, you need one conversation with a local health IT vendor or your regional Ministry of Health digital health desk. That single call will tell you more than I can."
discernment.antipattern: Human treating AI cost and timeline estimates as a budget, presenting them to funders or management without local validation — AI's global averages can be off by a factor of three or more in specific markets.

diligence.description: Before the human acts on any element of the AI-structured plan — commits budget, signs a procurement contract, hires a person, or sets a deadline with stakeholders — they must validate the four axes through real-world checks. AI closes each session with a named accountability matrix: one validation action per axis, one named person or source responsible, and a realistic date. The plan is not a plan until the matrix is filled by the human, not the AI.
diligence.example: AI closes the session: "Here is your validation matrix before this becomes a real plan: (1) Financial — get one local vendor quote to ground the cost estimate: target this week, owner: you. (2) Procurement — call your regional Ministry of Health digital health desk to ask if there is a subsidized or recommended solution for clinics your size: target within 10 days, owner: you. (3) Human resources — confirm with your administrator that they can own the data migration task: target before you sign anything, owner: you. (4) Timeline — validate the four-month window against the vendor's typical onboarding duration: ask in the vendor call. None of these take more than two hours total. Until they are done, treat everything we built today as a structured hypothesis, not a plan."
diligence.antipattern: Human sharing the AI-generated plan with stakeholders, funders, or management before completing the validation matrix — the plan has the appearance of rigor but the substance of a first draft.

general.summary: The human owns the problem definition and the final decision on which solution path to pursue. AI is granted autonomy to ask structured diagnostic questions, propose solution frameworks, and surface r

collaboration.description: Human names the problem from lived experience → AI maps constraint axes (financial, procurement, resources, timeline) through structured questions → AI surfaces solution options within the constraint set → Human challenges all estimates for local/contextual fit → AI closes with a named validation matrix. Loops back to Description if new constraints surface during discernment.

sequence.label: Name the problem and set the constraint envelope

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: "I have a problem I need to fix. I think AI can help but I don't know how. The problem is [X]. I have roughly [budget / no idea on budget]. I need it solved by [date / as soon as possible]. I have [these people / just myself]."

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "Understood. Before I suggest anything, I'll map what a solution requires across four axes: finances, procurement, people, and time. You confirm what's real; I structure from there. You stay in charge of what's acceptable. Let's start with the constraint that feels most fixed to you."

sequence.triggers_next: Problem named and at least one constraint confirmed as non-negotiable

sequence.label: Map the four axes through structured dialogue

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "Financial axis: do you have a figure in mind, or do we need to estimate what this category of solution costs before you can set a ceiling? Procurement axis: buy, build, hire, or partner — which feels most realistic given your context? Resource axis: who would own implementation — name a person, not a role? Timeline axis: is there a hard deadline, or a soft one? What happens if you miss it?"

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: "Budget: [figure or 'I don't know']. Procurement: [preference]. People: [name or 'just me']. Timeline: [date and consequence]."

sequence.triggers_next: All four axes mapped — AI has enough to propose options within the constraint set

loop_back.condition: A new constraint surfaces that changes the problem definition
loop_back.reason: Problem scope must be renegotiated before solution mapping continues

sequence.label: Human challenges every estimate for local and contextual fit

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: "Is that cost figure real for my context — my country, my sector, my scale? What assumption would make it wrong?"

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "That figure comes from [source type / general knowledge]. It would be wrong if [specific condition — local market, sector pricing, scale factor]. Before using it, verify with [specific source type]. My structured thinking is reliable; my numbers need local grounding."

sequence.triggers_next: Human understands which outputs are structural (reliable) vs numerical (needs validation)

sequence.label: AI delivers named validation matrix — one action per axis

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: "Your validation matrix: (1) Financial — [specific action], owner: you, target: [date]. (2) Procurement — [specific action], owner: [name], target: [date]. (3) Resources — [specific action], owner: [name], target: [date]. (4) Timeline — [specific action], confirm in [call/meeting]. Until this matrix is complete, treat today's output as a structured hypothesis, not a plan."

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: "I'll take action (1) to [person/source] by [date]. Action (2) I'll delegate to [name]."

sequence.triggers_next: Human has a named owner and date for each validation action

transitions.trigger: Problem named and at least one hard constraint confirmed
All four axes mapped within the constraint set
Human understands reliability boundaries of AI outputs
New constraint surfaces that changes the problem scope
Validation reveals the problem was mis-scoped — restart needed

human-dignity.description: The review assesses whether the AI system treats all patients with equal dignity — examining whether the system produces disparate outcomes for vulnerable populations, whether consent is meaningful, and whether the system's use is consistent with patient autonomy.
human-dignity.example: Ethics committee chair: "The proposed AI triage system ranks patients by 'predicted survival probability.' What dignity issues does that raise?" AI: "Three dignity concerns: (1) Algorithmic rationing — if the score is used to deprioritize patients, it must be validated on populations matching your hospital's demographic mix, not just the training dataset. Historical survival data often encodes systemic inequities. (2) Consent — do patients know their triage priority may be influenced by an AI score? Informed consent in AI-assisted triage is an emerging but real obligation. (3) Autonomy — is there a pathway for a patient or family member to request human-only triage? If not, the system overrides patient autonomy by default."
human-dignity.antipattern: Approving a clinical AI system based on aggregate accuracy metrics without assessing whether the system produces disparate outcomes for elderly, disabled, or minority patients — groups frequently under-represented in training datasets.

environment.description: The review considers the environmental footprint of the clinical AI system — energy consumption for training and inference — and whether the hospital has chosen the most resource-efficient option that meets clinical requirements.
environment.example: AI: "For a clinical AI system running continuous inference on patient data, estimate the annual compute cost: (1) How many inferences per day? (2) What is the model size — a distilled clinical model uses significantly less energy than a general-purpose large model. (3) Is the compute on-premise or cloud-based — and is the cloud provider on a renewable energy commitment? For a hospital with sustainability commitments, the choice between a 7B and 70B parameter model for the same clinical task is an environmental decision, not just a performance one."
environment.antipattern: Selecting the largest available AI model for a clinical task because it scores marginally higher on benchmarks, without assessing whether a smaller, more efficient model meets the clinical threshold — ignoring the environmental cost difference.

privacy.description: The review verifies that the AI system handles patient data according to the strictest applicable privacy standards — covering data minimization, retention, cross-border transfer restrictions, and secondary use prohibitions on clinical data.
privacy.example: Human: "The vendor's AI trains on patient data from multiple hospitals. Do our patients' data stay within our system?" AI: "Critical questions for the vendor: (1) Is patient data used to retrain or improve the model after deployment? If yes, is patient consent for this use in place? (2) Is patient data processed outside your jurisdiction — triggering cross-border transfer obligations? (3) Is the model's inference output (e.g., a risk score) itself personal data requiring protection? In most EU jurisdictions, an AI-generated health score linked to a patient is personal data under GDPR. (4) What is the data deletion protocol when the contract ends? Get all four answers in writing before contracting."
privacy.antipattern: Accepting a vendor's statement that 'patient data is secure' without obtaining written answers to data residency, secondary use, and deletion protocol questions — leaving the hospital with contractual exposure if a privacy incident occurs.

transparency.description: The review ensures that the AI system's outputs are explainable to the clinicians using it and, where appropriate, to the patients it affects — with particular attention to whether clinicians can identify when to distrust the system's recommendations.
transparency.example: AI: "Clinician transparency for a triage AI requires three things: (1) The clinician must see the key factors driving the score for each patient — not just a number. (2) The clinician must be trained on the system's known failure modes: which patient profiles produce unreliable scores? (3) There must be a documented 'when to override' protocol — specific clinical signals that should trigger human-only judgment regardless of the AI score. Without all three, clinicians cannot exercise meaningful oversight — they can only accept or reject a number."
