transparency.antipattern: Deploying a clinical AI that produces a risk score without any feature attribution — giving clinicians a number they cannot interpret or challenge, and no guidance on when the number is likely to be wrong.

accountability.description: The review establishes who is accountable for the system's clinical outcomes, how incidents are escalated, whether patients have a recourse pathway, and what governance oversight applies after deployment.
accountability.example: AI closes the review: "Accountability structure for the triage AI: (1) Clinical accountability — the attending physician retains final triage authority; AI score is advisory, not determinative. Document this in the clinical protocol. (2) Governance — AI systems in direct patient care must be reviewed by the ethics committee annually. Add this system to the annual review roster. (3) Patient recourse — patients and families may request human-only triage; this must be documented in the patient rights notice. (4) Incident reporting — any triage outcome where the AI score is later assessed as materially wrong must be logged and reviewed in monthly mortality and morbidity rounds."
accountability.antipattern: Deploying a clinical AI with accountability assigned to the vendor — 'the vendor is responsible for the model's outputs.' Clinical accountability cannot be contracted out; the treating institution and clinician remain legally and ethically responsible.

collaboration.description: Dignity audit examines disparate outcomes and consent implications → environmental footprint is assessed against clinical efficiency thresholds → privacy due diligence covers data residency, secondary use, and deletion → clinician transparency requirements are specified → clinical accountability and patient recourse are defined before deployment approval is granted.

sequence.label: Assess disparate outcomes, consent, and patient autonomy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The ethics committee is reviewing a proposed AI triage system. Start with dignity.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three dignity questions for the committee: (1) Disparate outcomes — has the system been validated on a patient population matching your hospital's demographic mix? Specifically, what are the performance metrics for elderly patients, patients with disabilities, and minority ethnic groups? (2) Consent — are patients informed that an AI score may influence their triage priority? If not, that is a consent gap. (3) Autonomy — is there a protocol for patients or families to request human-only triage? If the system provides no opt-out, it overrides patient autonomy by default. The committee should not approve deployment until all three are addressed.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Validation data shows lower accuracy for patients over 80. Consent notice is absent. No opt-out protocol exists.

sequence.triggers_next: Dignity gaps identified: three items require remediation before approval.

sequence.label: Assess computational footprint against clinical necessity

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The vendor is offering two model versions — a large high-accuracy model and a distilled model with 2% lower accuracy. Which do we choose?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: This is an ethical decision, not just a performance one: (1) Is the 2% accuracy difference clinically significant for triage? If the distilled model's error rate remains within the clinically acceptable range, the larger model's marginal accuracy gain does not justify the environmental cost. (2) Estimate the energy difference at your inference volume — for continuous inference on 300 patients per day, a 10x model size difference can translate to thousands of kWh per year. (3) Does the vendor's compute run on renewable energy? Request their data center energy disclosure. Unless the larger model is clinically necessary, the distilled model is the responsible choice.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Clinical team confirms distilled model meets the accuracy threshold. Distilled model selected.

sequence.triggers_next: Environmental assessment complete; model selection documented with rationale.

sequence.label: Conduct privacy due diligence on patient data handling

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The vendor processes patient data in the US and we are an EU hospital. What do we need?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four written confirmations required from the vendor before contracting: (1) Data residency — patient data must not leave the EU without a valid transfer mechanism (Standard Contractual Clauses or adequacy decision). Get the specific legal basis in writing. (2) Secondary use prohibition — patient data must not be used to train or improve the model without patient consent. Require a contractual prohibition on secondary use. (3) Data deletion — at contract termination, patient data is deleted within 30 days with a deletion certificate. (4) Breach notification — vendor must notify the hospital within 24 hours of any data incident involving patient records. Without all four in the DPA, do not sign.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Vendor confirmed EU-based processing and secondary use prohibition. DPA under legal review.

sequence.triggers_next: Privacy due diligence complete; DPA under legal review with four required clauses.

sequence.label: Define clinician explainability and override protocol

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What transparency requirements should we impose on the vendor for clinical use?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three transparency requirements for the clinical deployment: (1) Feature attribution — the system must display the top three factors driving each patient's triage score, in clinical language accessible to the triage nurse. (2) Failure mode training — before go-live, all triage staff must complete a 90-minute training on the system's known failure modes: which patient profiles (elderly, high comorbidity, atypical presentation) produce unreliable scores. (3) Override protocol — a documented clinical decision rule specifying which situations require the triage nurse to apply human-only judgment regardless of the AI score. This protocol must be approved by the chief of emergency medicine.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Vendor confirms feature attribution is available. Training program designed. Override protocol drafted for clinical review.

sequence.triggers_next: Clinician transparency requirements specified and vendor-confirmed.

loop_back.condition: Override protocol design reveals that the system cannot produce reliable scores for patients over 80, requiring a dignity re-assessment.
loop_back.reason: A system with known unreliable outputs for a demographic group requires a new dignity assessment before approval.

sequence.label: Define clinical accountability and patient recourse

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Who is accountable when the AI triage score contributes to an adverse patient outcome?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Accountability structure: (1) Clinical — the attending physician retains final triage authority. The AI score is documented as advisory in the clinical protocol; the physician's override is the authoritative clinical decision. This must be stated in writing in the clinical governance documents. (2) Ethics committee oversight — this system is added to the annual ethics review roster; the first review is 6 months post-deployment. (3) Patient rights — add to the patient rights notice: 'Our triage process may use AI decision support. You may request information about how AI is used or request human-only triage.' (4) M&M integration — any triage outcome where the AI score was later assessed as materially wrong is presented at the monthly mortality and morbidity round.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Clinical protocol updated. Patient rights notice amended. M&M integration confirmed.

sequence.triggers_next: Accountability structure defined; deployment approved by ethics committee.

transitions.trigger: Dignity gaps identified and remediation requirements documented.
Environmental assessment complete and model selection documented.
Privacy due diligence complete; DPA under legal review.
Override protocol reveals reliability gap for specific population — re-assess dignity.
Clinician transparency requirements specified.
Annual ethics review cycle — begin next assessment.

healthcare.title: AI-Assisted Clinical Documentation

delegation.description: AI can assist clinicians with drafting clinical notes from voice transcription, generating patient education material drafts, summarizing prior visit records, and producing structured documentation templates. All clinical judgment is non-delegable — diagnosis, treatment decisions, medication orders, and assessment of patient status require a licensed clinician's direct judgment and cannot be transferred to AI under any circumstances.
delegation.example: An internist uses an AI ambient documentation tool to generate a draft SOAP note from a recorded patient encounter. She reviews the full draft before it enters the EHR, corrects the AI's mischaracterization of the patient's symptom onset timeline, and signs only after verifying that the assessment and plan reflect her actual clinical reasoning.
delegation.antipattern: Allowing an AI documentation tool to finalize clinical notes without clinician review, or using AI-generated differential diagnoses as the actual clinical assessment without independent clinical evaluation — abdicating the professional judgment that defines clinical practice.

description.description: Provide AI with precise clinical context: the documentation type required (SOAP note, discharge summary, referral letter, patient education handout), the patient's relevant clinical background, the format required by your institution or EHR, and any clinical standards the document must meet. Vague inputs produce vague outputs that require more revision than starting from scratch.
description.example: Prompt: 'Draft a patient education handout for a 65-year-old patient with newly diagnosed Type 2 diabetes who speaks English as a second language. Reading level: 6th grade. Cover: what Type 2 diabetes is in plain language, the importance of blood sugar monitoring, dietary basics (Mediterranean diet pattern), and when to call the care team. Avoid medical jargon. Format: bullet points with short headers.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'Write patient instructions for diabetes.' This produces generic clinical text at an inappropriate reading level with medical terminology, requiring complete rewriting — no faster than drafting from scratch, and risking use of a generic template that doesn't match the specific patient's situation.

discernment.description: Scrutinize every AI-generated clinical document for medical accuracy before it enters the record or reaches a patient. Check for hallucinated medication names, incorrect dosages, fabricated lab values, inappropriate diagnostic language, or clinical claims that don't match the actual encounter. Patient safety depends on the clinician catching AI errors before they propagate into care decisions.
discernment.example: A hospitalist reviews an AI-generated discharge summary and catches that the AI has listed a drug at twice the prescribed dose and omitted a critical allergy from the medication list. She corrects both errors before the summary is sent to the receiving primary care physician — preventing a potential adverse drug event.
discernment.antipattern: Co-signing AI-generated discharge summaries, referral letters, or medication reconciliation documents without reading them in full — allowing a hallucinated dosage, an omitted allergy, or an incorrect diagnosis code to enter the medical record and influence downstream care decisions.

diligence.description: The clinician who signs a clinical document is fully accountable for its contents regardless of how it was drafted. Disclose AI use in documentation per your institution's policy and applicable regulatory requirements. Uphold patient safety as the absolute priority — when in doubt, rewrite rather than rely on AI output. Maintain the clinical judgment and interpersonal presence that AI documentation tools must never replace.
diligence.example: A health system requires all clinicians using AI documentation tools to complete a verified review checklist (patient identity, diagnoses, medications, allergies, follow-up instructions) before signing any AI-assisted note, and discloses the use of AI documentation assistance in its patient-facing privacy notice.
diligence.antipattern: Signing AI-generated clinical notes at volume without individual review because the ambient tool 'usually gets it right' — creating systematic documentation errors that accumulate in the medical record, affect quality metrics, and in serious cases contribute to patient harm through propagated incorrect information.

healthcare.summary: AI can assist clinicians with drafting clinical notes from voice transcription, generating patient education material drafts, summarizing prior visit records, and producing structured documentation te

collaboration.description: Clinician delegates note drafting, provides encounter details, reviews AI draft for clinical accuracy, then signs — strictly linear with no shortcuts due to patient safety requirements.

sequence.label: Define AI's documentation role

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Draft the SOAP note for this encounter. You structure the note — I'll verify all clinical content.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I include medication reconciliation in the plan section or keep that separate?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Include it in the plan — I'll verify it against the pharmacy record.

sequence.triggers_next: AI's documentation scope and human verification commitment agreed.

sequence.label: Provide encounter details and patient context

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Chief complaint: chest pain, 2-day duration. Vitals and exam findings attached. History of hypertension.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I flag any discrepancies I notice between the stated history and the exam findings?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — flag anything that seems inconsistent for my review.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has full encounter context and flag-on-inconsistency rule.

sequence.label: Review AI draft for clinical accuracy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The assessment lists 'rule out MI' — change to 'elevated troponin, workup in progress'.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Corrected. The plan section uses 'may consider' — do you want more definitive language?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Yes — change to 'initiate' for the aspirin order, pending cardiology consult.

sequence.triggers_next: Every clinical claim verified by the clinician — no AI-only assertions remain.

sequence.label: Sign and lock the note

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Note reviewed in full. Signing and locking in the EHR.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: AI-assisted draft flagged in the note metadata per compliance policy.

sequence.triggers_next: Note locked. Next encounter requires a fresh cycle.

transitions.trigger: Documentation scope and verification commitment agreed.
AI delivers draft note.
Every clinical claim verified.
Next encounter — fresh cycle.

legal.title: AI-Assisted Legal Research and Document Drafting

delegation.description: AI can assist with initial legal research sweeps, summarizing case law, generating first-draft document structures, and flagging potentially relevant statutes. All legal judgment remains with the licensed attorney — case strategy, legal conclusions, advice to clients, and all court filings are non-delegable professional responsibilities. AI is a research and drafting accelerant, never a substitute for attorney analysis.
delegation.example: An attorney preparing a breach of contract motion asks AI to identify relevant California appellate cases on implied covenant of good faith and generate a draft argument outline. She uses this as a research starting point, independently verifies every citation via Westlaw, and rewrites the argument section from scratch using the verified cases.
delegation.antipattern: Filing AI-generated legal briefs with unverified citations, or allowing AI to make legal strategy recommendations that the attorney adopts without independent analysis — the equivalent of filing research you haven't read, which creates professional responsibility violations and potential sanctions.

description.description: Provide AI with precise legal context: jurisdiction, court or regulatory body, matter type, applicable legal standard, client's factual situation, and the desired document format and purpose. Legal AI output quality depends heavily on the specificity of jurisdictional and doctrinal framing. Generic legal prompts produce generic legal text that may be wrong for your jurisdiction.
description.example: Prompt: 'Summarize the current standard for personal jurisdiction over foreign defendants in federal courts in the Ninth Circuit, focusing on cases decided after 2019 that address the specific vs. general jurisdiction distinction post-Bristol-Myers Squibb. Our client is a California-based plaintiff suing a German manufacturer. Identify the strongest and weakest points in our jurisdictional argument.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'Explain personal jurisdiction.' This produces a textbook overview applicable to no particular jurisdiction or procedural posture — unusable for actual legal work without the jurisdictional specificity, relevant time frame, and case-specific framing that makes the output actionable.

discernment.description: Every AI-generated legal citation must be independently verified in a primary legal database before use. AI models hallucinate case citations with high confidence — inventing plausible-sounding but nonexistent cases, misquoting holdings, or attributing dicta as holdings. Verify that each case exists, says what AI claims, is still good law, and applies in your jurisdiction.
discernment.example: An attorney receives an AI-generated brief draft citing eight cases. She runs each citation in Westlaw before reviewing the draft's argument. Two citations are for real cases that don't say what the AI claimed. One citation is for a case that was overruled. She treats the AI draft as a structural scaffold only and rebuilds the argument on verified authority.
discernment.antipattern: Submitting a brief with AI-generated citations after only spot-checking one or two, then receiving a sanctions motion when opposing counsel discovers that three cited cases either don't exist or don't stand for the propositions cited — a career-threatening outcome that has already occurred to real attorneys.

diligence.description: The licensed attorney remains fully and personally responsible for every document filed and every piece of advice given, regardless of how it was drafted. Disclose AI assistance in accordance with applicable bar rules and court standing orders (several federal courts now require this). Maintain professional standards of competence, including understanding AI's limitations in legal contexts.
diligence.example: An attorney follows her state bar's guidance on AI use in legal practice by disclosing in engagement letters that AI tools assist with research and drafting, maintaining a verification protocol for all AI-generated citations, and staying current with her court's local rules on AI disclosure in filings.
diligence.antipattern: Treating AI legal research as equivalent to Westlaw research without verification, failing to disclose AI assistance in jurisdictions where disclosure is required, or allowing junior associates to file AI-drafted documents without supervising attorney review — creating professional responsibility exposure and potential harm to clients.

legal.summary: AI can assist with initial legal research sweeps, summarizing case law, generating first-draft document structures, and flagging potentially relevant statutes. All legal judgment remains with the lice

collaboration.description: Attorney delegates legal research, provides matter context and jurisdiction, verifies AI findings against authoritative sources, then uses the work product — strictly linear due to professional liability.

sequence.label: Define AI's research role and limits

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Research current case law on non-compete enforceability in California. Surface the key cases — I'll do final legal analysis.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I include federal circuit decisions or only California state courts?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Both — but flag circuit splits if they exist.

sequence.triggers_next: Research scope, jurisdiction, and attorney analysis responsibility agreed.

sequence.label: Provide matter context and legal question

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Client is a software company seeking to enforce a 2-year non-compete against a departed engineer. Employment ended 6 months ago.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I focus on enforceability standards or also include remedy options?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Enforceability first — remedies are a separate memo.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has matter facts and specific legal question.

sequence.label: Verify AI research against authoritative sources

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Citation 3 — I need to verify that holding directly. Pull the full opinion.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Here is the full text of Edwards v. Arthur Andersen. The holding is on page 12.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Confirmed. Citation 4 has been superseded — remove it and note why.

sequence.triggers_next: Every citation verified against primary sources. No unverified AI assertions remain.

sequence.label: Review, sign off, and document

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Partner reviews research memo before it goes to client.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Noting AI-assisted research in the matter file per firm policy.

sequence.triggers_next: Memo approved and filed. Next matter question requires a fresh cycle.

transitions.trigger: Research scope and liability boundaries agreed.
AI delivers research findings.
Every citation independently verified.
Next legal question — fresh cycle.

risk-classification.description: The team systematically determines whether the AI system falls into the prohibited, high-risk, limited-risk, or minimal-risk category by applying the Act's annexes and the system's actual intended purpose and deployment context — not its marketing description.
risk-classification.example: Legal counsel: "Our AI system suggests interview questions to hiring managers. Marketing calls it a 'decision support tool.' Is it high-risk?" AI: "Yes — under Annex III of the EU AI Act, AI systems used in employment and recruitment decisions are classified as high-risk regardless of how they are marketed. 'Decision support' does not reduce the classification if the system materially influences the outcome. The relevant question is: does a human hiring manager typically follow the system's suggestions? If yes, the system is used in a manner that determines or influences the recruitment decision — high-risk classification applies."
risk-classification.antipattern: Accepting a vendor's classification of their own system as 'minimal-risk' without independently applying the Act's annexes to the actual use case and deployment context.

transparency.description: The team identifies the full set of transparency and documentation obligations applicable to the classified risk tier — technical file, instructions for use, logging, and user notification — and creates a compliance documentation plan.
transparency.example: AI: "For a high-risk hiring AI, the transparency obligations are: (1) Technical file (Article 11) — detailed system documentation including design specifications, training data description, performance metrics, and risk management records. Must be maintained for 10 years post-market. (2) Instructions for use (Article 13) — plain-language guidance for deployers including the system's purpose, performance limitations, and oversight requirements. (3) Logging (Article 12) — the system must automatically log its inputs and outputs to the extent technically feasible. (4) Notification — affected candidates must be informed that AI is used in the recruitment process. Which of these does your current system documentation cover?"
transparency.antipattern: Treating technical file preparation as a one-time pre-certification task rather than a continuous documentation obligation — allowing the file to diverge from the deployed system within months of certification.

human-oversight.description: The team designs human oversight mechanisms that comply with Article 14 — ensuring deployers can meaningfully monitor, intervene in, and override the AI system's outputs under real operational conditions.
human-oversight.example: Human: "We have a human review step before a hiring decision is made. Is that sufficient for Article 14?" AI: "It depends on the quality of the review, not its existence. Article 14 requires that human oversight is 'effective' — the reviewer must understand the system's outputs and limitations, have the ability and authority to override the system, and not be under time pressure that makes real review impossible. If your hiring managers are reviewing AI recommendations for 30 seconds before approving, that is nominal oversight, not effective oversight. I recommend documenting: the time allocated for review, the training provided to reviewers on the system's limitations, and the rate at which reviewers actually override the AI — as evidence of effective oversight."
human-oversight.antipattern: Satisfying Article 14 with a checkbox in the interface that confirms 'human reviewed this decision' without any mechanism to verify that the review was substantive or effective.

robustness.description: The team validates that the AI system meets technical standards for accuracy, cybersecurity, and resilience — and documents testing evidence that supports the conformity assessment.
robustness.example: AI: "For robustness documentation of a high-risk hiring AI, you need: (1) Accuracy metrics — overall and disaggregated by protected group. 'Accuracy' for a hiring AI should include candidate quality outcomes, not just model prediction metrics. (2) Bias testing — third-party disparity audit across gender, age, and ethnicity for the roles in scope. (3) Cybersecurity — evidence that candidate data is protected against unauthorized access; penetration test results. (4) Resilience — how does the system behave when input data is missing, corrupted, or anomalous? All four evidence types are needed for the technical file."
robustness.antipattern: Documenting only aggregate model accuracy without disaggregated performance metrics by demographic group — leaving a material compliance gap in the technical file for a high-risk system.

governance.description: The team plans the full conformity pathway — notified body involvement if required, EU AI database registration, post-market monitoring system, and incident reporting obligations — before the system is deployed to market.
governance.example: AI closes the cycle: "Governance compliance plan for the high-risk hiring AI: (1) Conformity assessment — high-risk AI in employment requires either a third-party notified body assessment or a manufacturer's own conformity assessment depending on whether harmonized standards are applied. Confirm which pathway with legal. (2) EU AI database registration — required before deployment under Article 71. Register at the Commission's AI Office portal. (3) Post-market monitoring — implement quarterly performance reviews including bias audits and incident log review. Name a post-market monitoring officer. (4) Incident reporting — serious incidents must be reported to the national market surveillance authority within the timeframe specified in Article 73. Name the reporting responsible. Target: all four items complete before first deployment."
governance.antipattern: Treating post-market monitoring as a future compliance task to be designed after the system launches — leaving a gap between deployment and active monitoring that creates legal exposure under Article 72.

collaboration.description: Risk tier is determined using the Act's annexes applied to actual use → full transparency and documentation obligations are mapped → human oversight mechanism is designed for real operational conditions → technical validation evidence is compiled → conformity assessment and post-market monitoring plan are completed before deployment.

sequence.label: Classify the AI system using Act annexes and actual use context

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're deploying an AI that screens job applications and ranks candidates. What's our EU AI Act classification?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Applying Annex III: AI systems used in employment and recruitment — specifically for 'screening or filtering applications, evaluating candidates in the course of interviews or tests, or making decisions about promotion or termination' — are classified as high-risk. Your system ranks candidates, which directly influences shortlisting decisions. Classification: High-Risk. This triggers the full compliance requirements under Articles 8-15. Note: if the vendor classifies this as 'minimal risk,' they are misclassifying it — you as the deployer are legally responsible for correct classification and compliance.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: High-risk classification confirmed. We'll update the project documentation and notify the vendor.

sequence.triggers_next: Risk tier confirmed as High-Risk with Article and Annex citations documented.

sequence.label: Map documentation and disclosure obligations

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What documentation do we need for a high-risk hiring AI?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four documentation streams: (1) Technical file — design specifications, training data description, performance metrics, risk management records, and post-market monitoring plan. Must be maintained for 10 years from market placement. (2) Instructions for use — plain-language deployer guide: system purpose, performance limits, oversight requirements, and prohibited uses. (3) Automatic logging — log inputs and outputs to the extent technically feasible; retain logs per your data retention policy minimum. (4) Candidate notification — inform applicants in the job posting or application process that AI is used in candidate screening. Which of these is not yet in place?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Candidate notification is missing from our job postings. Technical file is incomplete — no performance metrics for protected groups.

sequence.triggers_next: Documentation gaps identified and prioritized for remediation.

sequence.label: Design effective human oversight for Article 14

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Our HR managers review AI rankings before shortlisting. Is that Article 14 compliant?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three things to check: (1) Understanding — do HR managers understand what the system ranks on and what its known limitations are? If not, training is required before oversight is meaningful. (2) Authority — do they have explicit authority to override the ranking, and is there a documented process for doing so? (3) Time — how long do managers spend reviewing each ranking? If it is under 2 minutes for a ranking of 20 candidates, the review is likely nominal. Recommendation: document the oversight process, train reviewers on system limitations, log override rates, and set a minimum review time guideline. Override rate below 5% is a red flag — it suggests reviewers are rubber-stamping the AI.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Reviewer training scheduled. Override rate will be logged from launch.

sequence.triggers_next: Human oversight mechanism designed, documented, and training scheduled.

loop_back.condition: Oversight design reveals the system is being used beyond the classified use case.
loop_back.reason: Extended use cases may trigger additional Annex III categories — classification must be reviewed.

sequence.label: Compile technical validation evidence for the technical file

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What technical evidence do we need for the conformity assessment?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four evidence packages: (1) Performance metrics — overall and disaggregated by gender, age, ethnicity for each role type. Include both precision/recall and business outcome metrics (quality of hire). (2) Bias audit — third-party disparity analysis; I recommend contracting this independently of the vendor. (3) Cybersecurity — penetration test report not older than 12 months; data encryption documentation. (4) Resilience testing — what happens when the model receives incomplete CVs, names in non-Western scripts, or unusual formatting? Document the degradation boundary. Which of these four does the vendor currently provide, and which requires your own testing?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Vendor provides packages 1 and 3. We need to commission a third-party bias audit and run our own resilience tests.

sequence.triggers_next: Technical validation evidence compiled or commissioned; gaps documented.

sequence.label: Complete conformity assessment and post-market monitoring plan

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What is our path to lawful market deployment?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Deployment governance checklist: (1) Conformity assessment — for hiring AI, you can use the internal conformity assessment route if your technical file is complete and you apply harmonized standards. If no harmonized standard exists for your specific use case, a notified body assessment is safer. Confirm with legal. (2) EU AI database registration — required under Article 71 before first use; register via the Commission's AI Act portal. (3) Post-market monitoring plan — quarterly bias audits, monthly incident log review, annual technical file update. Name a post-market monitoring responsible person now, before deployment. (4) Incident reporting — serious incidents (significant impact on health, safety, or fundamental rights) reported to the national market surveillance authority within the Article 73 timeframe. Legal must confirm the reporting pathway for your jurisdiction.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Conformity assessment in progress. Database registration submitted. Post-market monitoring officer named.

sequence.triggers_next: Conformity assessment complete; database registered; post-market monitoring operational.

transitions.trigger: Risk tier confirmed with Annex citations.
Documentation obligations mapped and gaps identified.
Oversight design reveals use beyond classified scope.
Human oversight mechanism designed and documented.
Technical validation evidence compiled or commissioned.
System update or new use case triggers re-classification review.

legal.title: EU AI Act High-Risk System Documentation

risk-classification.description: The team confirms the high-risk classification in writing, citing the specific Annex III entry, the intended purpose, the deployment context, and any use-case changes that could affect the classification — creating the foundational document for the entire compliance package.
risk-classification.example: Legal counsel: "We need a written classification rationale for the technical file. What does it need to contain?" AI: "The classification rationale document should contain: (1) System identification — name, version, and intended purpose in the deployer's own words. (2) Annex III mapping — the specific Annex III entry that applies, with a one-paragraph explanation of why the system's actual use matches that entry. (3) Deployment context — who deploys it, in which jurisdictions, in what operational setting. (4) Scope boundaries — what uses are out of scope and how the system prevents out-of-scope use. (5) Review trigger — what changes to the system or its deployment context would require a new classification review. This document is a living record, not a one-time declaration."
risk-classification.antipattern: Writing the classification rationale as a summary of the marketing description rather than an analysis of the actual use against Annex III — producing a document that fails regulatory scrutiny.

transparency.description: The team produces the complete transparency documentation package: the technical file under Article 11, instructions for use under Article 13, the logging specification under Article 12, and the candidate/user notification mechanism.
transparency.example: AI: "Technical file (Article 11) must contain: (1) General description — system purpose, intended use, and technical specifications. (2) Design and development process — how the system was built, including training data description, data governance, and testing methodology. (3) Performance metrics — accuracy, precision, recall, and fairness metrics disaggregated by relevant subgroups. (4) Risk management — the risk management system applied during development. (5) Changes — documentation of all versions and material changes. (6) Post-market monitoring plan — how the system will be monitored after deployment. The file must be maintained for 10 years. Assign a document owner now."
transparency.antipattern: Preparing the technical file as a one-time document for the conformity assessment without assigning an owner responsible for keeping it current — allowing the file to diverge from the deployed system within months.

human-oversight.description: The team documents the human oversight mechanism in a format that satisfies Article 14 — including the oversight design, the reviewer training program, the override capability, and evidence that oversight is effective rather than nominal.
human-oversight.example: Human: "How do we document human oversight in a way that satisfies Article 14 for a regulator?" AI: "Article 14 documentation package: (1) Oversight design document — describes the oversight mechanism: who reviews, at what stage, with what information, and with what authority to override. (2) Reviewer training program — syllabus, duration, completion tracking, and assessment results. Regulators will ask whether reviewers understand the system's limitations. (3) Override capability specification — technical documentation showing that the override function exists, is accessible, and has been tested. (4) Effectiveness evidence — override rate data (what percentage of AI outputs are overridden?) and a sample of documented override decisions. A 0% override rate is a red flag — it suggests nominal rather than effective oversight."
human-oversight.antipattern: Documenting human oversight as a process description ('the reviewer checks the output before the decision is made') without providing evidence that the oversight mechanism is technically implemented, that reviewers are trained, and that overrides actually occur.

robustness.description: The team compiles the technical validation evidence for the technical file — accuracy testing, bias audits, cybersecurity assessment, and resilience testing — in a format that supports the conformity assessment.
robustness.example: AI: "Robustness evidence package for the technical file: (1) Performance testing report — aggregate and disaggregated accuracy metrics. For high-risk systems with individual impact, disaggregation by protected group is required — overall accuracy without subgroup analysis is insufficient. (2) Bias audit report — third-party preferred; document methodology, metrics used, and findings. If a disparity was found, document the remediation and re-test results. (3) Cybersecurity assessment — penetration test report not older than 12 months; data encryption documentation. (4) Resilience testing — what happens at the system's performance boundaries? Document the degradation profile: what inputs produce unreliable outputs and how does the system signal this to the user?"
robustness.antipattern: Submitting a conformity assessment with only aggregate performance metrics and no subgroup analysis — producing a documentation package that fails regulatory review for fairness-sensitive applications.

governance.description: The team produces the Declaration of Conformity, registers the system in the EU AI database, and documents the post-market monitoring system — completing the governance documentation required before first deployment.
governance.example: AI closes the documentation cycle: "Final governance documentation: (1) Declaration of Conformity — signed by the provider (not the deployer) under Article 47; contains: system identification, conformity assessment route taken, applied standards, and the provider's name and address. (2) EU AI database registration — register via the Commission's AI Act portal before deployment; retain the registration number. (3) Post-market monitoring plan — document: who monitors, what metrics, at what frequency, what triggers a review or incident report, and what the escalation path is for serious incidents under Article 73. (4) Technical file index — a table of contents listing every document in the technical file with its version and last-updated date. This index is what a notified body auditor will review first."
governance.antipattern: Treating the Declaration of Conformity as the end of the compliance process — missing the EU AI database registration and post-market monitoring obligations that are legally required before and after market deployment.

collaboration.description: Classification rationale is confirmed in writing → technical file and transparency documentation package is built out → human oversight mechanism is documented with effectiveness evidence → robustness evidence is compiled with subgroup analysis → Declaration of Conformity and database registration complete the governance package.

sequence.label: Confirm and document classification rationale

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We've classified our AI as high-risk. What's the first documentation step?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Write the classification rationale document before any other documentation work. Five sections: (1) System identification — name, version, purpose. (2) Annex III mapping — cite the specific entry and explain why your system's actual use matches it. Quote the Annex text directly. (3) Deployment context — jurisdictions, operator type, user type, and operational setting. (4) Scope boundaries — what uses are explicitly out of scope and what technical controls enforce that boundary? (5) Review trigger — what changes to the system or its use would require a new classification review? Have legal counsel approve this document before the technical file is assembled — it is the foundation for everything that follows.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Classification rationale drafted, citing Annex III paragraph 4 (employment screening). Legal approved.

sequence.triggers_next: Classification rationale documented and legally approved.

sequence.label: Build the technical file and transparency documentation

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What does the technical file need to contain and who owns each section?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Technical file sections with owners: (1) General description — Product owner; due: 2 weeks. (2) Design and development — ML engineering; includes: training data description, data governance, model architecture, training methodology. Due: 3 weeks. (3) Performance metrics — Data science; includes: aggregate and disaggregated accuracy, fairness metrics. Due: 3 weeks. (4) Risk management — Risk/legal; the risk management process applied during development. Due: 2 weeks. (5) Changes log — Technical writer; tracks all versions. Ongoing. (6) Post-market monitoring plan — Risk/compliance; Due: 4 weeks. Instructions for use (Article 13) — Technical writer; plain-language deployer guide. Due: 3 weeks. Assign owners and due dates now; the technical file cannot be assembled retroactively.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All section owners assigned. Three-week documentation sprint started.

sequence.triggers_next: Technical file section ownership assigned; documentation sprint started.

sequence.label: Document oversight mechanism with effectiveness evidence

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we document human oversight in a way that will satisfy a notified body auditor?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four-document oversight package: (1) Oversight design specification — describes the oversight mechanism in technical detail: who has access to the override function, at what point in the process, with what information visible to the reviewer. Include a process flow diagram. (2) Training program documentation — course outline, delivery method, duration, and assessment. Include completion records for all current reviewers. (3) Override capability test evidence — screenshot or screen recording showing the override function in the production UI; test log showing override testing in QA. (4) Override rate data — pull the past 3 months of production data: what percentage of AI outputs were overridden? If 0%, investigate — this suggests the override function is not being used, which is an oversight effectiveness finding.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Override rate: 8% — reviewers are actively overriding. All four documents compiled.

sequence.triggers_next: Human oversight documentation package complete with effectiveness evidence.

loop_back.condition: Oversight documentation reveals the system is used in ways beyond the classification rationale scope.
loop_back.reason: Extended use scope requires classification rationale update before the technical file is finalized.

sequence.label: Compile technical validation evidence

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What robustness evidence do we need for the technical file?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Four evidence packages: (1) Performance testing report — format: table with accuracy, precision, recall by: overall population, and each protected characteristic (gender, age band, ethnicity) for the employment screening use case. Any subgroup with accuracy more than 5 percentage points below overall must be flagged and explained. (2) Third-party bias audit — commissioned independently of the development team; include methodology, disparity metrics, findings, and any remediation and re-test results. (3) Cybersecurity — penetration test report (not older than 12 months) and data encryption documentation for applicant data at rest and in transit. (4) Resilience — document the system's behavior at edge cases: incomplete CVs, non-standard formats, languages outside the training set. Include the system's warning or degradation signal for out-of-distribution inputs.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: All four packages compiled. Third-party bias audit complete — one finding remediated and re-tested.

sequence.triggers_next: Robustness evidence package complete with subgroup analysis.

sequence.label: Produce Declaration of Conformity and register in EU database

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: What are the final governance steps before we can deploy?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three pre-deployment governance actions: (1) Declaration of Conformity — drafted by legal, signed by the provider's authorized representative. Must contain: system identification, conformity assessment route (internal assessment or notified body), list of applied harmonized standards or common specifications, and provider details. One copy retained for 10 years. (2) EU AI database registration — register at the Commission's AI Act portal. Required before first deployment. Retain the registration number and screenshot confirmation. (3) Post-market monitoring — confirm the monitoring plan is operational: monitoring owner named, first monthly review scheduled, incident reporting escalation path documented and tested. Once all three are complete, deployment is legally authorized. Document the deployment authorization date.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Declaration signed. EU database registration confirmed. Monitoring plan operational. Deployment authorized.

sequence.triggers_next: Full compliance documentation package complete; deployment legally authorized.

transitions.trigger: Classification rationale legally approved.
Technical file sections assigned and documentation sprint started.
Oversight documentation reveals use beyond classification scope.
Oversight documentation package complete.
Robustness evidence package complete.
Material system change triggers classification review and documentation update.

inclusive-growth.description: The policy is designed to distribute AI benefits across the full population — not just technologically advanced sectors — with explicit provisions for communities at risk of being left behind by AI-driven economic change.
inclusive-growth.example: Policy analyst: "Our national AI strategy focuses on economic competitiveness. The OECD Principles require we also address distributional impact. Can you help identify which population groups are most at risk of exclusion from AI benefits in our context?" AI: "Based on the OECD AI Policy Observatory data, the three groups most consistently at risk are: workers in routine-task occupations (displacement risk), rural communities with limited digital infrastructure, and populations with low AI literacy. I recommend the policy include specific measures for each — not just a general 'digital inclusion' clause."
inclusive-growth.antipattern: Writing an AI policy that focuses entirely on innovation promotion and competitiveness without any provisions for equitable distribution of benefits or mitigation of AI-related economic displacement.

human-centred.description: The policy enshrines human rights protections and democratic values as constraints on AI use — not optional considerations. It identifies which rights are most at risk from AI and prescribes protections.
human-centred.example: AI: "For human-centred value provisions, I recommend mapping three rights categories: (1) Procedural rights — the right to explanation and challenge for consequential automated decisions. (2) Substantive rights — non-discrimination protections applied to AI outputs. (3) Collective rights — protection of democratic processes from AI manipulation (deepfakes, synthetic influence). Which are already covered in your existing legal framework and which require new policy provisions?"
human-centred.antipattern: Treating human rights compliance as a legal reference in the policy preamble rather than a design constraint that shapes what AI applications the policy permits, restricts, or prohibits.

transparency.description: The policy requires disclosure standards for AI systems deployed in public services and high-impact private sector applications — including when citizens are subject to AI decisions and what recourse they have.
transparency.example: Human: "Should the policy require explainability for all AI systems or only specific categories?" AI: "The OECD framework supports a risk-proportionate approach: require full explainability for consequential decisions (benefits, justice, employment, credit) and AI-interaction disclosure for customer-facing systems. A blanket requirement for all AI would be unenforceable and technically impossible for all model types. I recommend three tiers: decision explainability, interaction disclosure, and general transparency reporting."
transparency.antipattern: Setting a transparency requirement so broad it is unenforceable, or so narrow it only covers government AI while leaving high-impact private sector AI unaddressed.

robustness.description: The policy establishes minimum technical and operational standards for AI safety and security in critical sectors, and assigns responsibility for defining and updating these standards over time.
robustness.example: AI: "For robustness provisions, the policy should address three levels: (1) Sectoral standards — what safety requirements apply to AI in healthcare, transport, critical infrastructure? (2) Incident reporting — mandatory reporting of significant AI failures to a national authority. (3) Standards body — which existing body (standards institute, sector regulator) is mandated to define and update technical AI safety standards? Without a named body, the provision has no enforcement mechanism."
robustness.antipattern: Listing 'AI safety' as a policy objective without specifying who sets technical standards, what those standards are, and what happens when they are violated.

accountability.description: The policy assigns clear accountability for AI outcomes to deployers, developers, and public authorities — and establishes redress mechanisms for individuals harmed by AI decisions.
accountability.example: AI: "Accountability provisions should distinguish three roles: (1) Developers — liable for fundamental safety properties of models they release. (2) Deployers — liable for how those models are applied to specific use cases. (3) Public authorities — accountable for AI in public services with an appeals pathway for citizens. Without this role distinction, enforcement is impossible — every actor will point to another when harm occurs."
accountability.antipattern: Writing accountability provisions that name no specific roles, reference only 'AI actors' generally, and provide no redress mechanism for individuals harmed by AI decisions.

collaboration.description: Inclusive growth analysis identifies who benefits and who is at risk → human rights mapping determines which rights require new policy protections → transparency provisions are scoped to risk proportionality → robustness standards are assigned to named bodies → accountability roles are distinguished and redress mechanisms defined. Loops back to human-centred if transparency provisions conflict with existing rights frameworks.

sequence.label: Map AI benefit distribution and inclusion risks

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We're drafting a national AI policy. Start with inclusive growth — who benefits and who doesn't in our current context?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: To map distribution risks for your context, I need: (1) Your country's current AI adoption profile — which sectors are leading? (2) Labor market data — which occupations are most exposed to AI displacement? (3) Infrastructure gaps — which regions lack digital access? Once I have these, I can identify the three to five population groups that need explicit policy provisions, and draft inclusion-specific measures beyond a general 'digital access' clause.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Leading sectors: finance and logistics. Displacement exposure: truck drivers and data-entry clerks. Infrastructure gaps: rural northern regions.

sequence.triggers_next: At-risk groups identified with sector and regional specificity.

sequence.label: Map rights at risk and design protective provisions

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Which human rights are most at risk from AI in our context?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: For your context (finance and logistics AI leaders, displacement risks, rural gaps): (1) Right to work — AI displacement in logistics without retraining provisions violates substantive economic rights. (2) Right to non-discrimination — AI in financial services without fairness requirements risks entrenching lending discrimination. (3) Right to an effective remedy — if AI makes a credit or benefits decision, citizens need an appeal pathway. Which of these is already covered in existing law and which requires new policy provisions?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Right to remedy is a gap — no current AI-specific appeals mechanism exists.

sequence.triggers_next: Rights gaps identified; new policy provisions scoped.

loop_back.condition: Transparency provisions in step 3 conflict with existing data protection rights.
loop_back.reason: Rights conflicts must be resolved before transparency requirements are finalized.

sequence.label: Define proportionate transparency requirements by risk tier

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How should our transparency requirements be structured?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Risk-proportionate three-tier model: Tier 1 (High-risk consequential decisions — credit, benefits, justice): full explainability required, citizen notification mandatory. Tier 2 (Customer-facing AI interaction): disclosure that AI is being used, opt-out right for sensitive services. Tier 3 (Back-office AI): general transparency reporting to a designated authority annually. This avoids both unenforceable blanket requirements and the opposite — no requirements at all.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We'll adopt the three-tier model with the designated authority being our existing data protection regulator.

sequence.triggers_next: Transparency tiers defined with enforcement body named.

sequence.label: Assign standards bodies and define sector safety requirements

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Who should set AI safety standards in our country?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three options: (1) Mandate an existing standards body (e.g., national metrology institute) to adopt international standards (ISO 42001, NIST AI RMF) with a national supplement. (2) Create a new AI technical committee under your standards body with industry and civil society representation. (3) Delegate to sector regulators — transport safety for autonomous vehicles, health authority for medical AI. Option 3 is fastest but risks inconsistency across sectors. I recommend option 1 with sector supplements.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: We'll mandate the national standards institute with a 12-month timeline to produce the first national AI safety standard.

sequence.triggers_next: Standards body named, timeline set, sector requirements scoped.

sequence.label: Assign accountability roles and define citizen redress

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: How do we structure accountability in the policy without it being unenforceable?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Three-role accountability structure: (1) AI developers — register high-risk AI systems with the national authority, provide technical documentation. (2) AI deployers — responsible for use-case compliance, must have a named AI risk officer for high-risk deployments. (3) Public authorities — use of AI in public services requires an impact assessment and a citizen appeals mechanism with a 30-day resolution SLA. Enforcement: existing sector regulators are the primary enforcers, with a cross-sector AI oversight body as the national coordinator. This maps directly to the OECD accountability principle without requiring a new regulatory body from scratch.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Agreed. We'll designate the data protection authority as the cross-sector AI coordinator.

sequence.triggers_next: Accountability roles, enforcement bodies, and redress mechanisms defined.

transitions.trigger: At-risk groups and inclusion gaps identified.
Rights gaps mapped and new policy provisions scoped.
Transparency provisions conflict with existing rights framework.
Transparency tiers defined with enforcement body.
Standards body named and sector safety requirements scoped.
Policy review cycle — restart at next annual assessment.

research.title: Research Paper Data Analysis with AI

delegation.description: Divide analytical work deliberately between human scholarly judgment and AI computation. AI handles pattern recognition in large datasets, running descriptive statistics, flagging outliers, generating visualization suggestions, and summarizing distributions. The researcher formulates hypotheses, interprets findings in disciplinary context, makes causal claims, and draws conclusions — these require domain expertise that AI cannot reliably supply.
delegation.example: A social scientist delegates to AI the task of computing correlation matrices across 47 variables in a survey dataset and generating a ranked list of significant correlations. She then applies her theoretical knowledge to determine which correlations are substantively meaningful versus statistically coincidental.
delegation.antipattern: Asking AI to not only run analysis but to formulate the research hypotheses, interpret what findings mean for the field, and draw conclusions — treating the model as a substitute for the researcher's own disciplinary expertise and scholarly judgment.

description.description: Give AI precise methodological context — your research question, the dataset structure, units of measurement, required statistical methods, software environment, and any domain-specific constraints. Ambiguous instructions produce analysis that may be technically correct but methodologically wrong for your research design.
description.example: Prompt: 'Analyze this CSV of longitudinal student performance data (n=312, 4 time points). Variables: test_score (0-100), SES_quartile (1-4), intervention_group (binary), school_id (cluster variable). Run a mixed-effects linear regression predicting test_score from intervention_group, controlling for SES_quartile, with school_id as a random effect. Report coefficients, standard errors, and interpret the intervention effect size.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'Analyze my research data and tell me what's significant.' Without specifying the research question, the correct statistical model, variable types, or analytical constraints, AI may apply an inappropriate method (e.g., OLS on clustered data) that produces misleading results.

discernment.description: Rigorously verify AI-produced analysis before incorporating it into research. Check that statistical methods are appropriate for your data structure, that calculations are correct, that effect sizes are interpreted accurately, and that the AI has not made assumptions that violate your study design. AI can produce plausible-looking but methodologically flawed analysis.
discernment.example: A researcher notices that AI-generated regression output reports a significant p-value but has failed to account for the clustered sampling structure, inflating the precision of estimates. She reruns the analysis herself with correct standard errors and finds the effect is no longer significant at her threshold.
discernment.antipattern: Inserting AI-generated statistical tables and interpretive text directly into a manuscript without independently verifying the calculations, checking model assumptions, or confirming that the chosen method is appropriate for the study design — risking publication of flawed findings.

diligence.description: Be transparent about AI's role in your analytical workflow per your institution's and journal's policies. Ensure all claims in the final paper reflect your own validated analysis. Maintain full methodological accountability — if AI analysis is used, you must understand it well enough to defend every number in peer review.
diligence.example: A research team documents in their methods section that AI-assisted tools were used for initial descriptive analysis and outlier detection, while all inferential statistics were conducted and verified by the authors using R. They retain full analytical scripts for reproducibility review.
diligence.antipattern: Publishing a paper that includes AI-generated statistical analysis without independent verification, failing to disclose AI assistance in violation of journal policy, or being unable to explain or reproduce the reported numbers during peer review.

research.summary: Divide analytical work deliberately between human scholarly judgment and AI computation. AI handles pattern recognition in large datasets, running descriptive statistics, flagging outliers, generating

collaboration.description: Researcher delegates analysis scope, provides dataset and hypotheses, evaluates AI findings for statistical validity, then reports — looping back to description if data quality issues emerge.

sequence.label: Agree on analysis scope and AI autonomy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Analyze this survey dataset for correlations between feature usage and retention. Can you decide which statistical tests to run?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I can select appropriate tests given the distribution. Should I flag non-significant results or only report significant ones?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Flag all results — I want the full picture, not just the highlights.

sequence.triggers_next: Analysis scope and reporting rule agreed.

sequence.label: Provide dataset and hypotheses

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Here's the dataset. Hypothesis: users who use feature X in the first week have higher 90-day retention.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: The dataset has 12% missing values in the retention column — how should I handle them?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Exclude records with missing retention data and note the exclusion count.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has clean dataset, hypotheses, and data handling rules.

sequence.label: Evaluate analysis findings

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: The correlation coefficient looks high — is this sample size large enough for that claim?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: N=340 gives 80% power for this effect size. The result is statistically robust.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Good. Flag finding 3 as exploratory — the sample for that subgroup is only N=28.

sequence.triggers_next: Findings validated for statistical robustness and appropriate caveats added.

loop_back.condition: Data quality issues (e.g., sampling bias, large missing data) undermine the analysis.
loop_back.reason: Dataset needs cleaning or re-scoping before analysis is meaningful.

sequence.label: Review, document, and report

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Senior analyst peer-reviews the methodology section before publication.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Documenting which tests were AI-selected and which were human-specified in the methods section.

sequence.triggers_next: Report approved and methodology documented. Cycle restarts for next analysis.

transitions.trigger: Analysis scope and reporting rules agreed.
AI delivers analysis results.
Findings validated.
Data quality issues require dataset revision.
Next research question — restart.

research.title: AI-Supported Academic Literature Review

delegation.description: AI can accelerate the literature review process by synthesizing themes across papers you have already identified, generating structured summaries of provided texts, suggesting search terms for database queries, and proposing a gap analysis framework. Researchers retain responsibility for: executing the systematic search in academic databases, verifying that all cited sources exist and are accurately represented, forming scholarly interpretations, and drawing conclusions about the field's state.
delegation.example: A PhD student compiles 60 papers from her PubMed and Scopus searches on microbiome-mental health connections. She feeds the abstracts to AI in batches, asking it to identify recurring themes, contradictions between studies, and underexplored angles. She then uses this thematic map as a scaffold for her own reading and synthesis, reading every paper herself before citing it.
delegation.antipattern: Asking AI to conduct the literature review from scratch — generating the reading list, summarizing papers AI has not actually read, and producing a synthesis the researcher then submits — creating a review full of hallucinated citations and misrepresented findings that the researcher cannot defend.

description.description: Specify your literature review's scope with precision: the exact research question, the academic field and subfield, the time range, inclusion and exclusion criteria, the required depth of analysis, and the review type (narrative, systematic, scoping). Imprecise framing leads to AI output that spans the wrong literature or treats your question at the wrong level of specificity.
description.example: Prompt: 'I have provided 45 abstracts from papers on cognitive behavioral therapy outcomes for adolescent depression, published 2015-2024, RCTs only. Identify: (1) the most common outcome measures used across studies, (2) the patient subgroups most frequently underrepresented, (3) the three most-cited methodological limitations across papers, and (4) any emerging treatment modifications mentioned in multiple papers. Do not add any studies I haven't provided.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'Write a literature review on therapy and depression.' Without scope constraints, AI generates a sweeping narrative that mixes study types, time periods, populations, and therapy modalities — producing a superficial overview that satisfies no specific research question and cannot withstand peer review scrutiny.

discernment.description: Verify every citation in an AI-assisted literature review independently in an academic database before including it in your work. AI confidently generates plausible-sounding but fabricated citations — author names, journal titles, and publication years that look real but aren't. Additionally, verify that real papers are accurately characterized: check that AI summaries correctly represent each paper's methodology, findings, and limitations.
discernment.example: A researcher asks AI to synthesize themes from a set of provided papers and flag potentially relevant additional sources. She treats every additional source AI suggests as unverified until she has confirmed it in Google Scholar. Of eight AI-suggested additional references, two do not exist and one exists but reaches the opposite conclusion from what AI claimed.
discernment.antipattern: Submitting a literature review with AI-generated citations that were never individually verified in an academic database — discovering during peer review that three cited articles are fabrications, resulting in rejection and reputational damage that follows the researcher's professional record.

diligence.description: The researcher is fully responsible for every claim and citation in the final review. Disclose AI assistance per your target journal's author guidelines and your institution's research integrity policy (requirements vary significantly across disciplines and publishers). Validate all sources independently regardless of AI confidence. Understand that your scholarly reputation is built on the accuracy and integrity of your literature representation.
diligence.example: A research team submits a systematic review that includes in the methods section a transparent account of how AI tools were used for thematic synthesis of author-identified papers, notes that all citations were independently verified in Cochrane Library and PubMed, and confirms compliance with the journal's AI transparency policy — meeting both disclosure and integrity standards.
diligence.antipattern: Relying on AI to identify and summarize literature without reading the papers yourself, submitting a review that misrepresents study findings because you trusted AI's characterizations over the original texts, or failing to disclose AI assistance to a journal that requires transparency — compromising academic integrity and potentially contributing false knowledge to the scholarly record.

research.summary: AI can accelerate the literature review process by synthesizing themes across papers you have already identified, generating structured summaries of provided texts, suggesting search terms for databas

collaboration.description: Researcher and AI iterate through literature in passes — each pass scopes the search, provides query context, evaluates source quality, and commits the accepted sources before the next domain.

sequence.label: Define search scope and AI autonomy

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Search for papers on transformer attention mechanisms published 2020–2024. Can you decide which databases to query?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: I'd suggest Semantic Scholar, arXiv, and ACL Anthology. Should I include pre-prints or only peer-reviewed?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Include pre-prints but flag them separately.

sequence.triggers_next: Search scope, databases, and pre-print policy agreed.

sequence.label: Provide query terms and exclusion criteria

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Focus on efficiency improvements to attention. Exclude papers that are purely theoretical with no empirical results.

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: Should I prioritize highly-cited papers or recent ones?

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Recent first — this is a fast-moving area.

sequence.triggers_next: AI has query terms, relevance criteria, and recency bias.

sequence.label: Evaluate source quality and relevance

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Paper 7 seems tangentially related — is the methodology actually relevant to our work?

example_prompts.speaker: ai
example_prompts.text: The dataset they use is different but the attention variant they propose directly addresses your efficiency problem. I'd include it.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Agreed — include it with a note on the dataset difference.

sequence.triggers_next: Source list validated for relevance and quality.

sequence.label: Curate and document the source list

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: PI reviews the final list before it goes into the literature section.

example_prompts.speaker: human
example_prompts.text: Adding search query log and exclusion decisions to the methodology appendix.

sequence.triggers_next: Sources committed. Begin next thematic area.

transitions.trigger: Search scope and databases agreed.
AI returns candidate sources.
Sources validated.
Next thematic area — new search pass.

writing.title: Story and Content Development with AI

delegation.description: Identify which creative elements benefit from AI exploration versus which must remain human-driven. AI excels at generating plot alternatives, brainstorming secondary character names, proposing scene structures, and expanding on prompts. Core voice, thematic meaning, emotional truth, and the author's unique perspective must stay with the human creator.
delegation.example: A novelist asks AI to generate five possible turning points for chapter 7, then selects and transforms the one that best fits her protagonist's established arc. The AI explores; the human decides and develops.
delegation.antipattern: Handing the AI a character description and asking it to write entire chapters wholesale, then lightly editing and publishing the result — abdicating the creative authorship that gives the work its distinctive voice and purpose.

description.description: Give AI rich, specific creative context — the story world's rules, character backstories, established themes, the emotional arc you are building toward, your narrative voice, and any stylistic constraints. Vague prompts produce generic content; detailed creative briefs produce useful raw material.
description.example: Prompt: 'This is a near-future climate noir story set in drowned New Orleans, 2067. Protagonist Maya Tran is a cynical water-rights detective, dry wit, Creole heritage, distrusts authority. I need three scene openings for chapter 4 where she visits the Floating Market. Tone: melancholy but alive with sensory detail. Echo Toni Morrison's eye for place.'
description.antipattern: Prompting: 'Write a scene for my sci-fi story where the hero goes to a market.' This yields generic content with no connection to the established world, character voice, or thematic purpose — unusable without complete rewriting.

discernment.description: Evaluate AI-generated content against your creative vision, not just surface quality. Good AI output that doesn't fit your voice or thematic intent is still the wrong output. Actively reject suggestions that feel generic, that undercut character consistency, or that introduce tropes that conflict with what you are building.
discernment.example: An author receives three AI-generated chapter openings. Two are competently written but feel tonally flat for her noir voice. The third has an interesting image she adapts, changing the phrasing to match her protagonist's sardonic register. She uses roughly 15% of the AI material, transformed.
discernment.antipattern: Accepting AI-generated dialogue or scene descriptions because they are grammatically sound and feel 'good enough,' without checking whether they match the character's established voice, advance the story's thematic arc, or reflect the emotional truth the author intended.

diligence.description: Be transparent about AI's role in your creative work according to the norms of your publication context. Preserve your human creative intent as the primary force shaping the work. Avoid over-relying on AI to the point where your own creative voice atrophies. Keep records of your creative process for attribution purposes.
diligence.example: A short story writer notes in her submission cover letter that AI tools were used for brainstorming and structural exploration, per the journal's disclosure policy. The published story reflects her voice, her decisions, and her thematic vision throughout.
diligence.antipattern: Submitting AI-generated content to publications that prohibit AI-assisted work without disclosure, or losing track of which creative decisions were yours versus the AI's — failing to maintain the human authorship that gives the work its integrity and legal standing.
